description:
Hard removal of the deprecated preset aliases introduced by agent-a365bcf2 (preset rename to intent-flavored names).

The rename of preset IDs from harness-flavored (agent / claude / codex / cursor / worker) to intent-flavored (default / smart / cheap / minimal / benchmark) introduced a deprecation period where the old names still resolve via prompt.DeprecatedPresetAliases, emit a stderr warning, and forward to the canonical preset. This bead is the hard-removal follow-up.

Trigger: one release after the rename ships. Downstream DocumentDrivenDX/ddx config files and any CI scripts using the old names MUST be updated first — otherwise this bead will break them.

Scope:
- Delete DeprecatedPresetAliases from prompt/presets.go
- Delete DeprecatedPresetNames() from prompt/presets.go
- Change ResolvePresetName to return a fatal error (or return the default with a louder warning — design decision at time of implementation) for unknown / deprecated names
- Remove the back-compat tests added in agent-a365bcf2 (TestResolvePresetName_DeprecatedAliases, TestResolvePresetWithWarning_DeprecatedCodexAliasEmitsWarning, TestResolvePresetWithWarning_DeprecatedAliasInConfig)
- Remove the Deprecated preset names section from README.md
- Remove the Deprecated harness-flavored names section from docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-003-system-prompts.md
- Remove the deprecated-alias paragraph from docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-005-provider-config.md
- CHANGELOG entry documenting the hard removal

Pre-flight check before starting this bead: grep DocumentDrivenDX/ddx repo for any reference to --preset (agent|claude|codex|cursor|worker) and confirm it has been migrated.
title: preset: remove deprecated harness-flavored preset aliases (agent, claude, codex, cursor, worker)

acceptance:
1. In the openai-compat provider's request serializer, the `thinking` field is omitted from the wire body when DetectedFlavor() is any of: lmstudio, omlx, openrouter, ollama, openai-native (or whatever the equivalent non-Anthropic-passthrough flavors are in the current registry).

2. The anthropic provider's serializer continues to include `thinking` in its native request bodies (no regression for Claude).

3. Unit test: construct a Request with thinking config, route it through the openai-compat provider with a stubbed DetectedFlavor() returning 'omlx'. Assert the serialized request body does NOT contain the string 'thinking'.

4. Unit test: same Request, same provider, stubbed DetectedFlavor() returning 'anthropic-passthrough' (if such a flavor exists) OR a test-only flavor whitelisted for thinking. Assert the body DOES contain thinking.

5. Integration test (optional but recommended): run against a real omlx endpoint or a test double that emulates omlx's silent-termination behavior when thinking is present. Assert the request completes without 'unexpected end of JSON input'.

6. CHANGELOG.md entry referencing this bead and ddx-6a5dfe35.

7. Audit note: other Anthropic-specific fields (metadata keys, system placement, etc.) checked during this fix; any additional leaks filed as follow-up beads.
description:
## Summary

The agent library sends an Anthropic-specific `thinking` field in OpenAI-compatible request bodies to non-Anthropic providers (lmstudio, omlx, openrouter, ollama). Providers that accept and parse the request then silently terminate their SSE stream after the first delta frame when their generation pipeline encounters the unknown field. Downstream consumers see `unexpected end of JSON input`.

This bug was classified via wire capture using the new AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE=1 flag (this repo's agent-941e7e42, landed in v0.3.12). Evidence was gathered from DocumentDrivenDX/ddx ddx-6a5dfe35.

## Wire evidence (v0.3.12, 2026-04-18)

```
POST http://vidar:1235/v1/chat/completions
Content-Type: application/json
Body includes:
  "stream":true,
  "stream_options":{"include_usage":true},
  "thinking":{"budget_tokens":8192,"type":"enabled"},
  "tools":[... 6 tools ...],
  "model":"Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit"
```

Response:
- HTTP 200
- Content-Type: text/event-stream; charset=utf-8
- Body captured: 186 bytes total
  - Frame 1: `: keep-alive\n\n` (SSE keepalive)
  - Frame 2: valid `data: {..."delta":{"role":"assistant"}}\n\n` (assistant role start)
  - Then: stream terminates. No `data: [DONE]`. No error frame. Just EOF.

`thinking` is Anthropic's extended-thinking API. It is NOT in the OpenAI chat-completions schema. vidar-omlx (OpenAI-compatible, no Anthropic semantics) has no handler for it and its generation pipeline fails silently mid-stream.

Reproduced on Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit AND Qwen3-Coder-Next-MLX-4bit — model-independent. Reproduced across two projects (ddx repo + agent repo) — project-independent. Reproduced on v0.3.11 and v0.3.12 — both versions exhibit the bug.

## Root cause

The openai-compat provider's request-serialization path does not gate flavor-specific fields. `thinking` is serialized into the wire body unconditionally when the caller has passed the equivalent Anthropic-style configuration (via preset or CLI flag). Since v0.3.12 now has Provider.DetectedFlavor() (agent-92f0f324), the library has the information it needs to gate this — it just doesn't use it in the serializer.

## Fix direction

In the openai-compat provider's Chat/ChatStream request builder:

1. Before writing the request body, check `p.DetectedFlavor()`.
2. If the flavor is NOT one that supports Anthropic thinking semantics (i.e., NOT `anthropic-passthrough` or similar — in practice all current openai-compat flavors: lmstudio, omlx, openrouter, ollama, plus `openai-native`), omit the `thinking` field from the serialized JSON.
3. Emit a debug log (at Trace level or via AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE when that flag is on) noting that `thinking` was stripped for flavor X. Operators should see this when diagnosing 'why isn't my local model using thinking'.

The anthropic provider package continues to include the field in its own serializer (no change there).

Long term, consider a broader per-flavor field allowlist/denylist in the openai-compat serializer — `thinking` is probably not the only Anthropic-specific field that leaks. Audit the request builder for other non-portable fields.

## Alternative (less preferred)

Reject at config-load: if Config passes `thinking` config AND any provider in the same config has a non-Anthropic DetectedFlavor, emit a startup warning. Does not help when providers are added at runtime and does not handle the case where a single agent run targets multiple providers with different flavors in the same session.

Stripping at serialization is cleaner.

## Other non-portable fields likely to have the same issue

These are candidates for the same per-flavor gate:
- `metadata` with Anthropic-specific keys
- `system` as a top-level field vs in messages array (OpenAI uses messages[].role=system; some providers may tolerate both, some may not)
- `anthropic_version` or similar API-version fields
- Extended-thinking budget controls beyond `thinking`

Audit as part of the fix; file follow-up beads if other fields exhibit the same symptom.

## Related

- agent-92f0f324 (v0.3.12): DetectedFlavor() — this fix consumes it
- agent-941e7e42 (v0.3.12): AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE — this bug was classified using it
- agent-767549c7 (v0.3.12): capability flags — related but orthogonal (capability gating wouldn't have prevented this specific leak)
- DocumentDrivenDX/ddx ddx-6a5dfe35: consumer bead tracking the symptom; closes when this upstream bead lands + DDx bumps dep.
title: provider: strip Anthropic-specific fields (thinking) from non-Anthropic request bodies using DetectedFlavor() gate

acceptance:
1. Implementation follows docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-19-provider-routing-tool-output.md.
2. `internal/sdk/openaicompat` contains shared protocol/client logic and no provider identity strings, provider-system names, URL heuristics, or provider cost attribution.
3. Concrete provider packages exist for openai, openrouter, lmstudio, omlx, and ollama at minimum, each with provider identity, capabilities, and provider-specific discovery/limit/cost behavior.
4. Config rejects `type: openai-compat` and `flavor`; concrete provider types are required or inferred only during config load for known URLs/ports.
5. Local provider endpoint pools are represented in provider/model listing, route candidates, and cooldown keys.
6. ProviderName/ProviderSystem or replacement fields report configured provider name and provider type; no runtime output reports `openai-compat` as provider identity.
7. Existing provider tests and the conformance suite pass for all new provider wrappers.
description:
Implement the provider identity split described in docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-19-provider-routing-tool-output.md.

Provider now means the model source/counterparty, not an HTTP protocol shape. `openai`, `openrouter`, `lmstudio`, `omlx`, `ollama`, and `anthropic` are providers. The OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions/SSE shape is a shared implementation concern, not a provider identity.

Break backward compatibility where needed: remove `openai-compat` as a config provider type and remove runtime `flavor` provider behavior. URL/port heuristics may remain only as config-load inference for incomplete config. OpenRouter owns cost attribution. Local providers own endpoint pools and provider-specific discovery/limit logic.

This parent bead is decomposed by child implementation beads. Land against the provider conformance suite.

notes: 2026-04-19 design decision from user: do not preserve provider back-compat. Provider means a model source/counterparty, not an HTTP protocol shape: openai, openrouter, lmstudio, omlx, etc. Each provider owns its idiosyncrasies: auth/API key requirements, model listings/discovery, pricing/cost attribution, and local endpoint host:port configuration (e.g. lmstudio and omlx can require one or more host:port entries). Providers may share implementation elements such as an API shape or API client library, but that is a separate concern from provider identity. The OpenAI-compatible transport should be a shared SDK/client layer beneath those provider identities.
title: refactor: split 'openai' provider into openai-compat SDK + flavor-specific providers

acceptance:
1. CONTRACT-003 and the relevant FEAT-002/design text state the chosen native `agent` permission semantics for safe, supervised, and unrestricted, including any intentionally unsupported mode.
2. Native `Service.Execute` enforces the chosen semantics: safe mode cannot execute side-effecting tools outside the allowed policy, unrestricted exposes the documented full tool set, and supervised either has an implemented behavior or is rejected clearly before execution.
3. Routing and capability matrix behavior matches enforcement: `agent` `PermissionModes` is promoted only for implemented modes, and unsupported permission requests fail deterministically.
4. Tests cover native `agent` safe/unrestricted/supervised behavior through the service boundary, not only unit-level tool wrappers.
5. `go test -tags=testseam .` and `go test ./internal/tool ./...` pass.
description:
`ServiceExecuteRequest.Permissions` is a first-class CONTRACT-003 field and codex/claude expose permission modes, but the native `agent` harness currently declares `PermissionModes` unsupported and does not enforce the request value. For primary-harness parity, decide and implement native permission semantics instead of silently treating all native service runs the same.

This may require reconciling FEAT-002's earlier out-of-scope note for tool permission management with the newer service contract. Keep secondary harness permission cleanup out of scope.
title: service: define and enforce native agent permission modes

acceptance:
1. internal/pty/cassette records version-1 manifests, timed input events, output.raw, timed output.jsonl raw chunk events, timed frame events, timed opaque service-event JSON, final metadata, optional quota data, and scrub reports through a documented API.
2. Every input, raw output chunk, frame, resize/signal, service-event, and final record includes monotonic global seq and monotonic t_ms; output.jsonl records byte offsets and lengths into output.raw with optional chunk digests and no inline bytes; manifest.timing records resolution_ms, clock policy, and replay default; version 1 defaults to resolution_ms=100 but accepts finer capture resolution without a schema bump.
3. Manifest v1 includes manifest.id, manifest.content_digest with sha256 over output.raw, and manifest.terminal.emulator {name, version}; readers expose these to assertion specs.
4. Recording is event-driven: timestamps are written on observed PTY/input/service transitions. Fixed-interval frame sampling may be supported only as an optional derived view, not as the authoritative recording model.
5. Event order is authoritative by global seq; same-t_ms events replay in seq order. Tests cover multiple events in one timestamp bucket.
6. A named CassetteTee-style interface lets harness adapters hand timed opaque service-event JSON and final metadata to internal/pty/cassette without any internal/pty package importing internal/harnesses or CONTRACT-003 typed-event decoders.
7. Replay supports realtime timing that honors recorded t_ms within one resolution tick, scaled timing, and collapsed timing under a virtual clock for deterministic CI.
8. Replay mode is credential-free, preserves input/output/frame/service-event ordering and raw output chunk boundaries, and exposes read-only event/frame/service-event iterators suitable for the follow-on internal/ptytest assertion framework.
9. Cassette replay supports read-only inspection inputs and never mutates recorded artifacts; readers reject unsupported newer major versions, missing required artifacts, missing required fields, unknown required feature flags, manifest/assertion ID mismatches, manifest/assertion digest mismatches, and emulator mismatches where frame assertions require a pinned emulator; readers may ignore unknown optional fields within the same major version; writers refuse to overwrite newer cassette schema versions.
10. Scrub rules and separate nondeterministic-output normalization are tested for environment allowlists, HOME/worktree path rewrites, tokens, account identifiers, UUID/request/session IDs, RFC3339/local timestamps, PIDs, elapsed durations, transient socket/file names, animation counters, and intentionally preserved fields.
11. Recorders compute t_ms from a monotonic elapsed clock such as time.Since(start) or injected test clock; tests cover a wall-clock jump without changing event ordering.
12. Authenticated record mode serializes per harness account and fails fast on lock contention rather than writing accepted artifacts from competing Claude/Codex record jobs.
13. Tests record and replay a deterministic synthetic terminal run through the direct PTY library and verify manifest fields, t_ms and seq ordering, realtime/scaled/collapsed playback behavior, raw output, output chunk offsets, frames, final status, schema-version handling, additive optional fields, unknown required fields, scrub/normalization reports, and parallel read-only replay safety.
14. The cassette package does not import internal/harnesses or contain Claude/Codex-specific quota/model logic; service events are stored as opaque JSON.
15. go test ./... passes.
description: Implement versioned cassette recording and playback as part of the ADR-002 direct PTY library, under internal/pty/cassette. The cassette layer consumes internal/pty/session and internal/pty/terminal, owns manifest/input/output/frame/service-event/final/quota/scrub artifacts, records monotonic event timestamps at a default 100ms resolution while allowing finer resolution_ms in version 1, and provides realtime, scaled, and collapsed replay. It remains free of harness-specific Claude/Codex policy.
title: harness-pty: implement PTY cassette recorder and playback library

acceptance: (1) Config schema supports endpoint-only blocks (no per-provider 'name' or 'model' fields required). (2) Service.Execute (not just cmd/agent routing_smart.go) probes /v1/models and normalizes the requested model against live catalogs case-insensitively, including suffix/prefix variants (Qwen3.5-27B-4bit ↔ qwen3.5-27b, qwen/qwen3.6 ↔ Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit). (3) Dead endpoints are skipped at probe time, not on the failing request. (4) When multiple live endpoints serve the same requested model, selection uses routing-metrics signals, not config ordering. (5) DDx's .ddx/config.yaml can express 'give me the best qwen3.6 you can find' without naming vidar-omlx or any specific provider. (6) Integration test: with two endpoints A (dead) and B (live, serves Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit), 'qwen3.6' resolves to B without hand-holding. (7) Regression: no existing acceptance test regresses.
description:
User-stated design direction (2026-04-21, DDx session). Current routing is built around named provider profiles (e.g. 'vidar-omlx', 'vidar-coder', 'bragi') each with a hard-coded model declaration. This breaks in multiple ugly ways and drove a zero-success drain of 32 test-migration beads on DDx:

Failure 1: 'cheap: qwen/qwen3.6' in .ddx/config.yaml routed to a DEAD vidar lmstudio endpoint (http://vidar:1234, 502 Bad Gateway) because the named profile declared its model as 'qwen3.5-27b' and the request fuzzy-matched on that string before any liveness check ran.

Failure 2: When failover hit vidar-omlx (http://vidar:1235), the request carried 'qwen3.5-27b' as the model string — but vidar-omlx actually serves 'Qwen3.5-27B-4bit' and 'Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit'. Every attempt 404'd with 'Model not found'.

Failure 3: routing_smart.go already has NormalizeModelID (internal/provider/openai/discovery.go) doing case-insensitive + suffix match against a discovered catalog, but it is only wired in cmd/agent routing_smart.go — NOT in the Service.Execute path that every consumer (DDx, etc.) actually hits.

Desired shape:

1. Config = endpoint pairs, not named profiles:
   endpoints:
     - {type: lmstudio, host: vidar, port: 1234}
     - {type: omlx, host: vidar, port: 1235}
     - {type: lmstudio, host: bragi, port: 1234}
     - {type: openrouter, api_key: ...}

2. On every dispatch, probe each endpoint's /v1/models with a short timeout. Skip endpoints that 502/timeout/empty.

3. Build a live {endpoint, model_id} matrix from the probe. The model_id is whatever the server actually serves — no per-provider name hardcoding.

4. Resolve the request (user asks for 'qwen3.6' or any case-variant) against the matrix:
   - case-insensitive contains / suffix / prefix match against every live model_id
   - if multiple endpoints match, tie-break on prior performance (latency, success rate, cost) from the routing-metrics store
   - if one matches, use it
   - if none match, explicit error ('no live endpoint offers a match for qwen3.6') + caller escalates

5. Kill the named-profile config-block lookups in the routing hot path. User-facing aliases (labels) are fine as shorthand but resolve through the same discovery flow.

6. NormalizeModelID's logic is the right primitive; extend and wire it into Service.Execute so every consumer benefits.

3. Routing/final metadata tests prove requested harness is preserved (for example `omlx`) while `RoutingActual.Provider` reports the resolved concrete provider name (for example `vidar-omlx`).

description:
Surfaced while upgrading ddx (consumer) from ddx-agent v0.5.0 -> v0.6.0. After DDx migrates mechanical fields (Effort->Reasoning, SupportedEfforts->SupportedReasoning) and config migrates (thinking_level->reasoning, type: openai-compat -> concrete types), two agent-side gaps remain:

1. HTTP provider harnesses are declared but not dispatched by service.Execute.
   Repro: `ddx agent run --harness lmstudio --text ping --timeout 30s`
   Result: `error: harness "lmstudio" dispatch not yet wired in service.Execute`.
   The same class must work for `omlx`; omlx is a first-class OpenAI-compatible provider with existing streaming workarounds and tests that must be preserved.

2. Harness-to-provider resolution assumes provider is literally named after the harness.
   Example: tier routing resolves harness=lmstudio model=qwen3.5-27b, but native config lookup fails with `config: unknown provider "lmstudio"` when the config has concrete provider names such as bragi (type: lmstudio), vidar-omlx (type: omlx), or openrouter.

Expected: explicit HTTP-provider harnesses (`lmstudio`, `omlx`, `openrouter`) dispatch through the native provider path. If no literal provider name matches, resolution falls back to configured providers whose `type` equals the requested harness/provider, preferring default_provider when its type matches and otherwise using stable ProviderNames order. Failures list viable provider names and types.

Preserve existing omlx work: `internal/provider/omlx` must continue to use the OpenAI-compatible provider path and the SSE comment-frame workaround in `internal/sdk/openaicompat/sse_filter.go`; add or keep regression coverage with omlx-shaped keep-alive comment frames.

In-scope files:
- internal/harnesses/registry.go and registry tests
- service_execute.go
- service_native_provider.go
- service/service-level tests next to existing service tests
- internal/provider/omlx and OpenAI-compatible streaming regression tests as needed

Out-of-scope:
- Removing ProviderPreference or redesigning public profile routing; tracked separately by agent-285a05d2.

acceptance:
1. A design or ADR document under docs/helix/02-design records the chosen PTY/cassette transport and explicitly rejects the alternatives with reasons.
2. The decision states whether tmux is a required dependency for harness execution, not a partial helper, or whether agent owns PTY lifecycle directly.
3. The decision defines the cassette data contract: input events, output bytes or frames, terminal size, environment scrub rules, exit status, timing normalization, service events, final metadata, usage, quota probe output, and versioning.
4. The decision defines record-mode credential failure behavior and replay-mode deterministic behavior.
5. CONTRACT-003 or linked design docs reference the decision from the harness integration-testing section.
6. go test ./... passes if code or generated docs are touched.
description:
Make the transport decision for golden-master testing of interactive harnesses before building cassette-heavy integration tests. The prior discussion rejected a small incidental tmux dependency: either the project standardizes on tmux for the whole harness lifecycle, or it owns PTY lifecycle directly. If the reusable cassette player is generic enough to be its own project, define that boundary instead of burying it inside agent.

The decision must evaluate:
- direct PTY ownership using Go primitives and os/exec
- tmux as the session/process lifecycle manager
- existing tools such as ntm or other terminal/TUI snapshot approaches if locally relevant
- how to record input/output bytes, screen snapshots, environment, resize events, exit status, timing, and structured service events
- how replay is deterministic without credentials
- how record mode fails fast when the target harness lacks credentials/auth/subscription/quota
- how users can inspect an in-flight or recorded run without corrupting the cassette

This bead does not implement the cassette runner. It creates the architecture decision and follow-up constraints for implementation beads.

events.body: Compared local gastown and dun with current ntm, claude-squad, dmux, creack/pty, go-expect, and asciinema. Conclusion: tmux is useful operator prior art but creates ambiguous service/cassette evidence. ADR-002 now says no tmux in core harness execution, quota/model probes, cassettes, cancellation, or inspection; existing tmux helpers are legacy diagnostics only. Queued direct PTY implementation beads agent-949a5ba4, agent-c3155c52, and agent-2e7c356c.
events.summary: Re-reviewed tmux/direct PTY decision and amended ADR-002 to require direct PTY only

events.body: Folded the review into ADR-002, harness golden integration docs, and follow-up beads. The plan now separates internal/pty/session, internal/pty/terminal, internal/pty/cassette, and harness-specific probe adapters; forbids internal/pty imports from internal/harnesses; adds Docker TUI conformance starting with Unix top over multiple screens plus pager/editor-style TUIs; adds cassette recorder/replay work; strengthens Claude/Codex quota and model-list beads with replay and authenticated recorder coverage; and adds a token-usage bead for Claude/Codex.
events.summary: Reviewed PTY plan for module boundaries and robust conformance coverage

events.body: Updated ADR-002 and follow-up beads so cassette record/playback is a first-class internal/pty library layer rather than a side helper. Version-1 cassettes now carry manifest.timing with default resolution_ms=100; input/frame/service-event records carry monotonic t_ms; replay supports realtime, scaled, and collapsed modes. Reconciled harness golden artifact names with ADR-002 and kept service events opaque below the cassette boundary.
events.summary: Folded cassette record/playback into PTY library

events.body: Ran a throwaway Go spike using creack/pty v1.1.24 and hinshun/vt10x against /usr/bin/top. The spike started top under direct PTY, captured 16,714 raw bytes, rendered four frames, sent input '1' to change the screen, resized the PTY/model to 100x30, and exited with 'q'. Result: direct PTY control is tractable, but raw output contains dense ANSI control sequences, so ADR-003 now requires a real terminal emulator behind internal/pty/terminal rather than regex stripping.

title: harness-test: decide PTY cassette transport for live TUI record/replay

acceptance:
1. The supported-harness TUI-only checklist in docs/helix/02-design/harness-golden-integration.md is current for agent, codex, claude, gemini, opencode, and pi, and every required TUI-only row has a named record/playback scenario.
2. The direct PTY substrate is implemented and tested through internal/pty/session and internal/pty/terminal with no tmux dependency or tmux shell-out.
3. internal/pty/cassette records and replays version-1 event-driven cassettes deterministically without credentials, with global seq plus monotonic t_ms on input, raw output chunks, frames, resize/signal, service events, and final events; output.jsonl preserves raw chunk timing and offsets into output.raw; manifests include cassette ID, content digest, and terminal emulator identity; version 1 defaults to 100ms timestamp resolution while allowing finer capture resolution, schema evolution rules, and realtime/scaled/collapsed replay modes.
4. The cassette assertion framework runs replay tests in collapsed time, supports realtime/scaled scheduler checks, is parallel-safe, and covers synthetic weird terminal fixtures.
5. Docker TUI conformance passes with Unix top over multiple useful screens plus at least two additional TUI shapes through reusable scenario fixtures and time-coded assertions.
6. Claude quota/status extraction, model-list discovery, and reasoning/effort discovery are proven through both opt-in authenticated record mode and credential-free playback mode; Claude token-usage capture remains a required capability proven by native stream tests unless it becomes TUI-derived, in which case a checklist row and record/playback test are required.
7. Codex quota/status extraction, model-list discovery, and reasoning/effort discovery are proven through both opt-in authenticated record mode and credential-free playback mode; Codex token-usage capture remains a required capability proven by native stream tests unless it becomes TUI-derived, in which case a checklist row and record/playback test are required.
8. For agent, gemini, opencode, and pi, all currently supported TUI-only checklist rows are either absent with documented CLI/API evidence or are proven through both opt-in record mode and credential-free playback mode if future rows are added.
9. Capability matrix supported/pass status requires the checklist gate: accepted evidence records captured_at, harness binary version, auth/account class when applicable, source, freshness TTL, and staleness behavior.
10. go test ./... passes, replay integration passes without credentials, and Docker/authenticated record commands are documented with explicit skip/fail behavior.
description: Umbrella for the finalized ADR-002 PTY capability-test gate. This closes only when the direct PTY probe substrate, cassette recorder/playback, assertion framework, TUI conformance tests, and supported-harness TUI-only capability checklist all pass. The current baseline leaves tmux off entirely. The first production live path is a short-lived background probe that starts a harness, extracts quota/status/model/reasoning facts when those are TUI-only, writes scrubbed evidence/cache data, and exits. Normal prompt execution stays on harness-native batch modes unless a future design promotes PTY interactive execution as a fallback. Supported harnesses are agent, codex, claude, gemini, opencode, and pi; HTTP providers and test harnesses are not harnesses for this checklist.

acceptance:
1. Service exposes ListProfiles, ResolveProfile, and ProfileAliases or an equivalent projection that lists profile names, deprecated aliases, provenance/catalog version, placement order, surface/model candidates, cost ceiling, reasoning default, and failure policy.
2. Consumers can resolve a profile name to a routable request without reading ~/.config/agent/models.yaml and without knowing internal surface strings such as agent.openai.
3. Unknown, deprecated, and alias profile cases have explicit tests.
4. CONTRACT-003 documents the profile projection API and includes a reference consumer migration from direct YAML reads.
5. go test . ./internal/modelcatalog passes.
6. go test ./... passes.
description:
Surfaced by DDx consumer review. DDx's cli/internal/agent/agent_catalog_shared.go reads ~/.config/agent/models.yaml directly and hard-codes surface translations like "agent.openai -> embedded-openai". That's a consumer baking in agent's catalog schema + internal surface naming. When agent renames a surface or restructures surface_policy (as v0.7.0 did), every consumer breaks.

With profile-owned placement policy (per agent-285a05d2), the catalog now carries placement, cost ceilings, failure policy, and reasoning defaults per profile/target. Consumers need to resolve a profile name to something routable — but they shouldn't be the ones reading YAML to do it.

## Proposal

Service exposes catalog + profile projection:

- Service.ListProfiles(ctx) -> []ProfileInfo  (name, description, deprecated alias-of, provenance)
- Service.ResolveProfile(ctx, name) -> ResolvedProfile (candidate surfaces + models + placement hint + cost ceiling + reasoning default + failure policy)
- Service.ProfileAliases(ctx) -> map[oldName]newName  (for deprecated aliases like the v0.6.0 harness-flavored preset removals — consumer gets a clear migration signal)

Consumers never touch ~/.config/agent/models.yaml, never know "agent.openai" surface naming.

## Consumer impact

Shrinks DDx ddx-e7254128 post-v0.7.0 catalog follow-up, ddx-ac5c7fdb (thin-consumer epic), ddx-bbb65768 (profile automation default), ddx-4b59b816 (cost-tiered routing lookups).

no viable harness found

no viable harness found

description:
THE consolidating bead. Replaces both DDx routing.go (BuildCandidatePlans / RankCandidates / SelectBestCandidate / evaluateCandidate / NormalizeRouteRequest, 915 lines) AND agent routing_smart.go + routing_provider.go with a single internal routing engine. Per CONTRACT-001:

ResolveRoute(ctx, req) → RouteDecision{Harness, Provider, Model, Reason, Candidates[]}.

Candidates ranks (harness, provider, model) tuples uniformly. The 6 "badly implemented" smells are fixed structurally:
1. ONE routing engine (not two parallel impls).
2. RouteRequest has Provider field from day one (fixes ddx-8610020e — close it when this lands).
3. Canonical-form fuzzy matcher — case + vendor-prefix normalization (fixes ddx-0486e601 — close it).
4. Per-(harness, provider, model) capability gating — context window, tool support, effort, permissions (fixes ddx-4817edfd subtree — close subtree).
5. Profile-aware tier escalation that talks to provider failover (fixes ddx-3c5ba7cc — close it).
6. Single observation store + cooldown abstraction.

Test rewrite: routing_test.go (915 lines) ports as mechanical rewrite — newTestRunnerForRouting() → newTestRoutingEngine(); fixtures preserved.

Depends on: agent-49e35134 (ListHarnesses), agent ports of subprocess wrappers (claude/codex/etc.), agent-713e9a99 (registry+state).
title: routing: implement DdxAgent.ResolveRoute (consolidated single routing engine; fixes 6 routing smells)

description: Automatic profile/tier routing must be separated from ad-hoc explicit harness execution. A registered harness may be available and still not be safe for automatic routing unless it has full baseline coverage: model selection/listing, reasoning support or explicit rejection, usage/final metadata, error/cancellation behavior, and quota/account evidence where applicable. The current primary baseline identifies agent, codex, and claude as the only intended primary harnesses. Secondary harnesses such as gemini, opencode, and pi must remain explicit opt-in until their coverage is promoted.\n\nThis epic tracks the routing policy gate plus per-harness coverage/promotion beads. It does not require every harness to become primary; it requires the system to be honest about which ones may be selected automatically.
notes: Documented the automatic-routing eligibility standard in docs/helix/02-design/harness-golden-integration.md and primary-harness-capability-baseline.md. Automatic routing now requires deterministic runner/model/reasoning/usage evidence plus fresh quota/account or concrete cost policy; secondary harnesses stay explicit-only until that bar is met. Reverted premature opencode/pi AutoRoutingEligible metadata and full go test ./... passed.
title: epic: require full harness coverage for automatic routing eligibility

acceptance: FEAT-001 has acceptance criteria covering all three loop-detection sentinels; each AC references the existing tests as verification; no code changes required
description:
FEAT-001-agent-loop.md acceptance criteria (AC-FEAT-001-01 through AC-FEAT-001-06) do not cover the three loop-detection behaviors shipped in v0.3.1:

1. ErrReasoningOverflow: stream aborted when model emits >32k chars of pure reasoning_content with no content/tool_call delta
2. ErrReasoningStall: stream aborted when only reasoning_content deltas arrive for >reasoningStallTimeout with no forward progress
3. ErrToolCallLoop: loop aborted when identical tool-call set repeats >=3 consecutive turns

Implementation: loop.go (toolCallLoopLimit, toolCallFingerprint, ErrToolCallLoop handling), stream_consume.go (reasoningByteLimit, stall timer, ErrReasoningOverflow/Stall), errors.go
Tests: TestRun_ToolCallLoopDetection, TestRun_ToolCallLoopCounterResetsOnDifferentCall (loop_test.go); TestConsumeStream_ReasoningOverflow, TestConsumeStream_ReasoningStall (stream_consume_test.go)

Work: Add AC-FEAT-001-07, AC-FEAT-001-08, AC-FEAT-001-09 (or equivalent) to FEAT-001 covering these three behaviors. Reference the existing tests as verification evidence. Discovered by alignment review agent-35bf3d08, review issue agent-0ac72188.

Resolution direction: plan-to-code (code already correct; planning must catch up)
title: docs: add loop-detection acceptance criteria to FEAT-001

acceptance: go test -race ./... passes; Tool interface or wrapper gains a Parallel bool indicator; read/glob/grep/ls are marked Parallel=true; write/edit/patch/bash/task are Parallel=false; TestRun_ParallelToolExecution exists in loop_test.go and verifies concurrent dispatch of parallel-flagged tools; Result.ToolCalls preserves original provider-returned order
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<governing>SD-001 §D4 — tools implement the Tool interface with Name(), Description(), Schema(), Execute(). SD-001 §Business Rules rule 2 — "Tool execution is sequential" (this bead changes that rule for Parallel-flagged tools). FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-02 — tool calls execute in provider order and record each call.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently loop.go executes all tool calls sequentially regardless of whether they could safely run in parallel. Codex uses an explicit per-tool parallel flag to run independent tools concurrently, reducing wall time.

What to change:

1. agent.go (Tool interface or Tool struct): Add Parallel bool field to the Tool interface or to a ToolOptions/ToolMeta struct embedded in Tool. The simplest approach: add a Parallel() bool method to the Tool interface, or add a Parallel bool to a concrete ToolDef wrapper. Choose whichever fits the existing pattern best.

2. tool/ built-ins: Mark read, glob, grep, ls as Parallel: true (read-only, safe to parallelize). Mark write, edit, patch, bash, task as Parallel: false (stateful/side-effects, must serialize).

3. loop.go tool execution: When a provider response contains multiple tool calls, partition them into parallel and serial groups. Execute all consecutive parallel tools as a group using goroutines + errgroup (or sync.WaitGroup). Execute serial tools one at a time. Preserve the original order in Result.ToolCalls (sort by provider-returned order, not completion order).

4. Tests: Add TestRun_ParallelToolExecution in loop_test.go verifying parallel-flagged tools execute concurrently (use a mock provider that returns 3 parallel tool calls in one response; use sync.WaitGroup or channel timing to confirm concurrent dispatch). go test -race ./... must pass.

Governing specs: SD-001, FEAT-001, FEAT-002.
title: feat: per-tool Parallel flag with concurrent tool execution

Remove hardcoded provider/model metadata from reports, make binary/config selection explicit, and preserve enough metadata to support evidence-grade comparison.

Verified with bash -n, py_compile, and dry-run against current and explicit binaries/configs.

acceptance:
- [ ] After the fix, a session that performs zero actual compactions emits zero compaction.start/end pairs at default verbosity (or at most one compaction.skip per turn)
- [ ] When compaction does perform real work (messages_after < messages_before OR token count drops), events are still emitted with the same shape
- [ ] Regression test asserts no-op compactions do not emit events at default verbosity
description:
The compaction package emits a compaction.start + compaction.end event pair around every turn (pre-turn check + post-turn check), even when no compaction is performed. Observed on 2026-04-18 in a DDx ddx work drain on bragi qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b at session log .ddx/executions/20260418T191746-684f612a/embedded/agent-1776539866960442383.jsonl:

  {"type":"compaction.start","data":{"messages_before":1}}
  {"type":"compaction.end","data":{"messages_after":1,"messages_before":1,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
  {"type":"compaction.start","data":{"messages_before":6,"mid_turn":true}}
  {"type":"compaction.end","data":{"messages_after":6,"messages_before":6,"mid_turn":true,"no_compaction":true,"success":false}}
  ... and so on every turn

When no_compaction:true and messages_before == messages_after, the compactor was asked, looked at the input, and decided nothing needed compacting. That is not interesting telemetry.

Real-world impact:
  - Misleads operators (the DDx team initially diagnosed cheap-tier slowness as compaction-thrash; the actual cause was per-turn latency)
  - Inflates session-log size with hundreds of no-op event pairs
  - Makes it harder to spot ACTUAL compactions when scanning the log

Fix options:
  1. Suppress the start+end pair entirely when the compactor would no-op. Only log when work was actually performed.
  2. Emit a single compaction.skip event instead of the pair, with a reason field.
  3. Demote no-op events to a low-verbosity tier behind a debug flag.

Cross-ref: filed downstream on DDx tracker as ddx-18c7de20 and is being closed in favor of this upstream bead.
notes: Superseded by agent-fbc6b277 (in the LLM-routing-consolidation epic agent-a770e781). The suppression logic was already in place in loop.go; agent-fbc6b277 added a regression test guarding it. Closing this bead as superseded.
title: compaction: suppress no-op start/end telemetry pair when nothing was compacted

<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>

<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>

Currently tool/bash.go returns raw combined output as the tool result string. Codex normalizes shell output into a human-readable structured format before inserting it into the prompt, improving model comprehension of tool results.

What to change in tool/bash.go:
- Format the Execute() return value as structured text instead of raw output:
  Exit code: <N>
  Wall time: <duration>s
  Output:
  <stdout/stderr>
- Use the existing wall-time measurement already tracked in bash.go for the duration field.
- Keep the existing output truncation (TruncateTail) applied to the Output section — truncation happens before formatting.
- If the command produced no output, the Output section should read "(no output)".
- Update bash_test.go to assert the new format.

Governing specs: SD-001, FEAT-002.
title: feat: structured tool output format for bash results

acceptance: go test -tags=integration ./provider/openai/... passes when vidar is reachable; tests skip cleanly when providers are unavailable
description:
The testing concern specifies provider integration tests against real LM Studio and real APIs using build tags. provider/openai/ has no integration-tagged tests today — all coverage is mock-based.

New file: provider/openai/discovery_integration_test.go
Build tag: //go:build integration

Required tests:

1. TestIntegration_LookupModelLimits_LMStudio
   - Probe vidar:1234 (or LMSTUDIO_URL) with flavor='lmstudio'
   - Assert limits.ContextLength > 0
   - Assert loaded_context_length is preferred over max_context_length when both present

2. TestIntegration_LookupModelLimits_Omlx
   - Probe vidar:1235 (or OMLX_URL) with flavor='omlx'
   - Assert limits.ContextLength > 0
   - Assert limits.MaxCompletionTokens > 0 (omlx exposes max_tokens, LM Studio does not)

3. TestIntegration_ProbeProviderFlavor_LMStudio
   - Probe vidar:1234 with NO flavor hint
   - Assert result == 'lmstudio'

4. TestIntegration_ProbeProviderFlavor_Omlx
   - Probe vidar:1235 with NO flavor hint
   - Assert result == 'omlx'

5. TestIntegration_DiscoverModels_LMStudio
   - Call DiscoverModels against vidar:1234
   - Assert len(ids) > 0

6. TestIntegration_DiscoverModels_Omlx
   - Call DiscoverModels against vidar:1235
   - Assert len(ids) > 0

7. TestIntegration_OmlxToolCalling (NEW)
   - Send a simple Chat() request with one tool definition to vidar:1235
   - Use a Qwen3.5 model (omlx translates its XML <function=...> format server-side)
   - Assert response.ToolCalls is non-empty (not leaked into response.Content)
   - Assert tool call has non-empty Name and valid JSON Arguments
   - Rationale: omlx docs say structured tool calls are emitted after parsing the completed
     turn, server-side. This test verifies the translation actually produces standard
     OpenAI tool_calls and does not leak raw XML markup into the content field.

Helper pattern (mirror lmStudioURL in integration_test.go):
- omlxURL(t) checks OMLX_URL env, then tries vidar:1235, localhost:1235; t.Skip if unreachable
- lmStudioURLForDiscovery(t) checks LMSTUDIO_URL env, then tries vidar:1234, bragi:1234; t.Skip if unreachable

OpenRouter tests (optional / separate): covered adequately by mock since the API is public and stable; skip for now unless a real OPENROUTER_API_KEY is available in test env.
title: test: integration tests for LookupModelLimits and probeProviderFlavor against live providers

acceptance: RouteRequest.ProviderPreference removed from public API. Profiles (named multi-axis policy bundles) documented in CONTRACT-003 as the routing knob. models.yaml surface_policy carries placement + cost + failure policy. Consumer migration note published.
description:
Surfaced while migrating ddx (consumer) from ddx-agent v0.5.0 → v0.6.0 (see DDx tracker ddx-4535f466; sibling agent bead agent-117a0868).

## Argument

v0.6.0 added `ProviderPreference` (`LocalFirst` / `SubscriptionFirst` / `LocalOnly` / `SubscriptionOnly`) to `RouteRequest` as the consumer-facing placement knob. This sits in an uncanny valley:

- It's **too abstract to be a real choice** — a caller who cares enough about placement to set it usually has more specific intent ("only local because I'm offline", "cloud because this one task is worth $2", "openrouter-haiku for this batch because I know the shape"). The 4-value enum can't express any of that.

- It's **too specific to delegate** — a caller who doesn't want to think about placement shouldn't have to pick one of four values at every call site. The right default depends on tier, cost budget, connectivity, and user policy — none of which `ProviderPreference` sees.

- Every consumer that plumbs it through ends up re-deriving the same tier→preference translation (cheap → LocalFirst, smart → SubscriptionFirst), which is (a) duplicated across consumers, (b) guaranteed to drift, and (c) not tunable without a code change.

## The richer abstraction that already almost exists: profiles

A profile is a named bundle of multi-axis policy. Examples of what profiles can express that `ProviderPreference` can't:

- `cheap`: prefer local; fall back to openrouter-haiku; never opus; stall-kill aggressively; cap reasoning at 2k tokens
- `offline`: local-only; fail loud if no local provider; no network calls at all
- `smart-but-cheap`: subscription-first but only models under $5/Mtok input
- `air-gapped`: local-only + disable telemetry + reject any http provider
- `code-high`: any provider, coding-tuned models, reasoning=high, prefer large context

`ProviderPreference` reduces all of that to one axis. A profile is a data structure, and data structures compose.

v0.6.0's `models.yaml` already has `surface_policy` per target carrying `effort_default` and `reasoning_default`. Extending that to carry placement order, cost ceilings, and failure policy is an additive change; the shape is already there.

## Proposal

1. **Remove `RouteRequest.ProviderPreference`** from the public service surface (v0.7.0 breaking change; acceptable now because adoption is low and we're already mid-migration).
2. **Reinstate profiles as the consumer-facing routing knob.** `RouteRequest.Profile` (or let `ModelRef` carry a profile name — today `ModelRef` already accepts tier names like `cheap`/`standard`/`smart`, so this may be a simple semantic consolidation, not a new field).
3. **Move placement policy into profile/catalog definitions.** Each profile/target declares its own candidate providers, candidate models, placement order, reasoning defaults, cost ceilings, and failure policy. Users retune by editing one file (`models.yaml` or user override).
4. **Pinpoint control stays as an escape hatch.** Callers that already know the exact provider + model pass `Provider` + `Model` directly and bypass the profile machinery entirely. That's the "if I'm going to micromanage I'll just set the provider" path — preserved, not denied.
5. **No per-call-site policy expression.** Consumers that don't know placement don't have to decide it. The profile does.

## Why this matters for the consumer side

DDx's ddx-4535f466 Track B was originally "audit every `ResolveRoute` call site and add the right `ProviderPreference`." If this proposal lands, that track collapses to zero: callers either use a profile (and the agent decides placement) or pin Provider+Model (and they own placement). No middle layer for DDx to translate.

## Acceptance

1. `RouteRequest.ProviderPreference` removed from the public surface; `ProviderPreference` constants no longer exported.
2. Profiles (named, multi-axis policy bundles) are the documented public routing knob. CONTRACT-003 updated.
3. `surface_policy` in `models.yaml` carries placement order + cost ceilings + failure policy, not just reasoning defaults.
4. A consumer wanting "local-first cheap" sets `Profile: "cheap"` and the catalog's cheap profile expresses "prefer local, fall back to openrouter-haiku". A consumer wanting pinpoint control sets `Provider` + `Model` and bypasses the profile machinery.
5. Migration note in CHANGELOG for any v0.6.0 consumer that started using `ProviderPreference`.
title: RouteRequest.ProviderPreference is the uncanny valley — reinstate profiles as the public routing knob

acceptance: Provider tests covering AC-FEAT-003-02, AC-FEAT-003-03, and AC-FEAT-003-04 exist and prove interrupted-stream behavior, endpoint error clarity/time bounds, and call-time auth failure semantics; the provider and default Go test suites pass.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Provider contracts should distinguish transport failures from model behavior · Constructor-vs-call-time semantics need explicit tests because they affect config loading and speculative provider creation</principles>
<concerns>stream interruption | endpoint errors | call-time auth failures</concerns>
<practices>Use `httptest` servers and short contexts for provider failure-path tests · Assert surfaced error strings include the attempted endpoint/base URL</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 for attempt metadata expectations</adrs>
<governing>FEAT-003 AC-FEAT-003-02, AC-FEAT-003-03, and AC-FEAT-003-04</governing>
</context-digest>

Provider tests already cover single-attempt behavior and some metadata, but they do not fully encode the remaining edge contracts. Add tests that:
- force interrupted/partial streaming responses in each provider package and prove partial response state is preserved while an error is surfaced
- prove unreachable local endpoints fail within a bounded timeout and include the attempted URL/base URL in the resulting error
- prove missing API keys for cloud configurations fail at call time, not provider construction time
- where practical, prove the documented default local OpenAI-compatible base URLs remain constructible without extra configuration

Relevant files: `provider/openai/openai_test.go`, `provider/anthropic/anthropic_test.go`, and supporting provider code.
title: Add provider edge-case tests for interrupted streams, endpoint errors, and call-time auth failures

acceptance: (1) reasoning_byte_limit and reasoning_stall_timeout are configurable in config.yaml (provider or global level). (2) Defaults raised to 256KB and 300s. (3) 0 means unlimited for both, matching max_iterations pattern. (4) Error messages include model name and current threshold value. (5) go test ./... passes; stream_consume_test.go covers configurable-limit paths.
description:
Hardcoded constants in stream_consume.go abort model responses prematurely for local models that use extended thinking:

- `reasoningByteLimit = 32 * 1024` (32KB) — aborts if pure reasoning_content exceeds 32K chars before any content delta
- `reasoningStallTimeout = 120 * time.Second` — aborts if only reasoning tokens arrive for 2 minutes

Observed failures:
- google/gemma-4-31b on LM Studio: reasoning overflow at 32KB on a small P3 bead
- qwen3.5-27b on LM Studio: reasoning stall on same bead
- Both models use extended thinking by default; coding tasks routinely produce >32KB of reasoning before the first content token
- Local models at 5-15 tok/s can easily spend 2+ minutes reasoning — 120s is normal behavior, not a stall
- qwen/qwen3-coder-next does NOT hit this (emits content tokens earlier)

## Fix scope

1. Make both thresholds configurable via config.yaml (reasoning_byte_limit, reasoning_stall_timeout)
2. Raise defaults: 256KB byte limit, 300s stall timeout
3. Allow 0 = unlimited for both (same pattern as max_iterations)
4. Include model name and current threshold in error messages

Relevant files: stream_consume.go (lines 13, 18), config/config.go.

GitHub issue: https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent/issues/1
resolution: fixed: reasoning_byte_limit and reasoning_stall_timeout configurable in config.yaml, defaults raised to 256KB/300s
title: fix: make reasoning stall/overflow thresholds configurable and raise defaults

acceptance:
1. Service final events expose normalized final response text through a stable public field or public method; old raw text_delta behavior remains available for one migration window.
2. Native Service.Execute maps agent result output to the normalized final text.
3. Codex, Claude, and at least one of pi, opencode, or gemini map existing stream aggregate final text into the normalized final text without exposing raw stream-frame envelopes.
4. A DDx-style reviewer verdict extraction regression parses APPROVE/BLOCK text from the normalized field and does not inspect raw codex item.completed JSON.
5. CONTRACT-003 documents the field or method plus migration guidance.
6. go test . ./internal/harnesses/... passes.
description:
Surfaced by DDx consumer ddx-f7ae036f. DDx's post-merge reviewer currently parses raw harness stream frames to extract the reviewer's final verdict text. For codex that means reading `{"type":"item.completed", ...}` envelopes directly. That's agent-owned stream format; consumers shouldn't depend on it.

## Concrete failure

DDx's verdict-extraction regex anchors on the reviewer's text, but the reviewer's text is embedded inside codex's stream-frame JSON. When the frame shape shifts (v0.6.0→v0.7.0, or per-harness variation), the regex silently misses and ParseReviewVerdict defaults to BLOCK. An APPROVE-verdicted review gets recorded as BLOCK; the bead reopens; the loop re-runs it; the reviewer APPROVEs again; same silent mis-extract. Cost-visible wasted throughput.

## Proposal

Expose the final cleaned response text from the harness at the service boundary. Two possible shapes — pick whichever fits the existing ExecuteResponse contract:

1. `ExecuteResponse.FinalText string` — the harness's user-facing final message, already stripped of stream-frame wrapping, reasoning blocks, and tool-call scaffolding.
2. New service method `Service.FinalResponse(sessionID) (string, error)` that reads from the session log and returns the normalized text.

Per-harness responsibility:
- codex: strip `{"type":"item.completed"}` envelopes, concatenate `content` fields.
- claude: last message.content in the assistant turn.
- gemini / opencode / pi: per their respective stream shapes.

DDx then parses verdict text from FinalText, not from raw frames. No consumer-side per-harness stream knowledge.

## Boundary argument

Agent owns harness dispatch and stream format (CONTRACT-003). Anything consumer-side that does `if response.startsWith('{"type":"item.')` is patching around agent ownership. This fix moves the stream-format concern back behind the service boundary where it belongs.

## Out of scope

- Tool-call output handling — separate concern.
- Context-overflow handling inside the native loop — DDx treats that as non-terminal; if agent's native loop emits context-overflow as a recoverable final response shape, consumers will see it via FinalText naturally.

Keep scope tight: add a public final_text field or equivalent public final-response method, thread native result.Output and subprocess aggregate FinalText into it, update CONTRACT-003, and add a DDx-style verdict parser regression that reads only normalized final text.

name: ddx
description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
---

# DDx

DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
items with acceptance criteria), an agent service (harness dispatch
with profile-first routing), a persona system (bindable AI
personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.

## How this skill works

The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.

**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
guidance, not this overview alone.**

## Vocabulary

Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
exact definitions.

- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
  `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` (alias for
  `ddx agent execute-loop`); see `reference/work.md`.
- **Execute-bead** — the primitive: run one agent on one bead in an
  isolated worktree. `ddx agent execute-bead <id>`. The loop wraps
  this.
- **Execute-loop** — the queue-drain orchestrator; picks ready beads
  one at a time, calls execute-bead, closes on success, unclaims on
  failure. `ddx agent execute-loop` (or `ddx work`).
- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010); broader
  than execute-bead. Includes execution definitions, execution
  records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/executions/<id>/`.
- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
  below).
- **Harness** — the abstraction DDx uses to dispatch an agent
  (subprocess for claude/codex/gemini; embedded for the built-in
  `agent` harness). `ddx agent list`, `ddx agent capabilities`.
- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
  addendum to `ddx agent run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
  personas.
- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
- **Profile** — intent-based routing policy for `ddx agent run`:
  `cheap` (low-cost), `fast` (low-latency), `smart` (balanced
  default). `--profile cheap|fast|smart`.
- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
  `ddx install <name>`.
- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx's `ddx agent run`
  (especially with `--quorum`) is the portable primitive; how a
  harness wraps that in a subagent is harness business. This skill
  does not take a position on subagent orchestration.
- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
  `ddx upgrade`.
- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
  acceptance criteria. **Quorum review** (`ddx agent run
  --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>`) dispatches a review prompt
  across multiple harnesses and aggregates. See `reference/review.md`.
- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
  via `spec-id`.
- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
  at its governing artifact.

## Intent router

Before responding, read the matching file.

| User says / asks about | Read this file |
|---|---|
| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
| "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "run work", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
| "run an agent", "dispatch", harnesses, profiles, models, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |

If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
user which concept they mean rather than guessing.

## Top-level policy reminders

These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
reference file; do not violate them.

- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
  bead history and cannot be audited.
- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
- **Preserve execute-bead commit history.** Branches containing
  `ddx agent execute-bead` commits carry an audit trail. **Never
  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
  branches.
- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
  manual parallel work should too.
- **Profile-first agent dispatch.** Default to
  `ddx agent run --profile smart` (or `cheap`/`fast` for specific
  intents). Only override with `--harness`/`--model`/`--effort`
  when pinning for a controlled test or known provider bug.

## Links out

- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
  executions, and skills features.
- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
- Open standard this skill conforms to:
  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).

DDx has two distinct review concepts. They are **not interchangeable**.

| Concept | Command | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| **Bead review** | `ddx bead review <id>` | Grades a bead's implementation against its declared acceptance criteria. Per-AC verdict with evidence. |
| **Quorum review** | `ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` | Dispatches a review prompt across multiple harnesses and aggregates the verdicts. Used for high-stakes or adversarial checks. |

## Bead review

```bash
ddx bead review <bead-id>
```

Generates a review-ready prompt that includes:

- Bead title, description, acceptance criteria (verbatim).
- Governing artifact content (follows `spec-id`).
- The diff since the bead's base commit (execution evidence commits
  excluded — see the related bug ddx-39e27896 for exclusion
  pathspec).
- Review instructions: output contract, verdict taxonomy.

The reviewer (whoever receives the prompt — a human, a harness via
`ddx agent run`, or a quorum) produces a structured verdict:

```
## Bead review: ddx-<id>

### Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK

### Per-criterion

1. [AC text]
   Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK
   Evidence: <file:line, test output, diff hunk, or explicit "not verifiable">

2. [AC text]
   ...

### Summary
<1-3 sentences of overall context>

### Findings
<detailed issues, if any; omit when verdict is APPROVE with no findings>
```

The review output can be stored as bead evidence:

```bash
ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body <path-to-review.md>
```

## Quorum review

```bash
ddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini \
  --prompt review-prompt.md
```

`--quorum=<policy>` values:

- `majority` — at least ⌈N/2⌉ harnesses must agree on the verdict.
- `unanimous` — all harnesses must agree.
- `any` — pass if any harness APPROVES (rarely useful; adversarial
  checks should require stronger consensus).

Use quorum when:

- The change is high-stakes (migrations, security surface, API
  contracts, auth).
- The review surface is controversial and one harness's opinion
  isn't enough.
- You want adversarial pressure against a single-model blindspot
  (different model families have different failure modes).

The aggregated output shows each harness's verdict side-by-side plus
the aggregate decision. Disagreements are surfaced, not hidden.

## Required evidence format

Both review kinds share an evidence contract: **no verdict without
evidence.**

- "APPROVE" with no `Evidence:` lines → reject as malformed.
- "REQUEST_CHANGES" without pointing at the change needed → reject.
- "BLOCK" without a reason → reject (see the related bug
  ddx-39e27896 about `review-malfunction` retries).

Evidence takes one of these forms:

1. **File:line reference**: `cli/internal/bead/store.go:142`
2. **Test output**: `FAIL: TestBeadClose_UnclaimsOnNonSuccess (0.01s)`
3. **Diff hunk**: a short quote from the diff showing the offending
   or approved change.
4. **Explicit "not verifiable"**: the AC is written in a way that
   cannot be mechanically checked; flag this as a bead-authoring
   issue rather than silently approving.

## Anti-patterns

- **Hallucinated approval.** "Looks good to me!" with no per-AC
  grading. The review is worthless.
- **Reviewing the prompt instead of the diff.** The reviewer evaluated
  "what you intended" not "what you actually changed." Verdict must
  reference the diff or the resulting code.
- **Verdict without evidence.** Every verdict must have at least one
  evidence line; APPROVE is no exception (evidence can be "AC met:
  `go test ./foo/... passes` output attached").
- **Silencing disagreement in quorum.** If two harnesses disagree,
  don't collapse to a single "decision." Surface the disagreement
  in the output and let the human decide.
- **Quorum for routine work.** Single-harness bead review is fine
  for most beads. Reserve quorum for the cases that justify the
  cost.

## Subagents: not this skill's business

Some harnesses can run a quorum in forked/isolated subagent
contexts; each harness implements this differently. This skill
doesn't specify how isolation is achieved —
`ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` is the
portable invocation, and the harness decides whether to fork,
spawn, or run inline.

## CLI reference

```bash
# Bead review (single-reviewer AC grading)
ddx bead review <id>                           # generate review prompt
ddx bead review <id> --execute --harness claude  # dispatch and grade
ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body r.md  # store review

# Quorum review (multi-harness)
ddx agent run --quorum=majority \
  --harnesses=claude,codex \
  --prompt review.md

ddx agent run --quorum=unanimous \
  --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini \
  --prompt review.md
```

Full flag list: `ddx bead review --help`, `ddx agent run --help`.

   319	// New constructs a DdxAgent. When opts.ServiceConfig is nil, New attempts to
   320	// load configuration automatically:
   321	//  1. If opts.ConfigPath is non-empty, load from filepath.Dir(opts.ConfigPath).
   322	//  2. Otherwise, load from the default global config location.
   323	//
   324	// Automatic loading requires the config package to be imported somewhere in the
   325	// binary (it registers the loader via init). If the config package is not
   326	// imported and ServiceConfig is nil, the service starts without provider config
   327	// (ListProviders/HealthCheck will return errors until config is injected).
   328	func New(opts ServiceOptions) (DdxAgent, error) {
   329		if opts.ServiceConfig == nil && loadServiceConfig != nil && shouldAutoLoadServiceConfig(opts) {
   330			dir := ""
   331			if opts.ConfigPath != "" {
   332				dir = filepath.Dir(opts.ConfigPath)
   333			}
   334			sc, err := loadServiceConfig(dir)
   335			if err != nil {
   336				return nil, fmt.Errorf("agent.New: load config: %w", err)
   337			}
   338			opts.ServiceConfig = sc
   339		}
   340		return &service{
   341			opts:     opts,
   342			registry: harnesses.NewRegistry(),
   343			hub:      newSessionHub(),
   344		}, nil
   345	}

   295		// Status to stderr
   296		fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "[%s] tokens: %d in / %d out", result.Status, result.Tokens.Input, result.Tokens.Output)
   297		if result.CostUSD > 0 {
   298			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, " | cost: $%.4f", result.CostUSD)
   299		}
   300		fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr)
   301	
   302		switch result.Status {
   303		case agent.StatusSuccess, agent.StatusIterationLimit:
   304			return 0
   305		default:
   306			return 1
   307		}
   308	}
   309	
   310	func executeViaServiceContract(ctx context.Context, req agent.Request, provider agent.Provider) (agent.Result, error) {
   311		svc, err := agent.New(agent.ServiceOptions{})
   312		if err != nil {
   313			return agent.Result{}, err
   314		}
   315	
   316		temperature := float32(0)
   317		if req.Temperature != nil {
   318			temperature = float32(*req.Temperature)
   319		}
   320		ch, err := svc.Execute(ctx, agent.ServiceExecuteRequest{
   321			Prompt:         req.Prompt,
   322			SystemPrompt:   req.SystemPrompt,
   323			Model:          req.ResolvedModel,
   324			Provider:       req.SelectedProvider,
   325			Harness:        "agent",
   326			WorkDir:        req.WorkDir,
   327			Temperature:    temperature,
   328			Seed:           req.Seed,
   329			Reasoning:      req.Reasoning,
   330			Metadata:       req.Metadata,
   331			NativeProvider: chatOnlyProvider{inner: provider},
   332			PreResolved: &agent.RouteDecision{
   333				Harness:  "agent",
   334				Provider: req.SelectedProvider,
   335				Model:    req.ResolvedModel,
   336				Reason:   "cli pre-resolved provider",
   337			},
   338		})
   339		if err != nil {
   340			return agent.Result{}, err
   341		}
   342	
   343		result := agent.Result{
   344			SelectedProvider:  req.SelectedProvider,
   345			SelectedRoute:     req.SelectedRoute,
   346			RequestedModel:    req.RequestedModel,
   347			RequestedModelRef: req.RequestedModelRef,
   348			ResolvedModelRef:  req.ResolvedModelRef,
   349			ResolvedModel:     req.ResolvedModel,
   350			Reasoning:         req.Reasoning,
   351			Model:             req.ResolvedModel,
   352		}
   353		var output strings.Builder
   354		var sawFinal bool
   355		for ev := range ch {
   356			switch string(ev.Type) {
   357			case "routing_decision":
   358				var data struct {
   359					SessionID string `json:"session_id"`
   360				}
   361				if err := json.Unmarshal(ev.Data, &data); err == nil && data.SessionID != "" {
   362					result.SessionID = data.SessionID
   363				}
   364			case "text_delta":
   365				var data struct {
   366					Text string `json:"text"`
   367				}
   368				if err := json.Unmarshal(ev.Data, &data); err == nil {
   369					output.WriteString(data.Text)
   370				}
   371			case "final":
   372				sawFinal = true
   373				var data struct {
   374					Status string `json:"status"`
   375					Error  string `json:"error"`
   376					Usage  *struct {
   377						InputTokens  int `json:"input_tokens"`
   378						OutputTokens int `json:"output_tokens"`
   379						TotalTokens  int `json:"total_tokens"`
   380					} `json:"usage"`
   381					CostUSD       float64 `json:"cost_usd"`
   382					RoutingActual *struct {
   383						Provider string   `json:"provider"`
   384						Model    string   `json:"model"`
   385						Chain    []string `json:"fallback_chain_fired"`
   386					} `json:"routing_actual"`
   387				}
   388				if err := json.Unmarshal(ev.Data, &data); err != nil {
   389					return result, fmt.Errorf("decode service final event: %w", err)
   390				}
   391				result.Status = serviceStatusToLegacyStatus(data.Status)
   392				if data.Error != "" {
   393					result.Error = errors.New(data.Error)
   394				}
   395				if data.Usage != nil {
   396					result.Tokens.Input = data.Usage.InputTokens
   397					result.Tokens.Output = data.Usage.OutputTokens
   398					result.Tokens.Total = data.Usage.TotalTokens
   399				}
   400				result.CostUSD = data.CostUSD
   401				if data.RoutingActual != nil {
   402					result.SelectedProvider = data.RoutingActual.Provider
   403					result.ResolvedModel = data.RoutingActual.Model
   404					result.Model = data.RoutingActual.Model
   405					result.AttemptedProviders = append([]string(nil), data.RoutingActual.Chain...)
   406				}
   407			}
   408		}
   409		if !sawFinal {
   410			return result, fmt.Errorf("service contract execution ended without final event")
   411		}
   412		result.Output = output.String()
   413		return result, nil
   414	}

func init() {
	// Register the config loader into the root package so that agent.New can
	// load configuration without importing this package directly (which would
	// create an import cycle: config → agent → config).
	agent.RegisterConfigLoader(func(dir string) (agent.ServiceConfig, error) {
		cfg, err := Load(dir)
		if err != nil {
			return nil, err
		}
		return &configServiceConfig{cfg: cfg, baseDir: dir}, nil
	})
}

// configServiceConfig wraps a loaded *Config and satisfies agent.ServiceConfig.
// It is the agent-internal equivalent of DDx's ServiceConfigAdapter.
type configServiceConfig struct {
	cfg     *Config
	baseDir string
}

func (c *configServiceConfig) ProviderNames() []string {
	if c.cfg == nil {
		return nil
	}
	return c.cfg.ProviderNames()
}

func (c *configServiceConfig) DefaultProviderName() string {
	if c.cfg == nil {
		return ""
	}
	return c.cfg.DefaultName()
}

func (c *configServiceConfig) Provider(name string) (agent.ServiceProviderEntry, bool) {
	if c.cfg == nil {
		return agent.ServiceProviderEntry{}, false
	}
	pc, ok := c.cfg.Providers[name]
	if !ok {
		return agent.ServiceProviderEntry{}, false
	}
	endpoints := make([]agent.ServiceProviderEndpoint, 0, len(pc.Endpoints))
	for _, endpoint := range pc.Endpoints {
		endpoints = append(endpoints, agent.ServiceProviderEndpoint{
			Name:    endpoint.Name,
			BaseURL: endpoint.BaseURL,
		})
	}
	return agent.ServiceProviderEntry{
		Type:      pc.Type,
		BaseURL:   pc.BaseURL,
		Endpoints: endpoints,
		APIKey:    pc.APIKey,
		Model:     pc.Model,
	}, true
}

func (c *configServiceConfig) ModelRouteNames() []string {
	if c.cfg == nil {
		return nil
	}
	return c.cfg.ModelRouteNames()
}

func (c *configServiceConfig) ModelRouteCandidates(routeName string) []string {
	if c.cfg == nil {
		return nil
	}
	route, ok := c.cfg.ModelRoutes[routeName]

// Load reads configuration from .agent/config.yaml (project) and
// ~/.config/agent/config.yaml (global), with env var expansion.
// Project config overrides global config. If no config files exist,
// returns defaults.
func Load(workDir string) (*Config, error) {
	cfg := Defaults()

	// Try global config first, then project config (project wins)
	var paths []string
	if home, err := os.UserHomeDir(); err == nil {
		paths = append(paths, filepath.Join(home, ".config", globalConfigDirName, "config.yaml"))
	}
	paths = append(paths, filepath.Join(workDir, projectConfigDir, "config.yaml"))

	for _, p := range paths {
		data, err := safefs.ReadFile(p)
		if err != nil {
			continue
		}
		expanded := expandEnvVars(string(data))
		if err := rejectLegacyProviderReasoningKeys([]byte(expanded)); err != nil {
			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: parsing %s: %w", p, err)
		}
		if err := yaml.Unmarshal([]byte(expanded), &cfg); err != nil {
			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: parsing %s: %w", p, err)
		}
	}

	// Migrate legacy flat format
	cfg.migrateLegacy()

	// Apply env var overrides to default provider
	cfg.applyEnvOverrides()

	if err := cfg.finalize(); err != nil {
		return nil, err
	}

	return &cfg, nil
}

acceptance:
- [ ] ListProviders returns one ProviderInfo per configured provider with accurate live status
- [ ] IsDefault matches default_provider config
- [ ] DefaultModel matches per-provider configured default
- [ ] CooldownState is populated when applicable (test induces cooldown)
- [ ] HealthCheck(provider) updates internal state observable via subsequent ListProviders
- [ ] HealthCheck(harness) routes to the harness implementation
description:
Implement two methods together since HealthCheck for Target.Type=provider exercises the same probe code as ListProviders status:

ListProviders(ctx) → []ProviderInfo where each entry includes Name, Type, BaseURL, Status (connected/unreachable/error), ModelCount, Capabilities, IsDefault, DefaultModel, CooldownState (nil if not in cooldown).

HealthCheck(ctx, target) where target = {Type: "harness"|"provider", Name: string}. Triggers a fresh probe and updates internal state (so subsequent ListX reflects it). Returns nil if reachable, error otherwise.

CooldownState surfaces the same data the agent uses internally for routing demotion. Read from the routing state store (ports from DDx routing_signals.go in subsequent beads).
title: service: implement DdxAgent.ListProviders + HealthCheck with polymorphic Target

FEAT-003 still claims a provider matrix that the repo does not currently encode as automated acceptance coverage. Add build-tagged integration/e2e tests that:
- exercise the same basic prompt path against at least one local OpenAI-compatible backend and Anthropic when credentials/backends are available
- skip explicitly with actionable messages when the required environment variables or services are missing
- preserve a deterministic assertion surface (status success, non-zero usage, provider/model attribution) rather than overfitting to exact wording from the model
