acceptance:
1. Claude runner applies supported request controls (model, reasoning/effort, permissions, workdir) to the CLI invocation, or metadata/routing explicitly marks unsupported controls so they cannot be silently claimed.
2. Tests capture argv/stdin/workdir behavior for model, reasoning, permissions, and prompt delivery.
3. Cancellation/error status, final text, usage input/output/total tokens, model/reasoning discovery, and quota replay evidence remain covered.
4. primary-harness baseline evidence marks Claude pass only for rows backed by tests/evidence and identifies blockers otherwise.
5. Claude AutoRoutingEligible is true only after AC1-AC4 are satisfied.
6. go test ./... passes.
description:
Claude is temporarily explicit-only for automatic routing because the current runner advertises model/reasoning/permission support in metadata, but the subprocess execution path does not apply those request controls before launching the CLI. Before re-enabling AutoRoutingEligible, the runner must pass model, reasoning/effort, permission, and workdir controls through the Claude CLI or explicitly downgrade unsupported controls in metadata and routing.

In-scope files are expected to include internal/harnesses/claude/, internal/harnesses/registry.go, service/routing tests, and docs/helix/02-design/primary-harness-capability-baseline.md.

Out-of-scope: changing unrelated harness priorities or promoting secondary harnesses.
notes: Follow-up hardening: Claude quota routing now rejects incomplete cache/cassette evidence, including missing account plan/source, invalid remaining/limit values, missing session window, or missing weekly window. Parser coverage now includes ANSI-stripped, decimal-percent, and standalone plan variants.
title: harness-claude: restore auto-routing after request-control coverage

acceptance: (1) GET /v1/models is enumerated by the discovery layer; the catalog is the canonical model-ID source per provider. (2) Bare model names ('qwen3-coder-next') passed by callers are normalized to canonical IDs ('qwen/qwen3-coder-next') by suffix-match against the catalog, with explicit ambiguity errors. (3) providers command shows http-connected and inference-ready-models per provider. (4) Routing policy consumes inference-ready-models, not 'connected (N models)'. (5) Regression test uses a mock LM Studio that lists 'qwen/foo' but rejects 'foo' on chat/completions; discovery normalizes 'foo' → 'qwen/foo' and the probe succeeds.
description:
Discovered 2026-04-15 while routing work to vidar-coder (LM Studio on vidar:1234).

## Two-part bug

### Part 1: bare-name JIT miss (the actual root cause)

Initial probe: `POST /v1/chat/completions {model: 'qwen3-coder-next', ...}` → 400 'No models loaded'.

We assumed LM Studio had no model loaded. Wrong. The actual model is listed by /v1/models as 'qwen/qwen3-coder-next' (with the 'qwen/' prefix). LM Studio's just-in-time loading IS enabled and DOES auto-load models on demand — but only when the requested model ID matches one in /v1/models exactly. Bare-name 'qwen3-coder-next' didn't match, so JIT couldn't fire, and LM Studio returned the misleading 'No models loaded' error instead of 'unknown model'.

Verified by re-probing with 'qwen/qwen3-coder-next' — JIT fired, model loaded, responded 'OK' in ~60s on cold start.

### Part 2: discovery should be standard practice

The routing/probe layer should never trust user-supplied bare model names against an LM Studio provider. The standard discovery flow should be:

1. GET /v1/models → enumerate the catalog the server is offering
2. For each catalog ID, optionally probe POST /v1/chat/completions with a 1-token request and a generous timeout (60s+ on cold start, 3s on warm)
3. Cache the (provider, model_id) → inference-ready bool with a short TTL
4. When user code asks for 'model qwen3-coder-next' against this provider, normalize against the discovered catalog: if there's exactly one match by suffix, accept it; if zero or multiple, error with the candidate list

This converts the 'No models loaded' misleading error into a clear 'requested model qwen3-coder-next not in catalog; closest match: qwen/qwen3-coder-next' (or just transparent normalization).

## Impact on cost-tiered routing

The cost-tiered routing policy (DDx bead ddx-dc231144 tier-based auto-escalation) needs reliable provider readiness signals. Without discovery + normalization:
- bare-name configs silently fail and force escalation
- the 'connected (N models)' status is unreliable as a routing input
- every misrouted attempt burns latency and forces the next tier

## Fix scope

- Update routing/health probe in cmd/ddx-agent/routing.go (or equivalent) to enumerate /v1/models and probe each listed model with its exact ID
- Add a model-id normalization layer that maps bare names to catalog IDs by suffix-match (with explicit error on ambiguity)
- Cache probe results per (provider, model) for a short TTL (30s reasonable)
- Surface the distinction in 'ddx-agent providers' output: http-connected (from /v1/models) vs inference-ready-models (the subset that actually responds)
resolution: fixed: /v1/models discovery + bare-name normalization in provider/openai
title: bug: /v1/models is not a liveness probe — providers shows 'connected' when LM Studio has no model loaded

acceptance: go test ./provider/openai/... covers all 10 cases above; no new test failures
description:
AC-FEAT-003-06 and AC-FEAT-003-07 specify that limit discovery and flavor detection must be tested.

Current state: discovery_test.go covers DiscoverModels, RankModels, NormalizeModelID, and lazy model discovery but has NO tests for:
- LookupModelLimits (all dispatch paths)
- probeProviderFlavor (omlx probe wins, lmstudio probe wins, both fail, flavor param bypasses probe)
- lmstudioLimits (loaded_context_length vs max_context_length priority)
- omlxLimits (model match by EqualFold)
- openrouterLimits (hyphen-dot normalization)

Required tests (httptest.NewServer mocks):
1. LookupModelLimits: explicit flavor='lmstudio' routes without probing
2. LookupModelLimits: explicit flavor='omlx' routes without probing
3. LookupModelLimits: explicit flavor='openrouter' routes without probing
4. LookupModelLimits: ambiguous URL fires probe; omlx probe wins
5. LookupModelLimits: ambiguous URL fires probe; lmstudio probe wins
6. LookupModelLimits: both probes fail → falls back to openAIIdentity result
7. LookupModelLimits: unreachable endpoint returns ModelLimits{} without error
8. lmstudioLimits: prefers loaded_context_length over max_context_length
9. omlxLimits: case-insensitive model match
10. openrouterLimits: hyphen-dot normalization (claude-haiku-4-5 == claude-haiku-4.5)
title: test: LookupModelLimits and probeProviderFlavor unit tests

acceptance: All child beads created from the 2026-04-10 acceptance review are closed; each targeted AC ID has an automated test or explicit environment-gated integration test path, and the default Go test suite remains green.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Docs are authoritative · Acceptance criteria must be deterministic and executable · Favor targeted regression coverage over vague smoke tests</principles>
<concerns>go-std | helix contract-driven design | test traceability</concerns>
<practices>Feature specs own authoritative AC IDs · Build-tag external tests must skip explicitly when env is unavailable · CLI behavior should be asserted through built binaries or `go run` wrappers, not by scraping implementation internals</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 observability surfaces and cost attribution</adrs>
<governing>PRD acceptance sketches now defer to feature-level AC tables added on 2026-04-10 in FEAT-001 through FEAT-007. This epic exists to close the remaining test coverage gaps against those AC tables.</governing>
</context-digest>

Review completed on 2026-04-10 tightened the feature specs with stable AC IDs. The current repo has broad unit coverage, but there are still uncovered or weakly covered criteria around runtime edge cases, CLI behavior, provider failure paths, routing attribution, usage/reporting surfaces, and installer/update flows.

Create and close child test beads until every acceptance criterion introduced in the 2026-04-10 feature-spec review either has a deterministic automated test in this repo or an explicit environment-gated integration/e2e test surface with skip semantics. Keep scope to test coverage and traceability; implementation fixes should only be made when required to make the new tests pass.
title: Close acceptance-test coverage gaps against feature AC matrix

acceptance:
1. `internal/harnesses/codex` contains a sanitized fixture or synthetic fixture that represents the current Codex JSONL tool call and tool result shape.
2. Codex stream parser tests prove tool call IDs, tool names, JSON inputs, outputs/errors, ordering, and final text/usage are preserved.
3. `Service.Execute` golden replay for the codex harness includes tool-event evidence and passes without live credentials.
4. If Codex tool events are now supported, `service_capabilities.go`, `service_test.go`, and CONTRACT-003 promote codex `ToolEvents` from unsupported to optional with a clear detail string.
5. `go test ./internal/harnesses/codex` and `go test -tags=integration . -run TestHarnessGoldenReplay_ServiceExecute/codex` pass.
description:
Codex is a primary harness and should be as close as practical to Claude and the native agent harness. Today `internal/harnesses/codex/stream.go` only maps text and usage events from `codex exec --json`; all other event types are skipped, so CONTRACT-003 `tool_call` and `tool_result` events are unsupported while Claude and native agent expose them.

Implement Codex JSONL tool-event parsing from current Codex CLI output. If the exact live schema is uncertain, first capture a sanitized fixture from a Codex run that performs a tool call, then implement the parser against that fixture. Keep opencode/pi/gemini tool parity out of scope for this bead.
title: harness: map codex tool events to CONTRACT-003

acceptance:
- [ ] DdxAgent.ListHarnesses(ctx) returns one HarnessInfo per registered harness
- [ ] SupportedPermissions and SupportedEfforts are accurate per harness (claude has all 3 of each; gemini has different subsets)
- [ ] Quota field is non-nil for claude/codex when their tmux probe succeeds
- [ ] Test exercises at least claude + 1 other harness
description:
Implement DdxAgent.ListHarnesses(ctx) per CONTRACT-001. Returns []HarnessInfo where each entry includes:
- Name, Type ("native" | "subprocess")
- Available, Path, Error
- IsLocal, IsSubscription, ExactPinSupport
- SupportedPermissions (subset of safe/supervised/unrestricted) — derived from PermissionArgs map keys
- SupportedEfforts (subset of low/medium/high) — derived from ReasoningLevels
- CostClass (local | cheap | medium | expensive)
- Quota (live; nil if not applicable; for claude/codex this consults the cache + tmux probe)

Backed by the registry+state ports (agent-X for registry+state). Per-harness Quota field populated from the harness implementation when available.
title: service: implement DdxAgent.ListHarnesses with rich HarnessInfo (install, perms, efforts, quota)

acceptance:
1. ExecuteRequest, RouteRequest, and internal/routing.Request expose ProviderPreference with values empty/local-first/subscription-first/local-only/subscription-only.
2. ResolveRoute validates ProviderPreference and normalizes empty to local-first.
3. Routing filters subscription candidates for local-only and local candidates for subscription-only.
4. Routing biases local-first or subscription-first within candidates that satisfy the same model/tier intent.
5. Subscription scoring considers live quota state, quota percent used, stale quota state, and burn trend where available.
6. Service routing inputs no longer hardcode all quotas as healthy when harness/provider quota state is known.
7. Unit tests cover default, subscription-first, local-only, subscription-only, local cooldown fallback, exhausted subscription quota, and stale quota behavior.
description:
Implement the routing policy from docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-19-provider-routing-tool-output.md.

Add ProviderPreference to the service and routing contracts. Empty/default is local-first within the requested tier. `subscription-first` reverses the within-tier bias. `local-only` and `subscription-only` are hard filters. Quota and trend signals bias subscription candidates among candidates that already satisfy the request; they do not automatically escalate model tier or quality intent.

The service must populate real quota/cooldown/freshness inputs where available instead of hardcoding QuotaOK for all candidates.

## Review: agent-4c482f60 iter 1

| # | Item | Grade | Evidence |

| 1 | ExecuteRequest, RouteRequest, internal/routing.Request expose ProviderPreference | APPROVE | `service.go:199-201` (ExecuteRequest), `service.go:259` (RouteRequest), `engine.go:23,33-36` (routing.Request) |
| 2 | ResolveRoute validates ProviderPreference and normalizes empty to local-first | APPROVE | `service_routing.go:21-30` — switch rejects invalid values; empty defaults to `local-first` |
| 3 | Routing filters subscription candidates for local-only and local candidates for subscription-only | APPROVE | `engine.go:308-317` (hard filter in `buildHarnessCandidates`); `engine.go:319-325` (`SubscriptionOK` hard gate) |
| 4 | Routing biases local-first or subscription-first within same model/tier intent | APPROVE | `score.go:63-71` — `scorePolicy` applies +30 local bonus for `local-first` and +30 subscription bonus for `subscription-first` |
| 5 | Subscription scoring considers live quota state, percent used, stale, and burn trend | APPROVE | `score.go:73-95` — stale penalty (-15), trend adjustments (exhausting -40, burning -20, healthy +10), near-limit penalty (`>=80%`); `service_routing.go:118-136` populates these from `ReadClaudeQuotaRoutingDecision` |
| 6 | Service routing inputs no longer hardcode all quotas as healthy | APPROVE | `service_routing.go:118-124` (claude: `QuotaOK = dec.PreferClaude` from live harness state); `service_routing.go:142-148` (non-Claude subscription harnesses: `SubscriptionOK = false`, hard-blocked when exhausted) |
| 7 | Unit tests cover all AC scenarios | APPROVE | `preference_test.go` — 7 new tests covering all AC7 scenarios; all routing tests pass (`go test ./internal/routing/...`) |

### Summary

All 7 acceptance criteria are fully and correctly implemented. The diff adds `ProviderPreference` to the three relevant request types, populates real quota/cooldown/freshness signals from the Claude harness for `claude` and hard-blocks non-Claude subscription harnesses (`SubscriptionOK=false`), implements all four preference values with proper normalization and hard gating, biases scoring via `scorePolicy`, and covers all scenarios with passing unit tests. No findings.

acceptance:
1. Gemini model discovery returns a non-empty tested model set and profile/catalog routing resolves smart/standard/cheap or documented supported equivalents to concrete Gemini model IDs without empty-model routes.
2. Gemini reasoning/thinking support is either implemented with tests and capability metadata or explicitly documented/declared unsupported with routing tests proving reasoning requests do not select Gemini.
3. Usage/token extraction is verified against real recorded Gemini CLI output and replay/fake tests; final usage metadata reports input/total/output tokens when the CLI exposes them.
4. Quota/account/auth status has documented behavior and tests, including stale/missing evidence handling.
5. Capability matrix and harness-golden-integration docs show Gemini evidence source, freshness, and record/replay commands or explicit non-applicability.
6. Only after AC1-AC5, Gemini is promoted out of experimental priority and automatic smart-tier routing tests prove it wins only when it is actually the best eligible candidate.
7. go test ./... passes.
description:
Gemini should become a primary automatic routing target only if the harness reaches the same operational bar as primary targets. Today it has a basic subprocess runner and fake-output usage parser tests, but no authenticated live evidence, model discovery, reasoning control, quota/account status, or catalog/profile mapping that prevents empty-model smart routes.

Promotion requirements:
- discover supported Gemini models from the CLI/API surface and map catalog/profile refs to concrete Gemini model IDs
- implement and test any Gemini reasoning/thinking controls if the CLI supports them; otherwise document unsupported status in the capability matrix
- validate usage/token extraction against real Gemini CLI output, not only fake stats lines
- add quota/account/auth status evidence where available, with freshness/staleness behavior
- add record/replay or equivalent credential-free evidence for all supported Gemini-specific capability surfaces
- only after the above, change routing metadata so Gemini is no longer experimental and can compete in automatic smart-tier routing

In-scope files are expected to include internal/harnesses/gemini/, internal/harnesses/registry.go, service_capabilities.go, service_routing.go or internal/routing tests, and docs/helix/02-design/harness-golden-integration.md.

Out-of-scope: changing unrelated harness priorities or weakening Claude/Codex support gates.

acceptance:
1. New service instances and normal status/routing calls trigger best-effort asynchronous Claude/Codex quota refresh when cache is missing or stale, with throttle/cooldown so calls do not spawn repeated PTY probes.
2. ResolveRoute remains cache-only in the foreground: it does not block on live probes, and stale/missing cache still makes the primary harness ineligible until refresh succeeds.
3. Tests prove refresh is initiated automatically without explicit HealthCheck, and that repeated calls within the margin do not repeatedly probe.
4. go test ./... passes.
description:
Claude and Codex automatic routing correctly consumes durable quota caches, but cache refresh is currently owned only by explicit HealthCheck calls. That leaves humans or external polling responsible for keeping caches fresh. Move ownership into the service: normal service lifecycle/activity should trigger throttled asynchronous refresh for primary subscription harnesses while foreground routing continues reading only durable cache state.

In-scope files: service.go, service_providers.go, service_routing.go, service_status/list harness tests, service provider tests.

Out-of-scope: synchronous live probes in ResolveRoute or promoting secondary harnesses.
notes: Implemented service-owned primary quota refresh. New services start a best-effort Claude/Codex refresh for missing/stale/incomplete caches and wait only the bounded startup window; ListHarnesses, RouteStatus, and ResolveRoute trigger debounced async refresh without blocking foreground routing; optional QuotaRefreshInterval enables server timer refresh. Added tests for automatic/throttled refresh, startup wait/timeout continuation, timer refresh, and ResolveRoute nonblocking behavior. Verified go test ./... passes.
title: service: automate primary harness quota cache refresh

acceptance: ddx-agent providers lists all configured providers with name/type/url/model/status; status checks connectivity; --json flag for machine output; tabular display
description: CLI providers listing per SD-005.
title: Implement ddx-agent providers command

acceptance:
1. Embedding callers can express unset, auto, explicit off, low, medium, high, extended reasoning levels, and explicit numeric values through one public `Reasoning` field.
2. Public Go API includes named constants and `ReasoningTokens(n int) Reasoning`; no separate public `ReasoningBudget` field is introduced.
3. Shared leaf package parser/normalizer is used by root `agent` and is importable by internal packages without root-agent import cycles.
4. Unit test proves `Request.Reasoning` wins over legacy thinking/effort-style aliases if both are set during migration.
5. Unit test proves unset request reasoning preserves a provider-level positive thinking/reasoning budget.
6. Unit test proves request-level `reasoning=off` and `ReasoningTokens(0)` suppress a provider-level positive thinking budget.
7. Unit test proves request-level `low|medium|high` maps to expected internal numeric budgets for a numeric-only provider path.
8. Unit test proves positive numeric reasoning values are parsed as explicit numeric budgets.
9. Unit test proves extended values such as `x-high` normalize to `xhigh` and are preserved for adapters that advertise support.
10. Unit test proves explicit unsupported extended values and over-limit numeric values fail clearly rather than silently downgrading or dropping.
11. Unit test proves `omlx`/unsupported flavors do not serialize reasoning/thinking fields when unsupported.
12. Unit test or shaped HTTP assertion proves sync `Chat` and streaming `ChatStream` resolve reasoning consistently.
13. Any public embedding API rename/type break is documented in a changelog or migration note.
14. `go test ./...` passes.
description:
Add embedding-level support for explicit reasoning control without losing the ability to distinguish unset from off. Current state: `agent.Options` has `ThinkingLevel` and `ThinkingBudget int`, but `0` means unset; `internal/provider/openai` starts from provider config budget and only overrides with request budget when `opts.ThinkingBudget > 0`. Therefore an embedding caller cannot force reasoning/thinking off when provider config has a positive budget. `agent.Request` also has no reasoning field, so `loop.go` only forwards `MaxTokens` into provider options. Current harness metadata also only models low/medium/high for some harnesses, while others already expose values like minimal/max; this must become extensible.

Required behavior:
- Create a small leaf package such as `internal/reasoning` that owns the canonical Reasoning scalar, constants, `ReasoningTokens(n)` helper, parser, normalization, and parsed policy representation. Root package `agent` re-exports the public type/constants/helpers for embedding callers. Internal packages import the leaf package to avoid importing root `agent`.
- Add public request-level reasoning control as one field, preferably `Request.Reasoning Reasoning` where `type Reasoning string` is re-exported from the leaf package.
- Add named constants for public values (`ReasoningAuto`, `ReasoningOff`, `ReasoningLow`, `ReasoningMedium`, `ReasoningHigh`, `ReasoningMinimal`, `ReasoningXHigh`, `ReasoningMax`, etc.) and helper `ReasoningTokens(n int) Reasoning` for numeric values.
- Do not add a separate public `Request.ReasoningBudget` or `Options.ReasoningBudget` field. Internally, parsing may produce a `ReasoningPolicy` struct containing kind/level/tokens, but the public input is one reasoning value.
- Add normalization helpers shared across CLI/service/config/provider paths: empty -> unset, auto -> auto, none/false/0 -> off, x-high -> xhigh, positive integer -> numeric token budget, preserve provider-specific level strings that are explicitly supported.
- Parser must accept scalar string and scalar integer inputs from YAML/JSON/config/catalog layers and normalize them to one canonical reasoning value.
- During migration, if public `Request.Reasoning` and legacy `Options.ThinkingLevel`/`ThinkingBudget`-style values are both present on the same call path, `Reasoning` wins. Cleanup bead agent-f78c3f25 removes or time-bounds the legacy aliases.
- Provider implementations must map named levels to numeric budgets when the underlying API/model accepts only numeric reasoning-token limits, using provider/catalog-specific maps when available and the portable defaults from the spec only as fallback for low/medium/high.
- Provider implementations must reject explicit over-limit numeric values with a clear error.
- `Run` must forward request reasoning into provider options.
- Unset request reasoning must still honor provider-level config, including a positive configured budget.
- Explicit `reasoning=off` or numeric 0 must suppress provider-level positive thinking/reasoning budgets.
- Explicit unsupported extended values or unsupported numeric values must fail clearly at the routing/adapter boundary instead of being silently downgraded to high or dropped.
- For native OpenAI-compatible providers, map reasoning to the appropriate provider-specific knob only after provider/catalog/config resolution. For current LM Studio-compatible behavior this means mapping to the nonstandard `thinking` budget field.
- Apply consistent behavior to both `Chat` and `ChatStream`; today only streaming injects the nonstandard `thinking` field.
- Keep provider capability detection as the final serialization gate; `omlx`, `openrouter`, `openai`, and `ollama` must never receive unsupported reasoning/thinking fields.
- Record release-note risk if any public embedding field changes name or type.

In-scope files:
- agent.go
- loop.go
- internal/reasoning/*
- internal/provider/openai/openai.go
- internal/provider/openai/openai_test.go or protocol support tests
- thinking_test.go, renamed or supplemented if appropriate

Out-of-scope:
- CLI flags.
- ServiceExecuteRequest plumbing.
- Model catalog default changes.
- Anthropic extended-thinking API support unless it already exists and can be wired trivially.
title: lib: make reasoning/off tri-state in Request and provider options

# DDx

DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
items with acceptance criteria), an agent service (harness dispatch
with profile-first routing), a persona system (bindable AI
personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.

## How this skill works

The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.

**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
guidance, not this overview alone.**

## Vocabulary

Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
exact definitions.

- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
  `ddx bead blocked`.
- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` (alias for
  `ddx agent execute-loop`); see `reference/work.md`.
- **Execute-bead** — the primitive: run one agent on one bead in an
  isolated worktree. `ddx agent execute-bead <id>`. The loop wraps
  this.
- **Execute-loop** — the queue-drain orchestrator; picks ready beads
  one at a time, calls execute-bead, closes on success, unclaims on
  failure. `ddx agent execute-loop` (or `ddx work`).
- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010); broader
  than execute-bead. Includes execution definitions, execution
  records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/executions/<id>/`.
- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
  below).
- **Harness** — the abstraction DDx uses to dispatch an agent
  (subprocess for claude/codex/gemini; embedded for the built-in
  `agent` harness). `ddx agent list`, `ddx agent capabilities`.
- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
  addendum to `ddx agent run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
  personas.
- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
- **Profile** — intent-based routing policy for `ddx agent run`:
  `cheap` (low-cost), `fast` (low-latency), `smart` (balanced
  default). `--profile cheap|fast|smart`.
- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
  `ddx install <name>`.
- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx's `ddx agent run`
  (especially with `--quorum`) is the portable primitive; how a
  harness wraps that in a subagent is harness business. This skill
  does not take a position on subagent orchestration.
- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
  `ddx upgrade`.
- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
  acceptance criteria. **Quorum review** (`ddx agent run
  --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>`) dispatches a review prompt
  across multiple harnesses and aggregates. See `reference/review.md`.
- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
  via `spec-id`.
- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
  at its governing artifact.

## Intent router

Before responding, read the matching file.

| User says / asks about | Read this file |
|---|---|
| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
| "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "run work", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
| "run an agent", "dispatch", harnesses, profiles, models, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |

If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
user which concept they mean rather than guessing.

## Top-level policy reminders

These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
reference file; do not violate them.

- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
  bead history and cannot be audited.
- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
- **Preserve execute-bead commit history.** Branches containing
  `ddx agent execute-bead` commits carry an audit trail. **Never
  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
  branches.
- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
  manual parallel work should too.
- **Profile-first agent dispatch.** Default to
  `ddx agent run --profile smart` (or `cheap`/`fast` for specific
  intents). Only override with `--harness`/`--model`/`--effort`
  when pinning for a controlled test or known provider bug.

## Links out

DDx has two distinct review concepts. They are **not interchangeable**.

| Concept | Command | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| **Bead review** | `ddx bead review <id>` | Grades a bead's implementation against its declared acceptance criteria. Per-AC verdict with evidence. |
| **Quorum review** | `ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` | Dispatches a review prompt across multiple harnesses and aggregates the verdicts. Used for high-stakes or adversarial checks. |

## Bead review

```bash
ddx bead review <bead-id>
```

Generates a review-ready prompt that includes:

- Bead title, description, acceptance criteria (verbatim).
- Governing artifact content (follows `spec-id`).
- The diff since the bead's base commit (execution evidence commits
  excluded — see the related bug ddx-39e27896 for exclusion
  pathspec).
- Review instructions: output contract, verdict taxonomy.

The reviewer (whoever receives the prompt — a human, a harness via
`ddx agent run`, or a quorum) produces a structured verdict:

```
## Bead review: ddx-<id>

### Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK

### Per-criterion

1. [AC text]
   Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK
   Evidence: <file:line, test output, diff hunk, or explicit "not verifiable">

2. [AC text]
   ...

### Summary
<1-3 sentences of overall context>

### Findings
<detailed issues, if any; omit when verdict is APPROVE with no findings>
```

The review output can be stored as bead evidence:

```bash
ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body <path-to-review.md>
```

## Quorum review

```bash
ddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini \
  --prompt review-prompt.md
```

`--quorum=<policy>` values:

- `majority` — at least ⌈N/2⌉ harnesses must agree on the verdict.
- `unanimous` — all harnesses must agree.
- `any` — pass if any harness APPROVES (rarely useful; adversarial
  checks should require stronger consensus).

Use quorum when:

- The change is high-stakes (migrations, security surface, API
  contracts, auth).
- The review surface is controversial and one harness's opinion
  isn't enough.
- You want adversarial pressure against a single-model blindspot
  (different model families have different failure modes).

The aggregated output shows each harness's verdict side-by-side plus
the aggregate decision. Disagreements are surfaced, not hidden.

## Required evidence format

Both review kinds share an evidence contract: **no verdict without
evidence.**

- "APPROVE" with no `Evidence:` lines → reject as malformed.
- "REQUEST_CHANGES" without pointing at the change needed → reject.
- "BLOCK" without a reason → reject (see the related bug
  ddx-39e27896 about `review-malfunction` retries).

Evidence takes one of these forms:

1. **File:line reference**: `cli/internal/bead/store.go:142`
2. **Test output**: `FAIL: TestBeadClose_UnclaimsOnNonSuccess (0.01s)`
3. **Diff hunk**: a short quote from the diff showing the offending
   or approved change.
4. **Explicit "not verifiable"**: the AC is written in a way that
   cannot be mechanically checked; flag this as a bead-authoring
   issue rather than silently approving.

## Anti-patterns

- **Hallucinated approval.** "Looks good to me!" with no per-AC
  grading. The review is worthless.
- **Reviewing the prompt instead of the diff.** The reviewer evaluated
  "what you intended" not "what you actually changed." Verdict must
  reference the diff or the resulting code.
- **Verdict without evidence.** Every verdict must have at least one
  evidence line; APPROVE is no exception (evidence can be "AC met:
  `go test ./foo/... passes` output attached").
- **Silencing disagreement in quorum.** If two harnesses disagree,
  don't collapse to a single "decision." Surface the disagreement
  in the output and let the human decide.
- **Quorum for routine work.** Single-harness bead review is fine
  for most beads. Reserve quorum for the cases that justify the
  cost.

## Subagents: not this skill's business

Some harnesses can run a quorum in forked/isolated subagent
contexts; each harness implements this differently. This skill
doesn't specify how isolation is achieved —
`ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` is the
portable invocation, and the harness decides whether to fork,
spawn, or run inline.

## CLI reference

```bash
# Bead review (single-reviewer AC grading)
ddx bead review <id>                           # generate review prompt
ddx bead review <id> --execute --harness claude  # dispatch and grade
ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body r.md  # store review

# Quorum review (multi-harness)
ddx agent run --quorum=majority \
  --harnesses=claude,codex \
  --prompt review.md

ddx agent run --quorum=unanimous \
  --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini \
  --prompt review.md
```

Full flag list: `ddx bead review --help`, `ddx agent run --help`.

The error message includes the model name and the current threshold. Setting the timeout to 0 disables the check. | `TestConsumeStream_ReasoningStall`, `TestConsumeStream_ReasoningStallErrorMessage` |

HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:139:{"acceptance":"1. Provider (or equivalent resolved-config struct) exposes a DetectedFlavor() accessor returning a string matching one of the known flavor values (or empty when indeterminate).\n\n2. The accessor reads from the cached probe result — no HTTP call on every invocation.\n\n3. If ProviderConfig.Flavor was set explicitly, DetectedFlavor() returns that value unchanged (caller-set wins over probe).\n\n4. Unit test: probe returns 'omlx' signal -\u003e Provider.DetectedFlavor() returns 'omlx'. ProviderConfig.Flavor='lmstudio' + probe says 'omlx' -\u003e DetectedFlavor() returns 'lmstudio'.\n\n5. CHANGELOG.md entry describing the new accessor.","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T01:40:17Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1635356","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:22:17.516353249Z","description":"## Context\n\nv0.3.11 introduced ProviderConfig.Flavor (lmstudio | omlx | openrouter | ollama) with probe-based autodetection when the field is absent. The probe hits /v1/models/status and /api/v0/models to figure out which flavor the endpoint speaks.\n\nDownstream consumers (DDx at DocumentDrivenDX/ddx, specifically the autorouting plan under ddx-2d974641 and the capability-gating bead ddx-4817edfd) need to know which flavor the library decided on for each configured provider — so their own routing/scoring/diagnostics can say 'this provider is omlx-flavor, so tool schemas go through auto-translation' without repeating the probe.\n\nToday this is not exposed via the public API. Downstream has two choices:\n1. Re-run the same probe independently. Wastes the library's work and can produce different answers if servers flap.\n2. Hard-code assumptions based on port numbers or URL patterns. Fragile — exactly the heuristic v0.3.11 replaced.\n\n## Ask\n\nExpose a getter on the resolved Provider (post-Load, post-detect) that returns the effective flavor:\n\n    // DetectedFlavor returns the flavor the runtime is using for this provider.\n    // If ProviderConfig.Flavor was set explicitly, returns that value. Otherwise\n    // returns the auto-detected flavor from the startup probe. Empty string when\n    // the probe was inconclusive or the provider is not a supported flavor-family.\n    func (p *Provider) DetectedFlavor() string\n\nCache the probe result so DetectedFlavor is a cheap lookup, not a fresh HTTP call.\n\n## Use case\n\nDDx uses this to:\n- Tag routing events with the effective flavor so debugging is self-contained in execution evidence.\n- Feed capability gating (supports_tools, supports_structured_output) off the flavor in scoring.\n- Generate human-readable 'ddx agent check --verbose' output showing each provider's flavor.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Exposing the RAW probe results (HTTP responses). Only the distilled flavor.\n- Making the flavor mutable at runtime. Providers are loaded + detected once per process.","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-18T01:40:17.954331827Z","id":"agent-92f0f324","issue_type":"task","labels":["agent","kind:enhancement","area:provider","area:api"],"notes":"Fresh-eyes review 2026-04-18: Provider already has a flavor-adjacent field — providerSystem (URL-heuristic from openAIIdentity, stored at New() time). It's used on 4 hot paths: AttemptMetadata.ProviderSystem, openRouterCostAttribution, ProviderIdentity(), and streaming attempt metadata. Those fire every response and cannot afford a network probe. DetectedFlavor() must layer on top: return explicit Config.Flavor if set, otherwise cached probe result via sync.Once, otherwise fall back to providerSystem. Do NOT migrate the 4 hot callsites off providerSystem in this bead — that's a telemetry contract change (CONTRACT-001) and scope creep. Docstring must explicitly document the two-path layering: DetectedFlavor() for pre-dispatch gating (probe-confirmed); ProviderIdentity() for per-response telemetry (eager, non-blocking).","owner":"erik","priority":2,"status":"closed","title":"expose Provider.DetectedFlavor() so downstream tools can introspect probe result without re-probing","updated_at":"2026-04-18T01:57:30.690987048Z"}

The module becomes the one stop shop for optimally routed one-shot noninteractive agentic prompts. Two roles: (1) direct first-class agent over native providers (bragi, vidar, openrouter, anthropic), designed to win at batch noninteractive; (2) wrapper around subprocess agents (claude, codex, opencode, pi, gemini) for convenience and cost management.\n\nDDx becomes a thin Service consumer with no agent-internal imports. The boundary is one Go interface (DdxAgent, 7 methods) defined in CONTRACT-001. Internal packages move under internal/ — compiler-enforced.\n\nReplaces the spec entanglement that produced the six well-documented routing smells (parallel routing engines, --provider bypass, fragmented fuzzy match, profile-vs-failover split, capability gating asymmetry, health/quota fragmentation). Plan: /tmp/ddx-agent-service-interface-plan.md (in DDx repo for reference).\n\nCross-tracker: paired with DDx epic for the consumer-side migration. Beads in this epic precede each corresponding DDx bead.","id":"agent-a770e781","issue_type":"epic","labels":["phase:design","area:contract","area:lib","kind:architecture"],"priority":0,"status":"open","title":"Epic: ddx-agent owns LLM execution + routing — single Service contract, harnesses ported, internal/-enforced boundary","updated_at":"2026-04-18T21:21:28.726590048Z"}

HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:280:{"acceptance":"Streaming timing uses real delta arrival timestamps rather than subtracting cumulative callback time; a regression test with callback sleep plus inter-delta provider delay verifies ddx.timing.generation_ms still includes the provider delay and EventLLMDelta timestamps remain monotonic.","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T01:44:09Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3948379","closing_commit_sha":"863eecb98d4dcca506fdb42a6a67864410c38442","created_at":"2026-04-10T01:42:19.102557411Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: stream_consume.go:44\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: consumeStream now computes each delta timestamp as time.Now().Add(-callbackDelay) and grows callbackDelay by the duration of prior EventLLMDelta callbacks. When a later streamed delta becomes ready while an earlier callback is still running, this subtracts both observer latency and real provider generation time that elapsed during that callback. In the shipped providers, ChatStream uses a single-slot channel, so later deltas can queue behind the consumer and then be back-dated when read. That makes ddx.timing.generation_ms and delta event timestamps too small or even flat across distinct arrivals, which violates CONTRACT-001's requirement that timing reflect the streamed output window rather than an adjusted observer schedule.\nSuggested fix: Capture an arrival timestamp at the producer or carry it on StreamDelta so consumeStream can use actual delta arrival times. At minimum, add a regression test that combines callback sleep with real delayBetween timing and verifies generation_ms still includes the provider delay that elapsed while the callback was running.","id":"hx-1fcb3c84","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"stream_consume.go","status":"closed","title":"correctness: callback-delay compensation undercounts real stream generation","updated_at":"2026-04-10T01:45:22.728662476Z"}
HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:285:{"acceptance":"A session whose chat spans all have known cost preserves a root invoke_agent ddx.cost.amount equal to the sum of child costs even when provenance differs across turns; the root span only emits ddx.cost.source=unknown when at least one contributing chat span has unknown cost; automated tests cover mixed provider-reported/configured cost rollups.","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T04:17:15Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"517258","closing_commit_sha":"c083df5f46fe37557b8feedf66af32013a0a9e35","created_at":"2026-04-10T04:02:07.463686182Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: loop.go:289\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new root-span cost rollup logic marks the invoke_agent span as unknown whenever known child chat spans disagree on ddx.cost.source, currency, or pricing_ref. That drops a valid total for mixed but fully known sessions, which violates CONTRACT-001's rule that the root cost only becomes unknown when a contributing chat span has unknown cost. Reproduced against the current code by running a two-turn session with a provider-reported first turn and an exact configured-cost second turn: result.CostUSD=0.03 while the root span still emits ddx.cost.source=unknown.\nSuggested fix: Roll up root ddx.cost.amount whenever every contributing chat span has known cost, and only force ddx.cost.source=unknown when a contributing span is actually unknown. If metadata such as pricing_ref or source cannot be represented cleanly at the root, omit or summarize those fields rather than discarding the known total. Add a regression test covering mixed known-cost provenance across turns.","id":"hx-264e7ea0","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"loop.go","status":"closed","title":"correctness: preserve known root cost rollups across mixed known cost sources","updated_at":"2026-04-10T04:18:57.184174548Z"}

HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:314:{"acceptance":"If attempt cost provenance is unknown, llm.response and session.end do not emit a positive cost_usd derived from DefaultPricing; known provider-reported or configured costs still propagate; regression tests cover known-cost and unknown-cost cases.","claimed-at":"2026-04-09T22:06:44Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1912325","closing_commit_sha":"770742fb96448ee3e5ea05811884d95f9e90c7e1","created_at":"2026-04-09T21:51:41.640860953Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit de8c772.\nFile: loop.go:219, loop.go:241, provider/openai/openai.go:83, provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:90, pricing.go:12\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: This change introduces structured cost provenance on resp.Attempt.Cost and explicitly marks both providers as source=unknown, but the runtime still unconditionally computes cost_usd from DefaultPricing and emits it on every successful llm.response. That produces internally contradictory telemetry: the same event can say the attempt cost source is unknown while also publishing a numeric USD value from a static fallback table. CONTRACT-001 explicitly forbids generic stale pricing tables as a fallback and requires unknown cost to remain unknown when no reported or configured cost exists.\nSuggested fix: Stop deriving per-attempt and session cost from DefaultPricing once attempt-level cost provenance exists. Use provider-reported or configured runtime pricing only when available, otherwise omit numeric cost and keep the rollup unknown. Add tests that assert unknown-cost attempts do not emit positive cost_usd values.","id":"hx-5521ea3d","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"loop.go","status":"closed","title":"correctness: llm.response cost_usd conflicts with unknown attempt cost provenance","updated_at":"2026-04-09T22:08:43.726212836Z"}

HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:336:{"acceptance":"CONTRACT-001 explicitly defines how the root invoke_agent span represents known session totals when child chat spans have mixed known provenance; the contract no longer requires an impossible or ambiguous ddx.cost.source/pricing_ref combination at the root; conformance tests or checklist coverage include a mixed provider-reported/configured session example.","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T04:23:38Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"547576","closing_commit_sha":"c083df5f46fe37557b8feedf66af32013a0a9e35","created_at":"2026-04-10T04:15:39.179486428Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-001-otel-telemetry-capture.md:221\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: CONTRACT-001 says the root invoke_agent span should emit a session cost rollup whenever every contributing chat span has known cost, and only become unknown when any child cost is unknown. But the contract never defines what ddx.cost.source, ddx.cost.currency, and ddx.cost.pricing_ref should look like when all child chat costs are known yet their provenance differs across turns, such as one provider-reported turn plus one configured-cost turn. That makes the required root shape ambiguous: ddx.cost.source is required when amount is known, but the allowed enum only models single-attempt provenance. The current implementation fell into this gap and downgraded a known total to unknown when provenance metadata differed.\nSuggested fix: Amend the root-span cost-rollup section to specify a conformant representation for mixed known-cost provenance. Either add an explicit aggregate/root rollup source shape, or state that the root may still emit ddx.cost.amount and currency while omitting or summarizing provenance fields that cannot be represented cleanly. Add a contract-focused regression test or checklist entry covering mixed provider-reported/configured sessions.","id":"hx-7853958b","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-001-otel-telemetry-capture.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: define mixed-provenance root cost rollup semantics","updated_at":"2026-04-10T04:24:48.231802016Z"}

HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:345:{"acceptance":"Streaming chat spans measure ddx.timing.first_token_ms from the same timestamp used to start the chat span; a test with a blocking EventLLMRequest callback and a streaming provider confirms first_token_ms includes callback delay before ChatStream begins.","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T01:18:48Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3894095","closing_commit_sha":"4df0f7e92c3f685d9843714ab7b10e5a4a04741e","created_at":"2026-04-10T01:17:28.821747551Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: loop.go:175\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: Commit 4df0f7e moved `streamStart` earlier inside `consumeStream`, but `ddx.timing.first_token_ms` is still measured after the chat span starts. `Run` starts the `chat` span at loop.go:175, then emits `EventLLMRequest` before calling `consumeStream`; because callbacks are user-supplied and can block, the exported attribute can still under-report first-token latency by the callback time and therefore violate CONTRACT-001's requirement that the value measure from span start to first streamed token.\nSuggested fix: Capture the chat span/request start timestamp in `Run` and thread it into `consumeStream` so `Attempt.Timing.FirstToken` is derived from the same timestamp that anchors the `chat` span. Add an end-to-end test where the request callback sleeps before streaming begins and assert the chat span's `ddx.timing.first_token_ms` includes that delay.","id":"hx-8bf6ee3a","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","area:lib"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"loop.go","status":"closed","title":"correctness: first_token_ms still excludes pre-stream callback latency","updated_at":"2026-04-10T01:19:57.86077717Z"}

HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:376:{"acceptance":"go test ./... passes in a clean worktree at HEAD; TestRun_CONTRACT001MixedKnownCostProvenance observes ddx.cost.amount on the root invoke_agent span for mixed known-cost sessions; root mixed-provenance rollups omit unstable provenance metadata instead of downgrading the whole session cost to unknown.","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T04:33:52Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"576954","closing_commit_sha":"421f59e695319df41a2ec9016b13f652cb98ed6e","created_at":"2026-04-10T04:32:06.30077824Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: loop.go:696\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: high\nDescription: Commit 5f14aaa updates CONTRACT-001 and adds TestRun_CONTRACT001MixedKnownCostProvenance, but the runtime still emits root `invoke_agent` cost as unknown when child chat spans have mixed known provenance. In a clean worktree at commit 5f14aaa, `go test ./...` fails because telemetry_contract_test.go expects the root span to carry ddx.cost.amount=0.03, while a standalone reproduction shows the ended root span still exports only ddx.cost.source=unknown. That leaves the implementation out of sync with the amended contract and keeps the original telemetry bug unfixed.\nSuggested fix: Update the root-session cost rollup logic in loop.go so mixed known provenance preserves ddx.cost.amount while omitting only the unstable provenance fields (ddx.cost.source, ddx.cost.currency, ddx.cost.pricing_ref) as CONTRACT-001 now requires. Keep the new conformance test green with go test ./... in a clean worktree.","id":"hx-cacc76f6","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"loop.go","status":"closed","title":"correctness: mixed-provenance root cost rollup still exports unknown","updated_at":"2026-04-10T04:34:13.786934256Z"}
HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:377:{"acceptance":"When any contributing attempt has unknown cost provenance, session.end omits cost_usd instead of emitting -1; replay/session parsing distinguishes omitted cost from zero-cost local cost; regression tests cover both known-cost and unknown-cost session rollups.","claimed-at":"2026-04-09T22:13:53Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1968732","closing_commit_sha":"0fd0aca909b39909db31ad74954f81e271c80174","created_at":"2026-04-09T22:12:30.075052671Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: loop.go:360, loop_test.go:506, session/event.go:52, session/replay.go:108\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The last-commit fix removed DefaultPricing fallback from per-attempt telemetry, but the session rollup still serializes a numeric cost on every session.end event by copying result.CostUSD unconditionally. When any contributing attempt has unknown provenance, result.CostUSD becomes -1, so session.end now emits `cost_usd: -1` instead of omitting the amount. That still violates CONTRACT-001 and SD-001, which require unknown rollup cost amounts to be omitted. The new regression test in loop_test.go also codifies unconditional session.end cost emission, so a contract-compliant fix would currently fail tests. Downstream session consumers compound the issue because SessionEndData uses a plain float64 and Replay treats zero as `$0 (local)`, so omitted cost cannot be represented distinctly from free local execution.\nSuggested fix: Make session.end omit cost_usd when the run cost is unknown, update session event/replay types to distinguish omitted cost from zero-cost local runs, and replace the current session-end assertion with regression coverage for both known-cost and unknown-cost sessions.","id":"hx-cce583e1","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"loop.go","status":"closed","title":"correctness: session.end still emits unknown cost as numeric sentinel","updated_at":"2026-04-09T22:14:59.058088662Z"}
HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:382:{"acceptance":"A session whose chat spans all have known cost preserves a root invoke_agent ddx.cost.amount equal to the sum of child costs even when provenance differs across turns; the root span only emits ddx.cost.source=unknown when at least one contributing chat span has unknown cost; automated tests cover mixed provider-reported/configured cost rollups.","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T04:41:54Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"615913","closing_commit_sha":"421f59e695319df41a2ec9016b13f652cb98ed6e","created_at":"2026-04-10T04:22:39.474002339Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: loop.go:289\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The last commit's root-span cost rollup logic marks the invoke_agent span as unknown whenever known child chat spans disagree on ddx.cost.source, currency, or pricing_ref. That discards a valid session total even though every contributing chat span has known cost, which conflicts with FEAT-005 requirement 22, ADR-001's known-vs-unknown cost policy, and CONTRACT-001's rule that the root rollup only becomes unknown when a contributing chat span has unknown cost. In the committed code, a two-turn session with one provider-reported attempt and one exact configured-cost attempt yields result.CostUSD=0.03 while applySessionCostAttributes still emits root ddx.cost.source=unknown because sessionCostStable became false.\nSuggested fix: Preserve root ddx.cost.amount whenever every contributing chat span has known cost, and only force ddx.cost.source=unknown when a contributing span is actually unknown. If source/currency/pricing_ref cannot be represented cleanly for mixed provenance, omit or summarize those metadata fields instead of discarding the known total. Add regression coverage for mixed provider-reported plus configured-cost sessions.","id":"hx-d2c7ec7e","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"notes":"Superseded by commit `421f59e695319df41a2ec9016b13f652cb98ed6e` (`hx-cacc76f6 preserve mixed-provenance root cost amount`).\n\nThe root invoke_agent span now preserves `ddx.cost.amount` for mixed known-cost sessions and the regression coverage for mixed provider-reported plus configured-cost rollups is in `loop_test.go`.","owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"loop.go","status":"closed","title":"correctness: mixed known-cost root rollups must stay known","updated_at":"2026-04-10T04:42:13.284855944Z"}
HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:383:{"acceptance":"Streaming chat spans measure ddx.timing.first_token_ms from before ChatStream is invoked; a test with a provider whose ChatStream blocks before yielding output includes that blocking time in FirstToken.","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T01:13:30Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3833023","closing_commit_sha":"4df0f7e92c3f685d9843714ab7b10e5a4a04741e","created_at":"2026-04-10T00:59:51.999478905Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: stream_consume.go:22\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: consumeStream records streamStart only after sp.ChatStream returns. Both provider/openai and provider/anthropic call the SDK's NewStreaming method before returning, and those SDK methods synchronously execute the HTTP request. That means ddx.timing.first_token_ms underreports time-to-first-token by excluding request setup and connection latency, so the emitted timing no longer matches CONTRACT-001's requirement to measure from request/span start.\nSuggested fix: Capture the stream start timestamp before invoking sp.ChatStream, or thread the chat-span/request start timestamp into consumeStream and compute FirstToken from that value.","id":"hx-d6785a0a","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"stream_consume.go","status":"closed","title":"correctness: TTFT starts after ChatStream setup","updated_at":"2026-04-10T01:14:10.450228871Z"}

HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:405:{"acceptance":"Streaming timing uses pre-callback delta arrival timestamps for ddx.timing.first_token_ms and ddx.timing.generation_ms; a test with a blocking EventLLMDelta callback confirms the timing values do not grow by callback sleep time.","claimed-at":"2026-04-10T01:36:44Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"3936062","closing_commit_sha":"863eecb98d4dcca506fdb42a6a67864410c38442","created_at":"2026-04-10T01:24:20.154769542Z","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: stream_consume.go:41\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new anchor fixes pre-stream request callback latency, but consumeStream still timestamps the first output only after emitting EventLLMDelta. Because emitCallback is user-supplied and can block, a slow llm.delta callback will still inflate ddx.timing.first_token_ms and ddx.timing.generation_ms, which violates CONTRACT-001s requirement that timing reflect span-to-token and generation windows rather than observer latency.\nSuggested fix: Capture the arrival timestamp for each delta before invoking the callback, use that pre-callback timestamp for the first output boundary and generation window math, and add a regression test where an EventLLMDelta callback sleeps while the first streamed delta arrives.","id":"hx-fd626e74","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-001-otel-telemetry-capture.md","status":"closed","title":"correctness: first_token_ms still counts blocking llm.delta callbacks","updated_at":"2026-04-10T01:38:50.580711052Z"}

{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_22","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc "git show HEAD:docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md | nl -ba | sed -n '1,320p'"","aggregated_output":"     1	# CONTRACT-003: DdxAgent Service Interface
     2	
     3	**Status:** Draft
     4	**Owner:** DDX Agent maintainers
     5	**Replaces:** CONTRACT-002-ddx-harness-interface (deleted; entanglement-era contract)
     6	
     7	## Purpose
     8	
     9	This contract defines the **entire public Go surface** of the `ddx-agent` module.
    10	Anything not reachable through the `DdxAgent` interface — or through the input/output
    11	struct types referenced by its methods — is internal and may change without notice.
    12	
    13	Consumers (DDx CLI, future HELIX/Dun integrations, the standalone `ddx-agent`
    14	binary, anything else) interact only through this surface. **They do not import
    15	agent internal packages.** When new behavior is needed, consumers file an issue or
    16	PR against this contract; agent maintainers decide whether the surface grows.
    17	
    18	## Module value proposition
    19	
    20	`ddx-agent` is the one stop shop for optimally routed one-shot noninteractive
    21	agentic prompts. Two roles:
    22	
    23	1. **Direct first-class agent** over native model providers (LM Studio, OpenRouter,
    24	   Anthropic, etc.). Designed to be the high-performance choice for batch
    25	   noninteractive tasks.
    26	2. **Wrapper around other agents** — subprocess harnesses (claude, codex,
    27	   opencode, pi, gemini) — used when their interactive features, vendor billing,
    28	   specific capabilities, or comparison/fallback routing wants them in the
    29	   candidate pool.
    30	
    31	A single internal routing engine ranks `(harness, provider?, model)` candidates
    32	uniformly. Consumers see one surface; the internals decide how to dispatch.
    33	
    34	## Interface
    35	
    36	```go
    37	package agentlib
    38	
    39	import (
    40	    "context"
    41	    "io"
    42	    "time"
    43	    "encoding/json"
    44	)
    45	
    46	// DdxAgent is the entire public Go surface of the ddx-agent module.
    47	type DdxAgent interface {
    48	    // Execute runs an agent task in-process; emits Events on the channel until
    49	    // the task terminates (channel closes). The final event (type=final) carries
    50	    // status, usage, cost, session log path, optional message history, and
    51	    // routing_actual (the resolved fallback chain that fired).
    52	    Execute(ctx context.Context, req ExecuteRequest) (<-chan Event, error)
    53	
    54	    // TailSessionLog streams events from a previously-started or in-progress
    55	    // session by ID. Used by clients (DDx workers, UIs) to subscribe to a run
    56	    // started elsewhere — e.g., a server-managed worker that the CLI wants to
    57	    // follow. Multi-subscriber-safe.
    58	    TailSessionLog(ctx context.Context, sessionID string) (<-chan Event, error)
    59	
    60	    // ListHarnesses returns metadata for every registered harness (native and
    61	    // subprocess). HarnessInfo includes install state, supported permission
    62	    // levels, supported reasoning values, and live quota when applicable.
    63	    ListHarnesses(ctx context.Context) ([]HarnessInfo, error)
    64	
    65	    // ListProviders returns providers known to the native-agent harness with
    66	    // live status, configured-default markers, and cooldown state.
    67	    ListProviders(ctx context.Context) ([]ProviderInfo, error)
    68	
    69	    // ListModels returns models matching the filter, with full metadata
    70	    // (cost, perf signals, capabilities, context length, ranking).
    71	    ListModels(ctx context.Context, filter ModelFilter) ([]ModelInfo, error)
    72	
    73	    // HealthCheck triggers a fresh probe and updates internal state.
    74	    // Target.Type is "harness" or "provider".
    75	    HealthCheck(ctx context.Context, target HealthTarget) error
    76	
    77	    // ResolveRoute resolves a single under-specified request to a concrete
    78	    // (Harness, Provider, Model). The returned RouteDecision can be passed
    79	    // back to Execute via ExecuteRequest.PreResolved to skip re-resolution
    80	    // (used by dry-run-then-execute flows).
    81	    ResolveRoute(ctx context.Context, req RouteRequest) (*RouteDecision, error)
    82	
    83	    // RouteStatus returns global routing state across all routes: cooldowns,
    84	    // recent decisions, observation-derived per-(provider,model) latency.
    85	    // Distinct from per-request ResolveRoute — this is the read-only operator
    86	    // dashboard view.
    87	    RouteStatus(ctx context.Context) (*RouteStatusReport, error)
    88	}
    89	
    90	// New constructs a DdxAgent. Options is intentionally minimal.
    91	func New(opts Options) (DdxAgent, error)
    92	```
    93	
    94	**Eight methods total.** `Execute` is the primary verb; `TailSessionLog`,
    95	`ListHarnesses`, `ListProviders`, `ListModels`, `HealthCheck`, `ResolveRoute`,
    96	`RouteStatus` are the supporting surface. (Counted as "7 + New" in earlier
    97	drafts; interface has 8 methods.)
    98	
    99	## Public types
   100	
   101	```go
   102	type Options struct {
   103	    ConfigPath string    // optional override; default $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/ddx-agent/config.yaml
   104	    Logger     io.Writer // optional; agent writes structured session logs internally regardless
   105	
   106	    // Test-only injection seams. Each MUST be nil in production builds —
   107	    // enforced by build tag `//go:build testseam`. Forming an Options with
   108	    // any of these set in a non-test build is a compile error. Four seams
   109	    // exist because consumers today inject at four different layers.
   110	    FakeProvider            *FakeProvider
   111	    PromptAssertionHook     PromptAssertionHook
   112	    CompactionAssertionHook CompactionAssertionHook
   113	    ToolWiringHook          ToolWiringHook
   114	}
   115	
   116	// Reasoning is the single public model-reasoning control. It is one scalar:
   117	// named values such as auto/off/low/medium/high/minimal/xhigh/max, or numeric
   118	// strings produced by ReasoningTokens for explicit token budgets.
   119	//
   120	// The root package may re-export this type, constants, and helper from a shared
   121	// leaf package such as internal/reasoning. Internal packages such as
   122	// internal/modelcatalog import the leaf package, not root agent, to avoid
   123	// root-agent/internal-modelcatalog import cycles.
   124	type Reasoning string
   125	
   126	const (
   127	    ReasoningAuto   Reasoning = "auto"
   128	    ReasoningOff    Reasoning = "off"
   129	    ReasoningLow    Reasoning = "low"
   130	    ReasoningMedium Reasoning = "medium"
   131	    ReasoningHigh   Reasoning = "high"
   132	    ReasoningMinimal Reasoning = "minimal" // accepted only when advertised
   133	    ReasoningXHigh  Reasoning = "xhigh"    // normalizes x-high
   134	    ReasoningMax    Reasoning = "max"      // requires known model/provider max
   135	)

   139	type ExecuteRequest struct {
   140	    Prompt       string  // required
   141	    SystemPrompt string  // optional; agent supplies a sane default if empty
   142	    Model        string  // optional; resolved via ResolveRoute if empty
   143	    Provider     string  // optional preference (soft); empty = router decides
   144	    Harness      string  // optional preference (hard); empty = router decides
   145	    ModelRef     string  // optional alias from the catalog: cheap/standard/smart/<custom>
   146	    Temperature  float32 // model sampling temperature; 0 = deterministic
   147	    Seed         int64   // sampling seed; 0 = unset/provider chooses
   148	    Reasoning    Reasoning // optional; auto|off|low|medium|high|minimal|xhigh|max|<tokens>
   149	    Permissions  string  // "safe" | "supervised" | "unrestricted"; default "safe"
   150	    WorkDir      string  // required when the chosen harness uses tools
   151	
   152	    // PreResolved bypasses ResolveRoute when the caller already has a decision
   153	    // (e.g., from a prior ResolveRoute call). When non-nil, agent uses these
   154	    // values verbatim and does not re-route. Provider/Model/Harness fields
   155	    // above are ignored in this mode.
   156	    PreResolved *RouteDecision
   157	
   158	    // Three independent timeout knobs:
   159	    //   Timeout         — wall-clock cap; the request fails after this duration
   160	    //                     regardless of activity. 0 = no cap.
   161	    //   IdleTimeout     — streaming-quiet cap; the request fails after this
   162	    //                     duration of no events from the model. 0 = use harness
   163	    //                     default (typically 60s).
   164	    //   ProviderTimeout — per-HTTP-request cap to the provider; longer requests
   165	    //                     are retried per the harness's failover rules. 0 = use
   166	    //                     provider default.
   167	    Timeout         time.Duration
   168	    IdleTimeout     time.Duration
   169	    ProviderTimeout time.Duration
   170	
   171	    // Optional stall policy. When non-nil, agent enforces and ends execution
   172	    // with Status="stalled" if any limit hits. The agent also derives an
   173	    // implicit MaxIterations ceiling from StallPolicy (typically 2× the
   174	    // ReadOnly limit) — caller does not configure MaxIterations directly.
   175	    StallPolicy *StallPolicy
   176	
   177	    // SessionLogDir overrides the default session-log directory for this
   178	    // request. Used by execute-bead to direct logs into a per-bundle evidence
   179	    // directory. Empty = use Options.ConfigPath-derived default.
   180	    SessionLogDir string
   181	
   182	    // Metadata is bidirectional: echoed back in every Event via Event.Metadata,
   183	    // AND stamped onto every line written to the session log (e.g., bead_id,
   184	    // attempt_id) so external log consumers can correlate.
   185	    Metadata map[string]string
   186	}

   302	// loadServiceConfig, when non-nil, is called by New to load a ServiceConfig
   303	// from a directory path when opts.ServiceConfig is nil. It is registered by
   304	// the config package via init() to break the import cycle (config imports root).
   305	var loadServiceConfig func(dir string) (ServiceConfig, error)
   306	
   307	// RegisterConfigLoader is called by the config package's init() to install the
   308	// config-loading function. Do not call this from application code.
   309	func RegisterConfigLoader(fn func(dir string) (ServiceConfig, error)) {
   310		loadServiceConfig = fn
   311	}
   312	
   313	// New constructs a DdxAgent. When opts.ServiceConfig is nil, New attempts to
   314	// load configuration automatically:
   315	//  1. If opts.ConfigPath is non-empty, load from filepath.Dir(opts.ConfigPath).
   316	//  2. Otherwise, load from the default global config location.
   317	//
   318	// Automatic loading requires the config package to be imported somewhere in the
   319	// binary (it registers the loader via init). If the config package is not
   320	// imported and ServiceConfig is nil, the service starts without provider config
   321	// (ListProviders/HealthCheck will return errors until config is injected).
   322	func New(opts ServiceOptions) (DdxAgent, error) {
   323		if opts.ServiceConfig == nil && loadServiceConfig != nil && shouldAutoLoadServiceConfig(opts) {
   324			dir := ""
   325			if opts.ConfigPath != "" {
   326				dir = filepath.Dir(opts.ConfigPath)
   327			}
   328			sc, err := loadServiceConfig(dir)
   329			if err != nil {
   330				return nil, fmt.Errorf("agent.New: load config: %w", err)
   331			}
   332			opts.ServiceConfig = sc
   333		}
   334		return &service{
   335			opts:     opts,
   336			registry: harnesses.NewRegistry(),
   337			hub:      newSessionHub(),
   338		}, nil
   339	}

   341	// harnessType returns "native" for HTTP/embedded harnesses, "subprocess" for CLI-invoked ones.
   342	func harnessType(cfg harnesses.HarnessConfig) string {
   343		if cfg.IsHTTPProvider || cfg.IsLocal {
   344			return "native"
   345		}
   346		return "subprocess"
   347	}
   348	
   349	// supportedPermissions extracts the permission levels from PermissionArgs keys,
   350	// returning them in canonical order.
   351	func supportedPermissions(cfg harnesses.HarnessConfig) []string {
   352		if len(cfg.PermissionArgs) == 0 {
   353			return nil
   354		}
   355		order := []string{"safe", "supervised", "unrestricted"}
   356		var out []string
   357		for _, level := range order {
   358			if _, ok := cfg.PermissionArgs[level]; ok {
   359				out = append(out, level)
   360			}
   361		}
   362		return out
   363	}
   364	
   365	func supportedReasoning(cfg harnesses.HarnessConfig) []string {
   366		return append([]string(nil), cfg.ReasoningLevels...)
   367	}
