CONSTANTS

const (
	ReasoningAuto    = reasoning.ReasoningAuto
	ReasoningOff     = reasoning.ReasoningOff
	ReasoningLow     = reasoning.ReasoningLow
	ReasoningMedium  = reasoning.ReasoningMedium
	ReasoningHigh    = reasoning.ReasoningHigh
	ReasoningMinimal = reasoning.ReasoningMinimal
	ReasoningXHigh   = reasoning.ReasoningXHigh
	ReasoningMax     = reasoning.ReasoningMax
)

FUNCTIONS

func RegisterConfigLoader(fn func(dir string) (ServiceConfig, error))
    RegisterConfigLoader is called by the config package's init() to install the
    config-loading function. Do not call this from application code.


TYPES

type CooldownState struct {
	Reason    string    // "consecutive_failures" | "manual" | etc.
	Until     time.Time // when the cooldown expires
	FailCount int       // number of consecutive failures that triggered the cooldown
	LastError string    // last recorded error message, if available
}
    CooldownState describes an active routing cooldown for a provider.

type CostInfo struct {
	InputPerMTok  float64 // USD per 1M input tokens; 0 = unknown/free
	OutputPerMTok float64 // USD per 1M output tokens; 0 = unknown/free
}
    CostInfo holds per-token cost metadata for a model.

type DdxAgent interface {
	Execute(ctx context.Context, req ServiceExecuteRequest) (<-chan ServiceEvent, error)
	TailSessionLog(ctx context.Context, sessionID string) (<-chan ServiceEvent, error)
	ListHarnesses(ctx context.Context) ([]HarnessInfo, error)
	ListProviders(ctx context.Context) ([]ProviderInfo, error)
	ListModels(ctx context.Context, filter ModelFilter) ([]ModelInfo, error)
	HealthCheck(ctx context.Context, target HealthTarget) error
	ResolveRoute(ctx context.Context, req RouteRequest) (*RouteDecision, error)
	RouteStatus(ctx context.Context) (*RouteStatusReport, error)
}
    DdxAgent is the entire public Go surface of the ddx-agent module. See
    CONTRACT-003 for the full specification.

func New(opts ServiceOptions) (DdxAgent, error)
    New constructs a DdxAgent. When opts.ServiceConfig is nil, New attempts to
    load configuration automatically:
     1. If opts.ConfigPath is non-empty, load from
        filepath.Dir(opts.ConfigPath).
     2. Otherwise, load from the default global config location.

    Automatic loading requires the config package to be imported somewhere in
    the binary (it registers the loader via init). If the config package is
    not imported and ServiceConfig is nil, the service starts without provider
    config (ListProviders/HealthCheck will return errors until config is
    injected).

type HarnessInfo struct {
	Name                 string
	Type                 string // "native" | "subprocess"
	Available            bool
	Path                 string
	Error                string
	IsLocal              bool
	IsSubscription       bool
	ExactPinSupport      bool
	DefaultModel         string   // built-in default model when no override is supplied
	SupportedPermissions []string // subset of {"safe","supervised","unrestricted"}
	SupportedReasoning   []string // values such as {"low","medium","high","xhigh","max"}
	CostClass            string   // "local" | "cheap" | "medium" | "expensive"
	Quota                *QuotaState
}
    HarnessInfo describes a registered harness as defined in CONTRACT-003.

type HealthTarget struct {
	Type string // "harness" | "provider"
	Name string
}
    HealthTarget identifies what to health-check.

type ModelFilter struct {
	Harness  string
	Provider string
}
    ModelFilter filters ListModels results.

type ModelInfo struct {
	ID            string
	Provider      string
	Harness       string
	ContextLength int
	Capabilities  []string
	Cost          CostInfo
	PerfSignal    PerfSignal
	Available     bool
	IsConfigured  bool   // matches an explicit model_routes entry
	IsDefault     bool   // matches the configured default model
	CatalogRef    string // canonical catalog reference if recognized
	RankPosition  int    // ordinal in latest discovery rank; -1 if unranked
}
    ModelInfo describes a model with full metadata per CONTRACT-003.

type PerfSignal struct {
	SpeedTokensPerSec float64 // 0 = unknown
	SWEBenchVerified  float64 // 0 = unknown
}
    PerfSignal holds observed performance data for a model.

type ProviderInfo struct {
	Name          string
	Type          string // "openai" | "openrouter" | "lmstudio" | "omlx" | "ollama" | "anthropic" | "virtual"
	BaseURL       string
	Endpoints     []ServiceProviderEndpoint
	Status        string // "connected" | "unreachable" | "error: <msg>"
	ModelCount    int
	Capabilities  []string       // e.g. {"tool_use","streaming","json_mode"}
	IsDefault     bool           // matches the configured default_provider
	DefaultModel  string         // per-provider configured default model, if any
	CooldownState *CooldownState // nil if not in cooldown
}
    ProviderInfo describes a provider with live status per CONTRACT-003.

type QuotaState struct {
	Windows    []harnesses.QuotaWindow `json:"windows"`
	CapturedAt time.Time               `json:"captured_at"`
	Fresh      bool                    `json:"fresh"`
}
    QuotaState is a live quota snapshot for a harness. Nil means not applicable.

type Reasoning = reasoning.Reasoning

func ReasoningTokens(n int) Reasoning

type RouteCandidateStatus struct {
	Provider          string
	Model             string
	Priority          int
	Healthy           bool
	Cooldown          *CooldownState
	RecentLatencyMS   float64 // observation-derived; 0 when unavailable
	RecentSuccessRate float64 // 0-1; 0 when unavailable
}
    RouteCandidateStatus describes a single candidate within a route.

type RouteDecision struct {
	Harness  string
	Provider string
	Model    string
	Reason   string
}
    RouteDecision is the result of ResolveRoute.

type RouteRequest struct {
	Model       string
	Provider    string
	Harness     string
	ModelRef    string
	Reasoning   Reasoning
	Permissions string
}
    RouteRequest specifies a routing query.

type RouteStatusEntry struct {
	Model          string // route key
	Strategy       string // "priority-round-robin" | "first-available" | etc.
	Candidates     []RouteCandidateStatus
	LastDecision   *RouteDecision // most recent ResolveRoute result for this key (cached)
	LastDecisionAt time.Time
}
    RouteStatusEntry describes one configured model route.

type RouteStatusReport struct {
	Routes          []RouteStatusEntry
	GeneratedAt     time.Time
	GlobalCooldowns []CooldownState
}
    RouteStatusReport is returned by RouteStatus.

type ServiceConfig interface {
	// ProviderNames returns provider names in stable order (default first).
	ProviderNames() []string
	// DefaultProviderName returns the name of the configured default provider.
	DefaultProviderName() string
	// Provider returns the raw config values for a named provider.
	Provider(name string) (ServiceProviderEntry, bool)
	// ModelRouteNames returns configured model-route names.
	ModelRouteNames() []string
	// ModelRouteCandidates returns the provider names referenced by a route.
	ModelRouteCandidates(routeName string) []string
	// ModelRouteConfig returns the full route config for a named route.
	// Returns zero value when the route is not found.
	ModelRouteConfig(routeName string) ServiceModelRouteConfig
	// HealthCooldown returns the configured cooldown duration (0 = use default 30s).
	HealthCooldown() time.Duration
	// WorkDir is the base directory for file-backed health state.
	WorkDir() string
}
    ServiceConfig provides provider and routing data to the service without
    creating an import cycle from the root package into agent/config. Callers
    wrap their loaded *config.Config in a type that satisfies this interface.

type ServiceEvent = harnesses.Event
    ServiceEvent is a contract-level event (mirrors harnesses.Event).

type ServiceExecuteRequest struct {
	Prompt       string
	SystemPrompt string
	Model        string
	Provider     string
	Harness      string
	ModelRef     string
	WorkDir      string
	Temperature  float32
	Seed         int64
	Reasoning    Reasoning
	Permissions  string

	// PreResolved bypasses ResolveRoute when the caller already has a
	// decision. When non-nil, agent uses these values verbatim and does
	// not re-route. Provider/Model/Harness fields above are ignored.
	PreResolved *RouteDecision

	// Three independent timeout knobs:
	//   Timeout         — wall-clock cap on the entire request.
	//   IdleTimeout     — streaming-quiet cap; per-stream gap.
	//   ProviderTimeout — per-HTTP-request cap to the provider.
	Timeout         time.Duration
	IdleTimeout     time.Duration
	ProviderTimeout time.Duration

	// Optional stall policy. When non-nil agent enforces and ends with
	// Status="stalled" if any limit hits. Default policy applies when nil.
	StallPolicy *StallPolicy

	// SessionLogDir overrides the default session-log directory for this
	// request (e.g. an execute-bead per-bundle evidence directory).
	SessionLogDir string

	// Metadata is bidirectional: echoed back in every Event AND stamped
	// onto every line of the session log so external consumers correlate.
	Metadata map[string]string
}
    ServiceExecuteRequest is the public ExecuteRequest type per CONTRACT-003.
    See docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md
    (§"Public types") for the canonical shape; this struct is its in-process
    twin under the agent module.

type ServiceModelRouteConfig struct {
	Strategy   string                       // "priority-round-robin" | "ordered-failover" | "smart" | ""
	Candidates []ServiceRouteCandidateEntry // ordered candidate list
}
    ServiceModelRouteConfig carries the routing strategy and candidates for one
    route.

type ServiceOptions struct {
	ConfigPath string    // optional override; default $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/ddx-agent/config.yaml
	Logger     io.Writer // optional; agent writes structured session logs internally regardless

	// ServiceConfig, when non-nil, supplies provider and routing data for
	// ListProviders and HealthCheck. Pass a value wrapping the loaded config.
	// When nil, those methods return an error.
	ServiceConfig ServiceConfig
	// Has unexported fields.
}
    ServiceOptions configures a DdxAgent instance.

    seamOptions is embedded so production builds (no testseam tag) get an empty
    struct — making it a compile-time error to set seam fields without the
    build tag. Test builds inherit the four CONTRACT-003 seams (FakeProvider,
    PromptAssertionHook, CompactionAssertionHook, ToolWiringHook) automatically.

type ServiceProviderEndpoint struct {
	Name    string
	BaseURL string
}
    ServiceProviderEndpoint is one configured provider serving endpoint.

type ServiceProviderEntry struct {
	Type      string // "openai" | "openrouter" | "lmstudio" | "omlx" | "ollama" | "anthropic"
	BaseURL   string
	Endpoints []ServiceProviderEndpoint
	APIKey    string
	Model     string // configured default model (may be empty)
}
    ServiceProviderEntry carries the minimal provider data the service needs.

type ServiceRouteCandidateEntry struct {
	Provider string
	Model    string // may be empty (provider default)
	Priority int
}
    ServiceRouteCandidateEntry is one candidate inside a model route.

type StallPolicy struct {
	MaxReadOnlyToolIterations int // 0 = disabled
	MaxNoopCompactions        int // 0 = disabled
}
    StallPolicy bounds how long the agent will spin without making progress
    before terminating with Status="stalled". A nil policy resolves to the
    default in service_execute.go.

     6	## [Unreleased]

     8	## [v0.5.0] — 2026-04-19
     9	
    10	### Breaking
    11	- **Removed the legacy `agent.Run` API from the public module surface.**
    12	  The former `Request`, `Result`, `Provider`, `StreamingProvider`, `Tool`,
    13	  `Message`, `ToolDef`, `Event`, session-log DTO, compaction callback, pricing,
    14	  and provider-conformance exports now live behind `internal/` for agent-owned
    15	  code only. External consumers must use `agent.New(...).Execute(...)` and the
    16	  DdxAgent service contract.
    17	- **Removed public compatibility packages for the old provider/tool/session
    18	  stack.** Provider implementations, compaction, prompt building, built-in
    19	  tools, session replay/logging, and provider conformance helpers are no longer
    20	  importable outside this module; Go `internal/` enforcement now blocks
    21	  consumers that have not migrated.
    22	
    23	### Changed
    24	- The standalone `ddx-agent` binary continues to use the internal native loop,
    25	  but that loop is no longer part of the exported Go API.
    26	
    27	### Breaking
    28	- **Removed harness-flavored prompt preset names.** The system prompt preset
    29	  surface now accepts only intent-flavored names: `default`, `smart`, `cheap`,
    30	  `minimal`, and `benchmark`. The old `agent`, `worker`, `cursor`, `claude`,
    31	  and `codex` names now fail with a clear replacement hint instead of
    32	  resolving as aliases. (`.execute-bead-wt-agent-a365bcf2-20260418T023201-36db884d-346c73b4`)
    33	- **Renamed the file-discovery tool from `glob` to `find`.** The built-in tool
    34	  catalog now exposes only `find`; there is no `glob` compatibility alias.
    35	  (`agent-1b00b3ea`)
    36	
    37	## [v0.3.14] — 2026-04-18
    38	
    39	### Fixed
    40	- **Filter SSE comment frames before the `ssestream` decoder.**

   409	
   410	## Reasoning contract
   411	
   412	`Reasoning` is the only preferred public control for model-side reasoning.
   413	Consumers do not set separate public thinking, effort, level, or budget fields.
   414	The scalar accepts named values (`auto`, `off`, `low`, `medium`, `high`) and
   415	provider/harness-supported extended values such as `minimal`, `xhigh` /
   416	`x-high`, and `max`. It also accepts numeric values through
   417	`ReasoningTokens(n)`, where `0` means explicit off and positive integers mean
   418	an explicit max reasoning-token budget or documented provider-equivalent
   419	numeric value.
   420	
   421	Normalization is tri-state:
   422	
   423	- Empty means no caller preference.
   424	- `auto` means resolve model, catalog, or provider defaults.
   425	- `off`, `none`, `false`, and `0` mean explicit reasoning off.
   426	- Positive integers mean an explicit numeric request.
   427	
   428	Default portable named-to-token budgets are `low=2048`, `medium=8192`, and
   429	`high=32768` only when a selected provider/model does not publish a more
   430	specific map. Providers and subprocess harnesses may map resolved reasoning to
   431	wire or CLI knobs named `reasoning`, `thinking`, `effort`, `variant`, or a
   432	numeric budget. They may also drop auto/default reasoning controls for models
   433	that do not support explicit reasoning control. Explicit unsupported values,
   434	unknown extended values, and over-limit numeric values fail clearly.
   435	
   436	Catalog tier defaults are part of route resolution: below-smart tiers
   437	(`cheap`, `fast`, `standard`, `code-economy`, and `code-medium`) default to
   438	`reasoning=off`, including local/economy Qwen targets. `smart` and `code-high`
   439	default to `reasoning=high`. Any explicit caller `Reasoning` value wins over
   440	these defaults, including supported values above high such as `xhigh` or
   441	`max`, and numeric values.
   442	
   443	## Sampling contract
   444	
   445	`ExecuteRequest.Temperature` and `ExecuteRequest.Seed` are the portable
   446	sampling controls. `Temperature=0` requests deterministic sampling. `Seed=0`
   447	means unset and lets the provider choose. Native OpenAI-compatible providers
   448	honor both fields. Providers or subprocess harnesses that do not expose an
   449	equivalent seed control may ignore `Seed`; callers that require strict parity
   450	must treat those runs as advisory/non-deterministic.
   451	
   452	## Bead Execution Policy
   453	
   454	DDx bead implementation should use a two-pass policy against this service:
   455	try a cheap or standard profile with `reasoning=off` first, then escalate only
   456	when the first pass produced evidence that a smarter model is likely to help.
   457	The initial pass should use `ModelRef=cheap` or `ModelRef=standard` with an
   458	explicit `ReasoningOff` request so local/economy models do not spend
   459	reasoning tokens by default.
   460

### Summary

The legacy loop/API removal and release changelog are substantially implemented, and the tag exists on the reviewed commit. The blocking issue is the “minimal public surface” criterion: the module still exposes public packages and root-level helper/config symbols outside the service contract.

acceptance:
1. ddx-agent exposes catalog show, catalog check, and catalog update commands.
2. catalog show reports manifest source, schema version, catalog version, and key tier mappings.
3. catalog check compares the local or embedded catalog against published channel metadata without affecting normal run behavior.
4. catalog update installs a verified manifest to the configured local path, preserves the previous file on failure, and never introduces implicit network fetches into ordinary request execution.
5. Integration tests cover happy path, checksum failure, unsupported schema version, and embedded fallback when no external file is installed.
description:
Add explicit catalog lifecycle commands to ddx-agent. The runtime must stay offline-by-default, but operators need a safe way to inspect the current manifest source and install newer shared policy locally. Implement the catalog command surface from the design: show, check, and update.

This command family should verify metadata and checksum before replacing the local manifest and should never mutate the embedded snapshot.
title: Add ddx-agent catalog show check and update commands

acceptance:
1. `go build ./...` succeeds; `go test ./...` passes (no behavior change).
2. `make build` produces binary named exactly `ddx-agent` (same as today).
3. `bash tests/install_sh_acceptance.sh` passes.
4. `internal/productinfo/productinfo.go` exists and exports `Name`, `BinaryName`, `ConfigDir` as `var` (not `const`).
5. Every non-test Go file under `cmd/` and `internal/` that previously contained `"ddx-agent"`, `"ddx-agent-bench"`, or `"ddx-agent-benchscore"` as a literal now references `productinfo.BinaryName` / derived constant / `productinfo.ConfigDir` / `productinfo.Name`. Verify with: `git grep -n '"ddx-agent' -- '*.go' ':(exclude)*_test.go' ':(exclude)internal/productinfo/*'` returns zero hits.
6. `Makefile` contains exactly one literal `ddx-agent` (the `BINARY_NAME := ddx-agent` assignment). Verify: `grep -c 'ddx-agent' Makefile` equals `1`.
7. `install.sh` references `${BINARY_NAME}` for the binary name. Literal `ddx-agent` appears only in the `BINARY_NAME=` assignment and in human prose (comments, success banners). Verify by inspection.
8. `.github/workflows/release.yml` declares `BINARY_NAME` once (at `env:`) and references it via `${{ env.BINARY_NAME }}` or `$BINARY_NAME` in shell steps. Literal `ddx-agent-` appears only in the env declaration or as a hyphenated derivation of the env var.
9. Running `./ddx-agent --version` still shows the version string; `./ddx-agent --help` still shows usage with `ddx-agent` as the program name.
10. State paths unchanged: `~/.config/ddx-agent/`, `~/.local/state/ddx-agent/` still resolve correctly (no existing users lose access to cached state).
description:
Follow-on to `agent-87b1b347` (closed in 794dd1d) — that bead decoupled `cmd/` directory names from the product brand. This bead pushes the decoupling down into Go source and shell/CI literals so product-identity strings live behind a small, replaceable surface.

## Goal

Reduce a future rename to changing 3 constants + module path + prose, with zero risk of missed call sites hidden in error messages, prompts, or state paths.

## Design: one Go package, one shell variable

**New Go package: `internal/productinfo/productinfo.go`**

```go
package productinfo

// Values stay at their current literals for this bead — no behavior change.
// A future rename changes these here; call sites update automatically.
var (
    Name       = "DDx Agent"   // human-readable prose
    BinaryName = "ddx-agent"   // executable name, log prefixes, state dirs
    ConfigDir  = "ddx-agent"   // subdir under ~/.config/, ~/.local/state/, XDG paths
)
```

Make them `var` (not `const`) so future versions can ldflags-inject or test-override. No ldflags injection in this bead — just the indirection.

**Shell/CI: one variable at the top of each file**

- `Makefile`: `BINARY_NAME := ddx-agent` near VERSION/BUILD_TIME; replace literals with `$(BINARY_NAME)`
- `install.sh`: `BINARY_NAME="${BINARY_NAME:-ddx-agent}"` near top; replace literals with `${BINARY_NAME}` (keep human prose like `"# DDX Agent installer"` as-is)
- `.github/workflows/release.yml`: add `env: BINARY_NAME: ddx-agent` at job level; replace literals with `${{ env.BINARY_NAME }}` in shell steps

## In-scope Go call sites (20 refs across 10 files)

All of these hardcode `"ddx-agent"` or `"ddx-agent-bench"` / `"ddx-agent-benchscore"` literals. Migrate to `productinfo.BinaryName` (possibly with a `BenchBinaryName` derived constant `BinaryName + "-bench"` etc., or three separate constants — implementer's call):

**CLI flag sets and exec calls:**
- `cmd/agent/main.go:49` — `flag.NewFlagSet("ddx-agent", ...)`
- `cmd/agent/update.go:190` — `exec.Command("which", "ddx-agent")`

**Bench/benchscore usage strings:**
- `cmd/bench/main.go:1,32,39,46` — package comment + usage/error strings
- `cmd/bench/discovery.go:101` — error printf
- `cmd/bench/report.go:35,46,52` — error printf
- `cmd/bench/runner.go:191,198,202,209,225,233,237,245,252,297` — error printf + `Name: "ddx-agent-bench"` struct field
- `cmd/benchscore/main.go:17,23,29` — usage/error strings

**State/config directory paths (most user-visible):**
- `internal/harnesses/claude/quota_cache.go:52,58` — `filepath.Join(xdg, "ddx-agent", ...)` and `filepath.Join(home, ".local", "state", "ddx-agent", ...)`
- `internal/harnesses/codex/quota_cache.go:32,38` — same pattern
- `internal/observations/observations.go:244,248,250` — `~/.config/ddx-agent/observations.yaml`

Use `productinfo.ConfigDir` here (not `BinaryName`) so the two can diverge if needed.

**Harness registry self-entry:**
- `internal/harnesses/registry.go:97` — `Binary: "ddx-agent"`

**Prompt presets (agent identifies itself to LLM):**
- `internal/prompt/presets.go:76` — `"Balanced, tool-aware prompt — the DDX Agent default"`
- `internal/prompt/presets.go:115` — `"DDX Agent benchmark mode — non-interactive, optimized for evaluation"`
- `internal/prompt/presets.go:116` — prompt body `"You are a coding agent running inside DDX Agent in benchmark mode..."`

Use `productinfo.Name` for prose-style references.

**Doc comments (low priority, update opportunistically):**
- `service.go:16,94,352` — `// DdxAgent is the entire public Go surface...`, `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/ddx-agent/config.yaml`, `CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md`
- `cmd/catalogdist/main.go:24` — flag description `"Minimum compatible ddx-agent version"`

## Shell/CI call sites

**`Makefile` (2 refs):**
- line 9: `go build $(LDFLAGS) -o ddx-agent ./cmd/agent`
- line 98: `rm -f ddx-agent`

**`install.sh` (~17 refs):** lines 2, 22, 26, 30, 34, 66, 103, 110, 112, 116, 118, 154, 165, 167, 189, 190, 194, 195, 205, 208, 209. Most expand `BINARY_NAME`; the "DDX Agent installer" comment and "🎉 DDX Agent installed successfully" banner stay as human prose.

**`.github/workflows/release.yml` (7 refs):** lines 48, 53, 54, 73, 74, 78, 82. All expand `${{ env.BINARY_NAME }}` or shell `$BINARY_NAME`.

## Explicitly out of scope

- **Module path `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent`** — separate concern; if we adopt a stable codename for the module path (see product decisions), that's a different bead.
- **README, CONTRIBUTING, CHANGELOG, website prose** — inherently human-facing; the rename PR handles these.
- **HELIX docs under `docs/helix/**`** — rename sweep handles, not in refactor scope.
- **Test fixtures using `"DDX Agent"` as example config values** (`internal/config/config_test.go:304`, `cmd/agent/cli_process_test.go:351`, `occompat/occompat_test.go:158`) — these verify parser behavior on arbitrary user-provided strings; the "DDX Agent" value is incidental, not a product-name reference.
- **Test assertions that check specific binary-name output** — update only if they fail after source migration; otherwise leave as behavioral assertions.
- **Config/cache path migration shims** — not needed in this bead (values unchanged). Will be needed when the rename bead changes `ConfigDir` value.
- **ldflags-based injection of productinfo vars** — keep it simple; just the indirection here.

## Why not `const`?

Using `var` leaves the door open for future ldflags-injection (`-X productinfo.BinaryName=foo`) without a schema change. No actual injection added here.

## Rollback

All changes are additive (new package) + mechanical string-to-symbol replacements. Revert the commit if anything breaks. No migrations, no external state, no user-visible behavior change.

acceptance: go test ./... passes; new test TestRun_MidTurnOverflowRetry exists in loop_test.go and covers: (1) provider returns context-overflow error on first call mid-turn, (2) compaction runs, (3) provider is called again with compacted history, (4) turn completes successfully with no ErrContextOverflow returned to caller
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 — OTel GenAI spans and JSONL session log are separate observability surfaces; error paths must record spans with error semantics</adrs>
<governing>SD-006 §Mid-iteration check — after tool results are appended, check context usage; if overflow, compact in-place and retry the same turn without aborting. FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-03 — transient retry and error paths must not issue extra provider calls beyond policy.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently loop.go handles context overflow by compacting and then aborting the turn. Codex discovered that aborting is unnecessary — after compaction succeeds, the same provider call can be retried within the same turn.

What to change in loop.go:
- In the mid-turn overflow recovery path (after IsContextOverflowError is detected and compaction runs), instead of returning an error, retry the pending provider call with the compacted history.
- Cap mid-turn retries at 1 (if the compacted history still overflows, abort with error to avoid infinite retry).
- Emit EventCompactionStart / EventCompactionEnd as today; add a mid_turn: true field on the compaction events so callers can distinguish mid-turn from pre-turn compaction.
- All existing overflow/compaction tests must still pass.
- Add new test TestRun_MidTurnOverflowRetry.

Governing specs: SD-006, FEAT-001.
title: feat: mid-turn overflow compaction — retry turn after inline compact

acceptance:
1. Docs define `reasoning` as the single public control knob and `thinking`/`effort` as provider or harness adapter terminology.
2. Docs define normalization for `none`, `false`, and `0` to explicit off, and distinguish empty/auto/off.
3. Docs define named values `auto|off|low|medium|high` and explicitly allow provider/harness-supported extended values such as `minimal`, `xhigh`/`x-high`, and `max`.
4. Docs define numeric reasoning values in the same `reasoning` key/field/flag, including numeric 0 as explicit off.
5. Docs define the shared leaf package approach for the Reasoning type/parser to avoid root-agent/internal-modelcatalog import cycles.
6. Docs define default portable named-to-token budgets for low/medium/high and state provider/catalog maps override them.
7. Docs state providers that only accept numeric reasoning controls must map named levels to numeric budgets and enforce model-specific max reasoning-token limits.
8. Docs define public config key `reasoning`, with `thinking_*` only as deprecated aliases if retained.
9. Docs define public catalog metadata `reasoning_default`, with `effort_default` only as a deprecated alias if retained, and require per-model reasoning maximums/maps when a numeric-only provider/model cannot derive safe limits live.
10. Docs state below-smart defaults are `reasoning=off`, explicitly including standard/code-medium and Qwen local/economy tiers.
11. Docs state smart/code-high defaults to `reasoning=high`, while explicit caller values above high or numeric values are permitted when supported.
12. Docs state unsupported providers/models may drop auto/default reasoning controls, but explicit unsupported reasoning values or over-limit numeric values should fail clearly rather than silently downgrade.
13. Required terminology is present in docs: `rg "reasoning|reasoning_default|minimal|xhigh|x-high|max" docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-003-providers.md docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-004-model-routing.md docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-005-provider-config.md` returns matches.
14. Public docs do not introduce preferred `reasoning_level` or `reasoning_budget` keys.
15. `go test ./internal/modelcatalog ./internal/config ./cmd/ddx-agent` passes after doc-only changes.
description:
Define the contract before implementation so downstream agents do not add parallel top-level `effort`, `thinking`, `reasoning_level`, or `reasoning_budget` knobs. Current state: FEAT-003 documents provider config `thinking_level`/`thinking_budget`; FEAT-004 and the catalog use `effort_default`; CONTRACT-003 has `ServiceExecuteRequest.Effort`; CLI docs do not define a direct run flag for reasoning; provider implementation treats `thinking` as an OpenAI-compatible wire field gated by flavor.

Required policy:
- `reasoning` is the single user-facing and service-facing model-reasoning control.
- Public named values are `auto`, `off`, `low`, `medium`, `high`; aliases `none` and `false` normalize to `off`.
- Extended named values are allowed when the selected provider/harness advertises support, including examples like `minimal`, `xhigh`/`x-high`, and `max`. Normalize spelling variants where reasonable (`x-high` -> `xhigh`) but never silently downgrade an explicit extended request.
- Numeric values are accepted by the same `reasoning` field/flag/key. Numeric values mean explicit max reasoning tokens or a documented provider-equivalent numeric value. Numeric 0 means explicit off.
- Tri-state semantics are canonical: empty/unset means no caller preference; `auto` means resolve model/catalog/provider defaults; `off`/`none`/`false`/`0` means explicit off; positive integer means explicit numeric budget.
- Shared implementation shape: create a small leaf package such as `internal/reasoning` that owns the canonical Reasoning scalar, constants, `ReasoningTokens(n)` helper, parser, normalization, and policy representation. Root package `agent` may re-export `type Reasoning = reasoning.Reasoning` plus constants/helpers so embedding callers use `agent.Reasoning`; internal packages such as `internal/modelcatalog` must import the leaf package, not root `agent`, to avoid import cycles.
- Public Go embedding shape should remain one field: use `type Reasoning string` (or equivalent typed scalar) with constants for named values and helper `ReasoningTokens(n int) Reasoning` for numeric values. Internally, parsing may produce a richer policy struct, but that is not the public input surface.
- Parser must accept scalar string and scalar integer inputs from YAML/JSON/config/catalog layers and normalize them to one canonical reasoning value.
- Default portable named-to-token budgets, used only when a provider/model does not publish a more specific map, are low=2048, medium=8192, high=32768. Extended values such as minimal/xhigh/max require provider/harness/catalog capability metadata before use.
- Provider implementations own mapping named values (`low`, `medium`, `high`, `minimal`, `xhigh`, `max`, etc.) to numeric budgets when the underlying model/API only accepts numbers. Those mappings must be capability-aware and respect model-specific maximum reasoning-token limits.
- `max` resolves at provider/harness boundary to the selected model/provider maximum. Numeric-capable models must expose this maximum through catalog metadata or provider capability metadata before `max` can be accepted.
- Public config key is `reasoning`. Legacy `thinking_level`/`thinking_budget` may be read only as deprecated aliases during migration; `reasoning` wins if both are set.
- Public catalog metadata is `reasoning_default`. Per-model/provider reasoning maximums or named-value maps are required for any catalog candidate whose selected provider/model accepts only numeric reasoning controls and cannot derive safe limits from live provider metadata. Legacy `effort_default` may be read only as a deprecated alias during migration; `reasoning_default` wins if both are set.
- Public CLI flag is `--reasoning <value>`. Do not add public `--thinking`, `--effort`, `--reasoning-level`, or `--reasoning-budget` unless a separate migration bead proves a consumer requirement; if added, it must be deprecated and normalize into `reasoning`.
- Internally, resolved reasoning may map to provider/model knobs named `reasoning`, `thinking`, `effort`, `variant`, or a token budget. It may also be dropped when the selected model/provider does not support explicit reasoning control and the value came from auto/default policy.
- Existing `Effort` service/harness fields are compatibility/adaptor terminology during migration; new CLI/config/catalog docs should prefer `reasoning`.
- Below-smart tiers (`cheap`, `fast`, `standard`, `code-economy`, `code-medium`) default to `reasoning=off`; `smart`/`code-high` defaults to `reasoning=high`.
- Explicit caller reasoning always wins over tier defaults, including explicit numeric values and requests above high such as `xhigh`/`x-high` or `max` when supported by the selected provider/harness.
- Bead implementation policy: first attempt should use cheap or standard models with `reasoning=off`; on eligible failure, retry with smart.

In-scope files:
- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-003-providers.md
- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-004-model-routing.md
- docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md
- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-005-provider-config.md

Out-of-scope:
- Code changes to agent.go, cmd/ddx-agent, internal/provider/openai, or internal/modelcatalog.
- DDx repo execute-loop implementation; only document/reference the paired consumer requirement.
title: spec: define reasoning as the public model-reasoning control contract

acceptance:
1. Integration tests are organized around the public capability matrix: every supported required capability is exercised for every required harness, and unsupported optional capabilities are asserted as unavailable.
2. Record mode can run against live claude, codex, pi, and opencode, fails fast with clear diagnostics when authentication or binaries are missing, and records cassettes with scrubbed environment/credential data.
3. Replay mode runs deterministically without credentials and verifies event order, progress events, final text, usage where available, quota/status where available, routing metadata, and session log metadata.
4. Tests cover model selection, configured reasoning level, workdir/context behavior, timeout/idle handling, and metadata propagation through Service.Execute.
5. The suite distinguishes reusable deterministic harness tests from real live-harness evidence and does not claim ad-hoc fake-binary tests as live integration coverage.
6. Documentation explains how to run record mode and replay mode.
7. go test -tags=integration ./... passes in replay mode without live credentials.
8. Record-mode commands for each live harness are documented and fail fast when credentials are missing.
description:
Build the integration-level evidence suite for the harness migration. The suite must prove the service can drive each supported live harness and can replay recorded interactions deterministically. This is the answer to whether wrapping Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Pi, and opencode is actually supported and tested.

Required harnesses for this bead: claude, codex, pi, opencode. Gemini may be included if the service dispatch bead wires it and the capability matrix marks it supported.

Required behaviors to test per harness when supported by its capability matrix:
- run a prompt through Service.Execute
- list or explicitly report model support/no-discovery semantics
- pass model, reasoning level, permissions, workdir/context, timeout, and metadata
- capture progress events and final normalized text
- capture token usage when the harness exposes it
- capture quota/status data for claude and codex when the capability matrix marks quota supported
- replay the same interaction sequence identically from cassette without credentials

Record mode must be opt-in and must fail fast when credentials, subscription, binary, configured model, transport dependency, or quota permissions are missing. Replay mode must be runnable in normal CI without live credentials. Ad-hoc fake binaries are acceptable only for shared parser/process infrastructure; they must not be counted as live harness integration evidence.
title: harness-test: build golden-master integration suite for real harnesses

acceptance:
- [ ] service.HealthCheck for claude/codex triggers tmux quota refresh when cache > 60s old
- [ ] ListHarnesses returns fresh HarnessInfo.Quota immediately after HealthCheck
- [ ] Test asserts: HealthCheck on claude triggers a write to the quota cache file (or a refresh of in-memory state)
- [ ] Test asserts: HealthCheck on non-tmux harness (gemini, opencode) does NOT trigger tmux invocation
description:
Surfaced by ddx-f49d7d1a (DDx tracker) revised decision. DDx delegates quota probing to ddx-agent. The agent already has tmux scrape implementations (internal/harnesses/{claude,codex}/quota_tmux.go from agent-f9ea8f16) and a quota cache (internal/harnesses/claude/quota_cache.go from agent-b0b7f306). What is missing: when DDx calls service.HealthCheck(target=claude or codex harness), the agent should refresh the tmux quota cache as part of the probe, then ListHarnesses returns the fresh HarnessInfo.Quota.

Today (per service_providers.go:106-117) HealthCheck for case "harness" only calls Registry.Discover() — checks binary availability. Doesnt trigger tmux quota refresh.

Extension: in case "harness" handler, when target.Name in {claude, codex}, call the per-harness internal quota refresh (the existing tmux scrape) so the cache is populated. ListHarnesses will then return the fresh value.

Cache freshness window: maybe accept "if cache is older than 60s, refresh; else use cached". This avoids tmux invocation on every HealthCheck.

Future: alternative to tmux — direct PTY manipulation (claude/codex CLIs may support quota query via stdin/stdout pipe in newer versions; investigate and add as PathB alternative).
title: service.HealthCheck refreshes tmux quota on demand for claude/codex harnesses

description:
Improve operator visibility into Codex usage using DDx-owned session logs, not private Codex sessions. The public HarnessInfo type already has UsageWindows, but Codex does not populate it. Claude/Codex review agreed that ~/.codex/sessions should be used only as fresh quota/status evidence, not as general historical analytics. Historical usage should come from DDx's own session logging and internal/session.AggregateUsage.

Required behavior:
- Add a service-side usage status path that can populate Codex-related UsageWindows from DDx-owned session logs when a log directory is configured/known.
- Use internal/session.AggregateUsage or a narrow shared helper rather than reading ~/.codex/sessions for historical usage.
- Report input/output/total/cache/reasoning tokens and known/unknown cost status only where DDx final events contain that data.
- Preserve privacy: do not read private Codex session files, raw prompts, responses, or tool payloads for this status surface.
- Keep this as status/reporting only; it must not affect routing eligibility or quota refresh.

In-scope files:
- service.go if UsageWindow fields need helper population
- service status/list harness implementation files
- internal/session usage helpers if needed
- service status/list harness tests
- cmd/agent usage/status output tests only if CLI output changes

Out-of-scope:
- Parsing ~/.codex/sessions for historical usage.
- Changing quota routing or refresh.
- Adding provider price/cost estimates where final events report unknown cost.
title: service: surface Codex usage from DDx-owned session logs

acceptance: DDX Agent governing artifacts explicitly define the shared model catalog and external manifest requirement; prompt presets are clearly distinguished from model policy; ownership split across agent, DDx, and HELIX is documented; downstream beads agent-94b5d420 and agent-66eef6fe are traceable to the evolved artifacts.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Make Intent Explicit · Validate Your Work · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>go library packages should have clear ownership boundaries; rapidly changing policy/data should be separable from core code; design docs must reflect the actual ownership split with downstream consumers</practices>
<governing>Recent discussion established a new ownership model: agent should own the reusable shared model catalog and updateable manifest; DDx should consume that catalog for harness/model resolution and guardrails; HELIX should own stage intent only. Existing agent docs (notably FEAT-004 / SD-005) currently focus on provider/routing concepts and do not yet explicitly thread this shared-model-catalog requirement through the artifact stack.</governing>
<evidence>
- agent has prompt presets already (`prompt/presets.go`), so model policy must remain a separate concept and naming surface.
- DDx currently duplicates model policy in `cli/internal/agent/models.go` and `cli/internal/agent/registry.go`.
- HELIX currently duplicates concrete model-version policy in `scripts/helix`.
- New agent beads `agent-94b5d420` and `agent-66eef6fe` cover design/build work for a shared model catalog, but the governing agent artifacts have not yet been explicitly evolved to reflect that requirement.
- Model release cadence is high enough that agent likely needs a separately maintained manifest/data source rather than only hardcoded Go tables.
</evidence>
</context-digest>

Thread the new shared-model-catalog requirement through agent's governing artifacts before implementation.

Scope:
- Identify which agent artifacts must change to make the shared model catalog canonical (PRD, FEAT-004, SD-005, SD-003, and any adjacent artifact that defines prompt presets, routing, or config ownership).
- Update artifact language so prompt presets, model tiers/profiles, aliases, canonical targets, deprecations, and manifest/data ownership are clearly separated.
- Make the ownership split explicit: agent owns model catalog data/policy, DDx consumes it for harness orchestration, HELIX consumes DDx tier/model resolution for stage execution.
- Clarify how the separately maintained model manifest relates to agent releases and consumer update workflows.
- Ensure downstream design/build beads (`agent-94b5d420`, `agent-66eef6fe`) trace cleanly back to the updated governing artifacts.

Expected deliverables:
- Updated agent planning/design artifacts that explicitly authorize a shared model catalog + external manifest.
- Clear terminology that distinguishes prompt presets from model policy.
- Traceability from evolved artifacts to the existing agent design/build beads.
title: Evolve agent artifacts for shared model catalog and external manifest

acceptance:
1. Primary harness replay tests cover success text, final status, routing_actual, usage when available, metadata echo, and failure final status.
2. Primary harness replay tests cover tool_call/tool_result ordering for every primary harness that claims `ToolEvents` optional.
3. Native agent replay/service tests prove a successful real tool execution, not only an unknown-tool/stall path.
4. The replay suite remains deterministic and skips live record mode unless `AGENT_HARNESS_RECORD=1` is set.
5. `go test -tags=integration . -run TestHarnessGoldenReplay_ServiceExecute` and `go test -tags=testseam .` pass.
description:
The current harness golden integration replay is useful but too shallow for the claims being made. It mainly verifies text/final/usage happy paths from fake POSIX scripts and does not cover primary-harness parity behavior such as successful tool calls, tool results, failures, timeout/cancellation surfaces, or metadata/session-log consistency. Strengthen replay-only coverage so CI proves the primary harness contract without requiring live credentials.

Prioritize codex, claude, and agent. Secondary harnesses (opencode, pi, gemini) only need coverage for their currently claimed capabilities unless a later bead promotes them.
title: harness-tests: expand primary harness golden replay coverage

acceptance: Test with virtual provider: 10+ tool call rounds with small context window forcing compaction; verify compaction fires, summary is structured, file lists present, conversation continues after compaction
description: End-to-end compaction test per SD-006.
title: Integration test: multi-round task with compaction

acceptance:
1. CONTRACT-003 defines the harness capability matrix and marks each capability required, optional, unsupported, or not-applicable.
2. Public service API exposes the matrix through ListHarnesses or an equivalent method without consumers reading internal registry structs.
3. Tests assert the matrix for agent, claude, codex, pi, opencode, gemini, virtual, script, openrouter, lmstudio, and omlx.
4. Claimed capabilities line up with existing registry/support fields for models, reasoning, permissions, locality, subscription status, and test-only status.
5. go test . ./internal/harnesses ./internal/routing passes.
6. go test ./... passes.
description:
Define and expose the supported-capabilities matrix for every registered harness so implementation and tests stop relying on implicit assumptions. The matrix must distinguish required capabilities from optional capabilities and must be visible through the public service boundary, not only internal registry constants.

Required capability categories to classify per harness:
- execute prompt through Service.Execute
- supported model discovery or explicit no-discovery semantics
- model pinning
- workdir/context support
- reasoning level support
- permission mode support
- progress events
- token/usage capture
- final normalized text
- tool-call/tool-result events
- quota/status monitoring
- record/replay or deterministic test support

Initial harnesses to classify: agent, claude, codex, pi, opencode, gemini, virtual, script, openrouter, lmstudio, omlx. The result should drive later integration-test expectations: every claimed capability must have at least one test; unsupported optional capabilities must be explicitly marked unavailable rather than silently absent.

acceptance:
- [ ] agentlib.Request no longer has a Compactor field
- [ ] Optional CompactionOverride field (or equivalent) preserved for test-injection of fakes
- [ ] Agent loop constructs the compactor on demand from (Provider, Model) using the existing 4-step resolution
- [ ] cmd/ddx-agent/main.go simplified to drop the inline resolution
- [ ] No public-API regression for consumers that only pass Provider/Model (the common case)
- [ ] Migration note in CHANGELOG for the breaking Request shape change
- [ ] Released in a tagged ddx-agent version
description:
Architectural fix. Today the agent library accepts an injected Compactor on agentlib.Request, forcing every caller to construct one with a context-window-sized config before invoking the agent. This is the wrong boundary — the agent already knows the provider and the model, so it has everything it needs to construct the right compactor itself. Pushing this work to callers (a) duplicates the 4-step resolution recipe, (b) leaks the compaction package as part of the public API, (c) leaves room for callers (DDx today) to pass a stale or wrong config that silently underuses the model.

Observed downstream pain: DDx cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:148 calls compaction.NewCompactor(embeddedCompactionConfig(agentCfg.Model)), where embeddedCompactionConfig hardcodes ContextWindow=131072 with a TODO comment about wanting per-model lookup. Result: bragi serves qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b at 262144 context, DDx pretends it is 131072.

Fix: agent library constructs the compactor internally during request handling. Resolution chain (already inlined in cmd/ddx-agent/main.go:207-230) moves into the agent loop:
  1. agentlib.Request.CompactionOverride compaction.Config (optional, for tests)
  2. Else resolve from (Request.Provider config, Request.Model) using existing primitives:
     - explicit ProviderConfig.ContextWindow
     - oaiProvider.LookupModelLimits(...).ContextLength
     - catalog.ContextWindowForModel(model)
     - compaction.DefaultConfig() fallback

After this:
  - agentlib.Request.Compactor field is removed
  - cmd/ddx-agent/main.go drops its inline resolution
  - Downstream consumers (DDx) drop their compaction wiring entirely
  - The compaction package can be moved internal/ if it has no other public consumers

Cross-ref: ddx-76df1a46 in DDx is the downstream cleanup, blocked on this.
title: compaction: agent library owns compactor lifecycle — remove Compactor from Request

acceptance: ddx-agent usage exists, reads session logs, reports aggregated token/cost totals by provider/model, and is covered by tests for parsing and aggregation

acceptance: Session log analysis produces a clear characterization of the timeout cause (model capability ceiling vs. agent loop defect). If a loop defect is identified, a follow-on fix bead is filed with the specific loop condition to patch. cancel-async-tasks result in session log shows either: graceful give-up before timeout, or evidence of productive iteration toward solution.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for simplicity · Tests first</principles>
<concerns>benchmark | agent-loop</concerns>
<practices>Go · ddx-agent CLI · Harbor</practices>
<governing>SD-009 benchmark mode</governing>
</context-digest>

cancel-async-tasks times out in every ddx-agent run regardless of model:
- qwen3.5-27b: timeout in all 3 runs (t1/t2/t3), even with 2x timeout multiplier (30 min)
- claude-opus-4.6-fast: also FAILED this task in the 2026-04-10 evidence-grade baseline (8/15 run)

Task description: implement async run_tasks() with max concurrency and graceful SIGINT cleanup. Rated 'hard', expert time estimate 120-600 min. Agent timeout in task.toml is 900s.

The timeout may reflect either: (a) genuine model capability ceiling — the task requires subtle async/signal handling that current models get wrong and loop on; (b) ddx-agent loop issue where the agent keeps retrying a broken implementation without converging; (c) context overflow causing repeated compaction without progress.

Investigation steps:
1. Pull the session log from a timed-out cancel-async-tasks run (benchmark-results/harbor-jobs/cancel-async-tasks-*/sessions/*.jsonl) and analyze the loop pattern — is the agent making progress, looping on the same error, or hitting context overflow?
2. Check if the compaction/overflow recovery added in 20eba26 prevents runaway loops or makes them worse for this task type.
3. Determine if gpt-oss-120b or another model passes this task — if so, it's a model capability issue; if not, investigate loop termination.

Note: do NOT over-invest in making qwen3.5-27b pass this specific task. The primary value is understanding whether the agent loop is healthy (converges or gives up gracefully) vs. unhealthy (loops indefinitely on the same failure).

events.body: qwen3.5-27b generated 221,567 chars of pure reasoning tokens (48,206 llm.delta events) with zero content output and zero tool calls over 1800s. The model wrote the same Python code block 128 identical times in its reasoning chain but never committed to an answer or called any tool. Root cause: thinking-budget misconfiguration. The benchmark config.yaml uses type=openai-compat with model=qwen3.5-27b but sets no thinking_budget/thinking_level, so LM Studio serves the model with no reasoning cap. The picompat auto-configure path (which would auto-set thinking_level=medium for qwen3 models via isThinkingModel()) only runs when translating Claude Desktop config, not for direct config.yaml runs. loop.go is healthy: it made exactly one LLM call (iteration 0 of max_iterations=20) and the timeout hit at the provider level (Harbor asyncio.CancelledError) while awaiting the first llm.response that never arrived. No loop defect found; no follow-on fix bead warranted.
events.summary: Model runaway reasoning, not a loop defect

title: investigate: cancel-async-tasks timeout — loop analysis and model capability floor

acceptance:
- [ ] --max-cost-usd cap halts sweep when exceeded; per-task pre-check works
- [ ] temperature=0 + seed set on every supporting harness call; recorded in results
- [ ] Non-deterministic harnesses flagged in report
- [ ] Parity report tabulates tool-call sequences and final-string-similarity for human review
description:
Add safety + reproducibility controls to the integration suite (depends on agent-X scaffolding bead).

- --max-cost-usd <N> flag (default $0.50). Halts the sweep when accumulated cost exceeds cap. Per-task estimate checked against remaining budget pre-invocation; over-budget tasks skip with explicit "skipped: cost cap" reason.
- temperature=0 + per-task seed for harnesses/providers that support deterministic sampling. Seeds recorded alongside results so reruns are reproducible.
- Non-deterministic harnesses (no seed support) flagged in the report ("non-deterministic: {reason}"); their parity-comparison results treated as advisory only.
- Parity comparison method: tool-call sequence equality is primary signal — same sequence of (tool_name, normalized_args) = behaviorally equivalent. String-similarity (cosine on character n-grams) as secondary signal flagging "looks similar but not identical." NO automated semantic equivalence — humans review divergences.
- Future scope (out, file follow-up beads): embedding-similarity, LLM-as-judge.
title: integration suite: cost cap + determinism controls + parity comparison reporting

description:
Implement DdxAgent.Execute as the primary verb. Wraps existing agent loop. Internal-only details:

- Per-model context-window resolution using existing modelcatalog.ContextWindowForModel + provider /api lookup (supersedes agent-6f8caa00 and agent-8682793b — close those when this lands).
- StallPolicy enforcement: ReadOnlyTools + NoopCompactions limits end execution with Status=stalled.
- Three timeout knobs: Timeout (wall-clock), IdleTimeout (activity-quiet), ProviderTimeout (per-HTTP).
- Orphan-model validation: Model set + no provider has it + no catalog entry → Status=failed with explicit error.
- Provider-deadline wrapping: every HTTP call wrapped; transport/auth/upstream failures eligible for failover; prompt/tool errors not.
- Route-reason attribution: start-event routing_decision includes the reason string.
- OS-level subprocess cleanup: ctx.Done() reaps PTY/orphan processes (matters more for subprocess harnesses in Phase 3, but the hook lives here).
- Metadata is bidirectional: stamps every emitted event AND every session-log line.
- SessionLogDir per-request override.

Returns <-chan Event closed when execution terminates. Final event carries status, usage, cost, session log path, optional message history, routing_actual.
title: service: implement DdxAgent.Execute over native providers (with StallPolicy, three timeouts, contract behaviors)

acceptance:
1. Provider exposes SupportsTools(), SupportsStream(), SupportsStructuredOutput() — all bool, all cheap (no HTTP call).

2. Values reflect flavor + probe result + library's internal knowledge. E.g. a probe-detected openai-compat provider returns true for all three by default; omlx flavor sets them per the per-flavor knowledge already encoded in the request-assembly paths.

3. Unit tests cover at least: openai-compat (openrouter) all-true, omlx (per v0.3.11 claims), a hypothetical non-streaming provider returning false for SupportsStream.

4. CHANGELOG.md entry.

5. (Optional / nice to have) a GoDoc example on Provider that shows the recommended pattern: if !p.SupportsTools() { skip this provider for tool-using prompts }.
description:
## Context

The library already knows per-flavor what a provider endpoint can and cannot do: omlx does server-side tool-call translation, openrouter passes through, lmstudio historically has quirks with tools on certain models, etc. This knowledge lives implicitly in the flavor-specific request-assembly code paths and the tool-schema translation logic.

Downstream routing (DocumentDrivenDX/ddx ddx-4817edfd 'capability gating') needs to make pre-dispatch decisions: if the bead prompt uses tools and the routed provider doesn't support them, routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and fail mid-stream. Without a public capability API, DDx has to either re-derive the knowledge (duplication + drift risk) or ship with a hand-curated table that rots as flavors evolve.

Concrete failure scenario this enables a fix for: omlx v0.3.11 claims auto-translated tool calling in release notes, but we observed 'unexpected end of JSON input' under real execute-bead prompts with tools (DDx ddx-6a5dfe35). If omlx were in a state where a specific provider/model/prompt-shape doesn't actually support tools, a capability flag would let routing skip that combination instead of dispatch-and-fail.

## Ask

Expose capability flags on the resolved Provider that reflect what the library's internal knowledge + the probe results imply:

    func (p *Provider) SupportsTools() bool             // provider+flavor handles /v1/chat/completions with tools field
    func (p *Provider) SupportsStream() bool            // provider+flavor returns valid SSE for stream=true
    func (p *Provider) SupportsStructuredOutput() bool  // provider+flavor honors response_format json_object / tool-use-required

Returned values reflect the COMBINATION of (flavor, probe result, library knowledge). E.g. openrouter returns true for all three unconditionally. omlx returns true for SupportsStream based on the /v1/models/status response; SupportsTools is true when the server exposes the translation endpoint.

## Optional: per-model capability

Some flavors have per-model nuance (a model in the list may not support tools even if the flavor generally does). v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits already contacts the provider for per-model context_window / max_tokens. A follow-up could extend that to per-model capability flags. Out of scope for this bead's minimum — flavor-level flags are enough for the DDx Phase-2 scope — but note it in the bead for future expansion.

## Out of scope

- Per-model capability (see Optional above — tracked separately).
- Runtime mutation of capability flags based on observed errors (adaptive).
- User-overridable capability assertions (config.yaml 'force_supports_tools: true').
notes: Fresh-eyes review 2026-04-18: 'capability' is already used in cmd/ddx-agent/routing_smart.go (CapabilityWeight, CapabilityScore) for BENCHMARK capability (SWE-bench %). These protocol flags are a DIFFERENT axis. To avoid naming confusion: (a) file is provider/openai/protocol_support.go, not capabilities.go; (b) docstring says 'Protocol-level capabilities. Distinct from routing capability which is a benchmark quality score.' Also: consumes DetectedFlavor() from agent-92f0f324, so that lands first. Spec update required before code lands: FEAT-003 new requirement 24 (protocol-capability introspection); SD-005 new decision D13 (protocol capabilities are flavor-keyed and conservative — unknown flavors return false). Do NOT pre-emptively set omlx SupportsTools=false; no wire evidence yet per ddx-6a5dfe35. Honest v1: true for all claimed-supporting flavors, revisit after evidence.
title: expose per-flavor capability flags (SupportsTools, SupportsStream, SupportsStructuredOutput)

acceptance:
1. A test-only package such as internal/ptytest exposes scenario loading, cassette replay execution, virtual-clock control, and assertion evaluation without being imported by production code.
2. Scenario specs can select cassette path, expected manifest.id, expected manifest.content_digest, replay mode, terminal size, environment policy, expected artifacts, timestamp resolution expectations, and named assertion groups.
3. Assertions cover at least: frame contains/never/eventually/stable_for predicates, cursor/size/style facts, raw output chunk ordering and t_ms/seq placement, input bytes and paste boundaries, resize ordering, service-event JSON predicates, final metadata, exit status, timeout/cancel status, and timing gap checks.
4. Collapsed replay uses a virtual clock so time-coded assertions run at the recorded logical t_ms positions while preserving seq ordering and running fast in default tests.
5. Realtime and scaled replay are available and tested for scheduler behavior within one timestamp resolution tick.
6. Parallel replay is tested: multiple cassette scenarios run concurrently with read-only fixture inputs, unique temp dirs, isolated HOME/config roots where applicable, no global tmux/session state, no artifact path collisions, and deterministic cleanup.
7. Synthetic fixture cassettes cover weird terminal behavior: partial ANSI/VT escapes, one-byte chunking, alternate screen, cursor addressing, screen clear, SGR style changes, OSC title, OSC 8 hyperlinks, OSC 52 clipboard writes, bell, DECRQM/mode-query responses, line-drawing/alternate character sets, bracketed paste, SGR mouse 1006, focus-in/out, Unicode wide/combining characters, resize during output, resize during an escape sequence, rapid redraw/spinner frames, delayed output, no-newline prompt, PTY backpressure or large buffered output, final output burst at process exit, EOF during redraw, cancellation, and timeout.
8. Assertion specs fail fast when manifest.id or manifest.content_digest does not match the cassette they are paired with.
9. Failure output includes the failed assertion, expected/actual values, nearest frame timestamps, relevant screen excerpt, and service-event context.
10. go test ./... passes, and the framework tests run without credentials, Docker, network, or manual input.
description:
Implement the automated PTY cassette assertion framework required by ADR-002. The framework is test-only infrastructure on top of internal/pty/session, internal/pty/terminal, and internal/pty/cassette. It must let tests replay PTY-level cassettes in collapsed time, scaled time, or realtime; run time-coded assertions against frames, raw output chunks, input events, resize events, service events, final metadata, exit status, and timing gaps; and execute safely in parallel without credentials or provider access.

This is the shared test surface for the first scenarios: Docker top conformance, Claude quota/model/reasoning/token/prompt cassettes, and Codex quota/model/reasoning/token/prompt cassettes. Future weird rendering cases must be added by writing a scenario fixture plus assertion spec, not by adding one-off sleeps or manual inspection.

In-scope files:
- internal/ptytest or equivalent test-only package
- internal/pty/cassette test helpers as needed
- internal/pty/terminal synthetic fixture tests as needed
- docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-002-pty-cassette-transport.md only if implementation reveals a necessary clarification

Out-of-scope:
- Production harness adapter behavior
- Live authenticated cassette content for Claude/Codex beyond deterministic synthetic fixtures
- Any terminal UI, manual viewer, or tmux dependency
title: harness-pty: implement cassette assertion test framework

description:
Add the final parity and encapsulation check for the external API migration. DDx and other consumers should use public service APIs and CLI surfaces, while agent internals stay encapsulated. This bead guards against future entanglement where the CLI exposes a feature by reading internal structs or consumers mirror private payloads again.

The parity check should cover the service and CLI surfaces that emerge from the child beads: supported capabilities, typed execute/drain output, profile projection, route-attempt/status reporting, quota/provider health, and explicit test-only harness dispatch where appropriate. The CLI does not need to expose every low-level method, but any overlapping user-visible command must report the same normalized semantics as the service API.

This is a test/audit bead, not a broad refactor. File separate follow-up beads for any discovered command gaps that cannot be fixed narrowly.

acceptance: Unit test constructs an agent loop scenario where the mock provider returns the same tool call 4 times in a row and asserts the loop exits with ErrToolCallLoop after 3 identical turns. A second test asserts the counter resets when a different tool call appears mid-sequence. Loop exits with StatusError and code 0 in benchmark mode.
description:
The agent loop has no detection for iteration-level loops where the model repeatedly calls the same tool with the same arguments without making progress. Observed pattern: agent calls bash('go test ./...'), gets a compile error, calls bash('go test ./...') again unchanged, same error, repeats up to max_iterations.

Implementation: after each tool call round-trip, hash (tool_name, normalized_tool_args) for each call in the turn. Track consecutive turns where the full set of tool calls is identical to the previous turn. After N identical consecutive turns (default 3), abort the loop with a sentinel error (ErrToolCallLoop or similar) and exit non-retryably.

Reset the counter when any turn produces a different tool call set.

Files: loop.go (tool call dispatch and iteration), errors.go (sentinel). The hash/comparison should be cheap — a simple string concat or encoding/json marshal of the call list is fine.
notes: Verified shipped in v0.3.1: loop.go lines 117/703/710/713/717 (toolCallLoopLimit=3, toolCallFingerprint, ErrToolCallLoop wiring); tested in loop_test.go:2031 (TestRun_ToolCallLoopDetection). Closing per AR-2026-04-12 agent-39586e24.
title: fix: detect and abort identical consecutive tool-call loops

acceptance:
1. Explicit Service.Execute requests with Harness=virtual run through the service boundary using a documented fixture or dictionary input, emit the normal event sequence, and populate the typed/drained final result.
2. Explicit Service.Execute requests with Harness=script run a pinned deterministic script or script definition through the service boundary and emit the normal event sequence.
3. ResolveRoute and profile routing never choose virtual or script unless the caller explicitly pins that harness.
4. Tests cover one virtual replay and one script invocation end to end through Service.Execute; these tests do not rely on ad-hoc fake claude/codex/opencode/pi binaries.
5. CONTRACT-003 documents virtual/script as explicit test-only harnesses and defines their request inputs.
6. go test . ./internal/routing ./internal/provider/virtual passes.
description:
Surfaced by DDx consumer review. DDx's RunViaService special-cases harness="virtual" and harness="script" and routes them back to a private DDx Runner. The reason: service-level dispatch for those harnesses is incomplete, so the service registry lists them but Service.Execute can't drive them.

This keeps a Runner alive in DDx purely for test-only harnesses. As long as that persists, the thin-consumer epic (ddx-ac5c7fdb) can't delete DDx's Runner and associated files (state.go, registry.go, routing.go, compare_adapter.go, claude_stream.go, virtual.go, script.go).

## Proposal

Service.Execute handles explicit Harness="virtual" and Harness="script" with deterministic test seams (or documented public behavior). Profile routing continues to exclude them unless explicitly pinned (they're test-only; we don't want ResolveRoute to ever land on them by accident).

The virtual harness is already the replay/fixture harness — it should be addressable via Service.Execute with a session-id / fixture-path input. The script harness runs a pinned deterministic script — same shape.

## Consumer impact

Unblocks DDx Phase 5 deletes: ddx-c0f6b19c (Runner internals tests), ddx-f0cab7b3 (state/registry.go), ddx-4e3e149f (Phase 5h Runner-method files), ddx-1cc9933c (Phase 5i file-reading helpers). DDx can delete Runner entirely.
notes: Consolidated plan order 04. This is test-substrate work, not production routing. It follows the capability matrix and typed drain so its tests can assert the same public output shape real harnesses use. Do not implement this with throwaway fake binaries. Use the existing internal/provider/virtual dictionary semantics where possible and a reusable script harness contract for deterministic command-driven tests.

acceptance: Running ddx-agent --json --preset benchmark on a task that produces correct output exits with code 0. Harbor no longer records NonZeroAgentExitCodeError for solved tasks. Verified by re-running build-pmars and code-from-image tasks and observing n_errors=0 in result.json alongside score=1.0.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Tests first · Design for simplicity</principles>
<concerns>benchmark | harness</concerns>
<practices>Go · ddx-agent CLI</practices>
<governing>SD-009 benchmark mode</governing>
</context-digest>

When ddx-agent completes a task successfully (Harbor verifier scores it 1.0), the process still exits with a non-zero exit code. Harbor records this as NonZeroAgentExitCodeError and counts it as an error in n_errors, even though the reward is 1.0.

Observed in benchmark run 20260412T172048Z:
- build-pmars: score=1.0, NonZeroAgentExitCodeError
- code-from-image: score=1.0, NonZeroAgentExitCodeError

Root cause to investigate: the agent likely exits non-zero when the session ends with no more iterations (ErrNoMoreAttempts or similar) or when the --json flag causes the process to exit with a status reflecting internal state rather than task outcome. In benchmark mode the exit code should be 0 on clean completion regardless of whether the model hit iteration limits.

Files to check: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go exit path, loop.go termination conditions. The fix is likely setting exit code 0 when the agent loop ends cleanly (exhausted iterations or received done signal) vs. exits on an unrecoverable error.
title: fix: agent exits non-zero after successful task completion in benchmark harness

acceptance: (1) When a model has context_window set in models.yaml, the compaction config uses that value instead of DefaultContextWindow. (2) The CLI main.go and embedded-agent paths both resolve context_window from the catalog when LookupModelLimits returns 0. (3) LM Studio models with no /v1/models context_length field get the catalog value, not the default. (4) go test ./compaction ./modelcatalog ./cmd/ddx-agent -count=1 passes with at least one test verifying catalog-driven context window flows to compaction config.
description:
## Problem

When LM Studio does not expose context_length in /v1/models (it only returns {id, object, owned_by}), the agent falls back to DefaultContextWindow. Before v0.3.9 that was 8192; now it is 131072. But models like qwen3-coder-next and qwen3.5-27b have 262144 — the compaction fires at 124k (95% of 131k) instead of ~249k.

The v4 catalog manifest already has context_window per model (added in v0.3.9 for qwen models). The standalone CLI (cmd/ddx-agent/main.go) does use LookupModelLimits which queries the API, but when that returns 0 it should fall back to the catalog entry rather than the hardcoded default.

## Fix

1. In cmd/ddx-agent/main.go after LookupModelLimits returns 0, look up the model in the catalog and use its context_window.
2. Export a helper (e.g. modelcatalog.ContextWindowForModel) that the DDx embedded runner can also call.
3. DDx will consume the helper in embeddedCompactionConfig() to get accurate per-model windows.

## Files to touch
- modelcatalog/catalog.go — add ContextWindowForModel(modelID string) int helper
- cmd/ddx-agent/main.go — fall back to catalog context_window when API returns 0
- compaction/config.go — no changes (default is already 131k)
- modelcatalog/catalog_test.go — test ContextWindowForModel returns correct value for qwen3.5-27b

## Review linkage
Discovered / re-confirmed in AR-2026-04-17 alignment pass (agent-bad5ec4e). CLI currently falls through from LookupModelLimits→0 directly to DefaultContextWindow=131072 without checking catalog.
title: Wire per-model context_window from v4 catalog into compaction config

acceptance: Prompt construction test confirms stable prefix is byte-identical across repeated calls with same context; no timestamps or dynamic fields in system prompt block
description:
Cache efficiency requires that stable content (system prompt, persona, tool definitions, file context) comes before variable content (user message, current task). Any change to early tokens invalidates all downstream cache entries.

Audit prompt construction in ddx-agent and enforce ordering rules:
1. System prompt / persona — must be deterministic, no dynamic fields (timestamps, file lists, git hashes in this block)
2. Tool definitions — stable, comes after system prompt
3. Persistent file context — ordered consistently (e.g. alphabetically) within a session
4. Conversation history — append-only, never reorder
5. Current user turn — always last

Add a test that verifies the same session produces byte-identical prefix across multiple calls.
title: Enforce stable prefix ordering in prompt construction
