acceptance: Unit test feeds a stream of pure reasoning_content deltas past the threshold and asserts the stream read returns the sentinel error. The agent loop treats the error as non-retryable and exits cleanly (exit code 0 in benchmark mode). Both the byte-threshold and stall-timer paths are exercised.
description:
When a thinking model generates reasoning tokens without ever producing output content or tool calls, the agent hangs until the external timeout (Harbor's asyncio.wait_for) fires. Observed in cancel-async-tasks: qwen3.5-27b emitted 221k reasoning chars over 1800s, rewrote the same solution 128 times, and never finished.

The agent loop should detect this condition and abort the stream early rather than waiting for the provider or harness to kill it.

Detection heuristics (implement at least one, preferably both):
1. Reasoning-token threshold: if accumulated reasoning content exceeds a configurable limit (default ~32k chars) with no content or tool_call delta yet seen, cancel the stream and return a sentinel error (ErrReasoningOverflow or similar).
2. Reasoning-stall timer: if only reasoning_content deltas arrive for longer than a configurable duration (default 120s) with no content or tool_call delta, cancel the stream.

The agent loop should treat ErrReasoningOverflow as a non-retryable loop-termination condition — log a warning and stop iterating rather than retrying.

Files likely involved: stream.go or stream_consume.go (stream reading), loop.go (error handling).
notes: Verified shipped in v0.3.1: stream_consume.go (ErrReasoningOverflow/Stall, reasoningByteLimit, reasoningStallTimeout); tested in stream_consume_test.go:483/530/507/597. Closing per AR-2026-04-12 agent-39586e24.
title: fix: detect runaway reasoning loop and abort stream early

acceptance: go test ./... passes; CompactionConfig gains UserMessageTailTokens field (default 20000); new test TestCompact_UserMessageTailReInclusion exists and verifies that after compaction the replacement history contains both the summary message and the most recent real user messages up to the token budget, in chronological order
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for Change · Design for Simplicity · Validate Your Work · Make Intent Explicit · Prefer Reversible Decisions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>gofmt · go vet · golangci-lint · gosec · govulncheck · go test · go test -race · testify/assert for assertions</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 — OTel GenAI spans and JSONL session log are separate observability surfaces</adrs>
<governing>SD-006 §Summary Injection — the compaction summary replaces all compacted messages. SD-006 §Codex research — keeps up to 20K tokens of recent user messages alongside summary. FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-06 — compaction no-fit paths must fail closed.</governing>
</context-digest>

Currently compaction/compaction.go replaces compacted history with only the summary message. Codex retains the most recent real user messages (up to a token budget) alongside the summary so the model retains actual request history.

What to change in compaction/compaction.go (or the Compactor function):
- After generating the summary, collect real user-role messages from the tail of the pre-compaction history (exclude system prefix messages, previous compaction summaries, and tool result messages — only real user messages).
- Accumulate messages from newest to oldest until a configurable token budget is exhausted (default: 20000 tokens, chars/4 heuristic). Per SD-006 §Overlong individual user messages, truncate individual messages that exceed the budget rather than dropping them.
- The replacement history becomes: [summary_message, ...tail_user_messages] ordered so the summary is first and user messages follow in chronological order.
- Expose the budget as a new field UserMessageTailTokens int in CompactionConfig (default 20000; 0 = disabled).
- All existing compaction tests must still pass.
- Add test TestCompact_UserMessageTailReInclusion verifying tail messages appear in replacement history.

Governing specs: SD-006, FEAT-001.
title: feat: compaction user-message tail re-inclusion after compact

acceptance:
1. Bash output filtering is configurable and defaults to current behavior unless explicitly enabled.
2. RTK mode uses an allowlisted rewrite/proxy path for at least `git status` and `go test` and is covered by tests with a fake rtk binary.
3. Missing/unusable RTK falls back to current raw bash execution with a clear marker and without changing command exit-code semantics.
4. Exit code, stderr, timeout/cancellation behavior, and nonzero-command handling are preserved.
5. Oversized filtered and fallback stdout/stderr remain bounded with clear filtered/truncated markers and raw-output recovery text if a raw-output path is implemented.
6. Documentation states mode, tradeoffs, and that built-in read/find/grep tools are not automatically RTK-filtered.
7. go test ./internal/tool ./cmd/ddx-agent ./... passes.
description:
Implement the bash output filtering slice from docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-04-19-provider-routing-tool-output.md.

Filtering is opt-in and scoped to the bash tool boundary. Built-in read/find/grep/ls-style tools are not filtered in this slice. RTK mode may use an installed `rtk` binary for allowlisted commands such as `git status` and `go test`; missing or failing RTK falls back to current bash behavior with a marker. Agent does not install RTK, run `rtk init`, collect RTK analytics, or mutate shell configuration.
title: tool: add optional RTK-style bash output filtering

acceptance:
1. `ddx-agent import pi` accepts the current Pi `models.json` shape where `providers` is an object map keyed by provider name.
2. Backward compatibility with the older array-based `providers` shape is preserved or explicitly rejected with a deterministic migration error if true compatibility is not feasible.
3. Import continues to translate Pi auth, provider URLs, model IDs, and default provider/model settings into valid `ddx-agent` config.
4. Regression tests cover both Pi schema variants and prove `import pi --diff` and normal import behavior work against realistic fixtures.
description:
`ddx-agent import pi` is stale against the current Pi config layout. Today the importer fails with:

`reading pi models.json: json: cannot unmarshal object into Go struct field ModelsConfig.providers of type []picompat.ProviderDefinition`

Current Pi stores `~/.pi/agent/models.json` with `providers` as an object map keyed by provider name, while `picompat/models.go` still expects the older array form. That makes the import surface unusable for real Pi installs even though the command still advertises `pi` as a supported source.

Update `picompat` and the import CLI to accept the current Pi schema while preserving compatibility with the older format if it still exists in the field. The importer should continue translating provider auth, base URLs, models, and defaults into `ddx-agent` config without requiring manual edits.
title: Fix pi import for current models.json schema

acceptance: (1) Compaction loop bounded with a structural stop. (2) Native structured error signals stuck state to callers. (3) Regression test reproduces the stuck state via seeded read-only tool calls. (4) DDx repo can remove the caller-side circuit breaker once this upstream fix is consumed.
description:
The compaction package can enter a stuck state where compaction.end events fire with no_compaction=true and success=false repeatedly, pulsing the inactivity timer on every iteration so the caller's timeout never fires. Downstream consumer DDx ran into this mid-session on a production worker: ~8461 stuck-compaction events accumulated before the hosting process had to be killed.

## Evidence

DDx-side session log:
/Users/erik/Projects/axon/.ddx/executions/20260415T005652-ebe7a16a/embedded/agent-1776214612561376672.jsonl (33 MB)
Parent process killed at PID 4141811 after wedging for hours on a completed task.

## DDx-side workaround (defense in depth, does not fix the root cause)

Commit 604151773f9b905346ee95b051e024a02f850cf0 in ddx repo (bead ddx-25a41f51) wraps the embedded agent call with two guards:
1. compaction.start and compaction.end(no_compaction=true) events no longer pulse the caller's inactivity timer — only real-progress events (LLM calls, tool results, commits) extend the timeout.
2. Circuit breaker counts consecutive compaction.end(success=false, no_compaction=true) events and aborts with a structured compaction_stuck error after a configurable threshold (default 50).

The workaround catches the stuck state at the DDx/caller layer. The native fix belongs in github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent so every ddx-agent user (not just DDx CLI) benefits.

## Requested upstream fix

- Compaction driver detects its own stuck state via the same heuristic (consecutive success=false/no_compaction=true events) and returns a structured error to the caller
- Eliminates the need for the caller to interpret compaction events as activity signals
- Exposes a clean API signal so consumers can decide whether to retry, escalate, or abort
- Regression test seeds the stuck state and verifies the driver bails instead of looping

## Scope

- Bounded attempt counter inside the compaction loop (not just the caller)
- Structured error: compaction.ErrStuck or similar
- Integration test using a deterministic seed that reproduces the 33MB jsonl pattern

## Relationship to DDx

When this lands upstream, DDx can remove its circuit-breaker workaround and rely on the native error. Until then, DDx keeps both layers as belt-and-suspenders.
resolution: fixed: bounded compaction loop with ErrCompactionStuck sentinel
title: bug: compaction loop can enter stuck state — no_compaction=true repeating without bound

acceptance:
1. All child beads in this epic are closed with implementation evidence.
2. CONTRACT-003 documents capabilities, final text, typed event/drain surface, real subprocess dispatch, virtual/script explicit execution, profile projection, route-attempt feedback, health/quota status semantics, golden-master record/replay behavior, and CLI/API parity boundaries.
3. go test ./... passes after the child beads land.
4. go test -tags=integration ./... passes in replay mode without live credentials after the integration bead lands.
5. A DDx-style consumer can execute, drain, inspect capabilities/routing/profile/quota status, parse final reviewer text, and run replay integration checks using only public service APIs.
description:
Consolidating epic for the 2026-04-20 service-contract and harness-migration feature request batch. The goal is to make agent the owner of harness streams, event payloads, model/profile catalog projection, supported-capability reporting, deterministic and live/replay harness testing, route-attempt feedback, and quota/provider health reporting so DDx and other consumers stop parsing raw harness frames, provider-native files, or agent-owned YAML.

Execution order:
0. Define and expose the supported-capabilities matrix for every harness.
0a. Decide the PTY/cassette transport architecture for live TUI record/replay.
1. Normalize final response text at the final event boundary.
2. Add typed public event payloads or a drain/decoder result API.
3. Wire pi, opencode, and gemini through Service.Execute.
4. Wire explicit virtual and script execution through Service.Execute as the deterministic service-level test substrate.
5. Expose catalog profiles and profile aliases through the service API.
6. Add route-attempt feedback and make ResolveRoute/RouteStatus react to active failures.
7. Expose the larger health/quota/provider-endpoint status surface once profile, route-attempt, capability, and cassette decisions exist.
8. Build golden-master integration tests for claude, codex, pi, and opencode using record mode plus deterministic replay.
9. Add CLI/API parity and encapsulation tests so external surfaces stay synced and internals remain private.

Scope rules:
- Do not add consumer-side parsing of raw codex, claude, pi, opencode, gemini, provider config, or quota files.
- Do not count ad-hoc fake binaries as harness integration evidence except for low-level parser/process infrastructure.
- Deterministic tests should use reusable virtual/script harness behavior or recorded live cassettes with explicit record/replay semantics.
- Live record mode must fail fast when credentials, binaries, transport dependencies, model config, subscription, or quota are missing.
- Replay mode must run without live credentials and produce an identical interaction sequence.
- CONTRACT-003 must remain the source of truth for public service behavior.
title: service: consolidate public harness API and integration-test surface

acceptance:
1. Public API exposes either typed payload structs plus decode helpers for every ServiceEvent kind, or a DrainExecuteResult helper that returns final status, final text, usage, cost, routing actual, tool calls/results, session log path, and terminal error state.
2. The API covers final, text_delta, tool_call, tool_result, routing_decision, stall, and compaction event payloads without consumers redefining agent-internal shapes.
3. At least one test demonstrates draining a Service.Execute event stream into the typed result including normalized final text from agent-32e8ff5e.
4. CONTRACT-003 documents the typed event/drain surface and includes a before/after migration example for DDx-style consumers.
5. go test . passes.
6. go test ./... passes.
description:
Surfaced by DDx consumer review. ServiceEvent.Data is currently raw JSON: consumers have to redefine the payload types (final, tool-call, routing-actual) locally to decode drained events. In DDx that shows up as cli/internal/agent/agent_runner_service.go mirroring agent-internal shapes — serviceFinalData, serviceToolCallData, serviceRoutingActual — just to read fields the service already knows about.

This keeps stream-adjacent knowledge in every consumer. Even after agent-32e8ff5e normalizes final text, consumers still have to parse their own copies of event data structs to get usage, cost, routing-actual, tool-call sequence, and session-log paths.

## Proposal

Expose stable public payload types for each ServiceEvent.Kind, or a higher-level DrainExecuteResult / decoder API that returns:
- final status (success/stalled/timed_out/cancelled/error)
- usage (input/output/total tokens)
- cost USD
- routing actual (provider, model, harness as finally chosen)
- tool call sequence
- session log path
- normalized final text (paired with agent-32e8ff5e)

Pick whichever fits the existing CONTRACT-003 shape — typed events vs a drain helper. Either removes the need for consumers to know agent event data shapes.

## Consumer impact

Shrinks DDx ddx-ac5c7fdb (thin-consumer epic), ddx-4e3e149f (Phase 5h), ddx-f7ae036f clause (1) to pure consumer wiring.
notes: Consolidated plan order 02. Depends on agent-32e8ff5e so normalized final text is part of the typed result from day one. Prefer a small public decoder/drain helper over widening every consumer if that best matches the existing channel contract. The implementation must make DDx-side payload shadow structs unnecessary for final, tool_call, tool_result, routing_decision, and routing_actual/final routing fields.
title: service: expose typed final/tool/routing event payloads from Service.Execute

acceptance:
1. Root cause of the SDK↔omlx SSE truncation identified via packet-level evidence or instrumented transport logs — not just 'probably a timeout'.

2. Fix landed in a v0.3.14+ release. What the fix looks like depends on the root cause:
   - If idle/read timeout: document the timeout default and expose a knob, OR extend timeout to handle reasoning warmup.
   - If HTTP/2 interaction: force HTTP/1.1 for omlx flavor, or fix the HTTP/2 handling.
   - If decoder buffering: fix the decoder.

3. Regression test: against a test double that simulates an omlx-style stream with a 10+ second silence between the role frame and the first content frame, the SDK correctly consumes the full stream without surfacing 'unexpected end of JSON input'.

4. AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE extended to capture the full stream (not just first batch) so the next integration defect of this shape is trivially diagnosable.
description:
## Summary

After agent-04639431 landed in v0.3.13 (stripped 'thinking' field for non-Anthropic flavors), 'ddx work --provider vidar-omlx' still fails with 'unexpected end of JSON input' in the same symptom shape. The v0.3.13 fix is correct — wire capture confirms 'thinking' is no longer in the request body — but the root cause of the parse failure is deeper.

## Tightened wire evidence

Captured 2026-04-18 on v0.3.13 via AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE=1:

Request body (33,426 bytes, top-level keys: messages, model, stream, stream_options, tools):
- model: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit
- stream: true
- stream_options: {include_usage: true}
- NO 'thinking' field (v0.3.13 strip working)
- NO 'system' top-level (uses messages[].role=system)
- 7 tools with valid JSON Schema shapes

Response (captured by AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE):
- HTTP 200, Content-Type: text/event-stream; charset=utf-8
- Body captured: 186 bytes
  - : keep-alive\n\n
  - data: {...choices:[{delta:{role:"assistant"}}]}\n\n
- Then AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE capture stops; SDK parser surfaces 'unexpected end of JSON input'

## The key finding: same body works via curl

Piping the exact same 33,426-byte request body via curl to the same endpoint returns a valid, continuously-streaming SSE response:

    curl -sS -N -X POST http://vidar:1235/v1/chat/completions \
      -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
      -H 'Accept: application/json' \
      -d @req.json

    → role delta → reasoning_content stream → (continues generating)

The server takes ~9 seconds between 'role:assistant' frame and first 'reasoning_content' frame (model warmup). During this silence the server emits ': keep-alive' SSE comment frames periodically.

So the wire-level body is fine. The server is fine. The difference is entirely in how the openai-go SDK (wrapped by ddx-agent) consumes the stream.

## Suspected root causes (ranked)

1. **The 9-second reasoning-warmup silence exceeds an openai-go client-side idle/read timeout.** The SDK may be closing or aborting the underlying HTTP connection during the server's reasoning phase, before generation tokens arrive. ': keep-alive' SSE comment frames are supposed to prevent this — the SDK may not be resetting its idle timer on comment frames.

2. **HTTP/2 connection negotiation differs from curl's HTTP/1.1 default.** omlx server (uvicorn per response headers) may serve SSE differently under HTTP/2 multiplexing vs HTTP/1.1. openai-go client likely enables HTTP/2 by default.

3. **Buffer/goroutine interaction in the SDK's streaming decoder.** The decoder's internal buffering may drop frames under specific arrival-time patterns (long silence, then burst).

4. **AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE may be misleading.** The 186-byte response capture may represent only the first response batch read by the debug middleware, with subsequent frames going to the SDK parser unobserved. If so, the parse failure occurs later in the stream, not at the 186-byte point. Either way, the result is the same: the SDK parser surfaces 'unexpected end of JSON input' when curl against the same server does not.

## Reproduction

- Host: eitri, DDx binary v0.6.0-alpha22-15 (commit 9d87594b, agent-lib v0.3.13)
- Provider: vidar-omlx (http://vidar:1235/v1)
- Model: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit (a reasoning model — 9s warmup is typical)
- Command: ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit --max-tier cheap

Wire evidence: /tmp/vidar-omlx-wire2.jsonl on eitri.

## Suggested investigation steps

1. Add a packet-level capture (tcpdump on port 1235) during a failing ddx-agent run AND a working curl run with identical body, compare.

2. Instrument openai-go client transport to log HTTP connection-level events (idle timeout, connection close, context cancel).

3. Test with an explicit http.Transport that disables HTTP/2 (ForceAttemptHTTP2: false) and see if that changes behavior.

4. Test with an artificially slow reasoning phase on a simpler omlx model to reproduce the silence-triggered cutoff deterministically.

5. Extend AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE to capture the ENTIRE stream (not just the first batch) so the exact client-side truncation point is visible in the evidence bundle.

## Related

- agent-04639431 (v0.3.13): 'thinking' field strip — correct but orthogonal. This bead is what ddx-6a5dfe35 should have classified AFTER thinking was ruled out.
- agent-941e7e42 (v0.3.12): AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE — needs extension per step 5 above.
- DocumentDrivenDX/ddx ddx-6a5dfe35: consumer bead; now blocked on THIS bead, not on agent-04639431 alone.
notes:
Root cause confirmed: openai-go ssestream decoder dispatches an event on ANY blank line — including the blank line that terminates an SSE comment-only frame like `: keep-alive\n\n`. Stream.Next then calls `json.Unmarshal([]byte{}, &chunk)` which surfaces "unexpected end of JSON input". This is a spec-violating defect in the SDK decoder (WHATWG SSE requires empty-data events to be silently ignored).

Importantly, this has nothing to do with the 9-second reasoning-warmup timeout or HTTP/2 theories. The regression test in provider/openai/openai_test.go reproduces the exact symptom with a 10ms inter-frame sleep — the decoder fails on the first keep-alive frame regardless of subsequent timing.

Fix: `provider/openai/sse_filter.go` adds `sseCommentFilter`, wired in via `sseFilterMiddleware()` in the openai-compat Provider constructor. It strips comment lines AND suppresses blank-line dispatches when the current frame has not yet seen any field line, so the decoder never observes an empty-event dispatch.

AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE extended (step 5 of acceptance): new `AGENT_DEBUG_WIRE_STREAM_FULL=1` opt-in disables the 64KB cumulative cap on teeBody so the entire stream is captured. Default behavior unchanged.

Regression test: `TestChatStream_SurvivesSSECommentFramesAndLongSilence` in provider/openai/openai_test.go fails before the fix ("unexpected end of JSON input") and passes after.

Follow-ups filed:
- agent-8e0dcca1: per-provider conformance test suite so the next flavor-specific wire defect is caught at flavor-introduction time rather than in the field.

acceptance:
1. `go test . ./internal/harnesses/codex -run 'Codex|Quota|Refresh|ResolveRoute|TokenCount' -count=1` passes.
2. Tests prove refresh order: live DDx-owned token_count evidence when available, then fresh bounded session evidence, then PTY fallback for stale/absent/incomplete session evidence.
3. Tests prove session-derived CapturedAt is not set to now and stale session evidence cannot suppress PTY refresh.
4. Tests prove ResolveRoute remains foreground cache-only and nonblocking while refresh runs asynchronously.
5. Tests prove non-subsidized/API-key-only account metadata remains ineligible for automatic Codex routing.
6. `go test ./...` passes.
description:
Wire fresh Codex token_count rate-limit evidence into the existing service-owned quota refresh path. Current refresh flow checks Codex quota cache and falls back to interactive PTY /status scraping. After the session token_count reader exists, refresh should prefer fresh session-derived rate_limits before spawning a PTY probe, while keeping foreground routing cache-only.

Required behavior:
- In Codex quota refresh, try fresh bounded session token_count evidence first. If it produces a complete fresh quota snapshot with account/plan metadata, write the durable Codex quota cache and do not spawn PTY.
- If session evidence is absent, stale, malformed, incomplete, or non-subsidized, fall back to the existing PTY /status probe where appropriate.
- Freshness must align with existing Codex quota policy: currently 15 minutes. Do not introduce a second 30m policy.
- CapturedAt for session-derived cache entries must come from token_count event timestamp or file mtime fallback, never from now.
- Keep ResolveRoute foreground cache-only. Refresh may start asynchronously through existing debounce/startup/timer paths, but route resolution must not synchronously scan sessions or probe PTY.
- If the codex exec stream parser exposes live token_count.rate_limits from a DDx-owned run, update the quota cache from that evidence after execution when safe to do so. Treat live DDx-owned evidence as fresher than private session logs.
- Preserve existing auto-routing eligibility gates: fresh quota, complete windows, subsidized ChatGPT plan, and no blocked/exhausted window.

In-scope files:
- service_providers.go and service_providers_test.go
- service_routing.go only if tests need to assert nonblocking behavior
- internal/harnesses/codex/quota_cache.go/tests if source metadata or helper methods are needed
- internal/harnesses/codex/runner.go only for live token_count cache update after execution

Out-of-scope:
- Changing non-Codex refresh behavior.
- Synchronous live probes inside ResolveRoute.
- Populating HarnessInfo.UsageWindows from private Codex session files.
title: service: prefer fresh Codex token_count quota evidence before PTY

acceptance: go test ./... passes; isTransientError correctly classifies socket hang up, 429, 503, connection refused as transient and auth errors as fatal; loop retries transient errors with backoff and fails fast on fatal errors; go vet ./... clean
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Design for simplicity · No speculative abstractions</principles>
<concerns>go-std | testing</concerns>
<practices>Go stdlib · testify/assert</practices>
<governing>SD-001 §agent loop — provider retry; SD-004 §streaming; SD-005 §provider config</governing>
</context-digest>

Replace the generic 3-attempt retry in the agent loop with pattern-matched transient error detection and proper exponential backoff.

**Problem**: ddx-agent treats all provider errors identically. Local LLM servers (LMStudio, Ollama) commonly produce transient errors — socket hangs, connection resets, brief server restarts — that should be retried. Currently these look identical to fatal errors and the agent stops.

**Reference implementation**: pi matches errors against a large regex covering: `overloaded|rate.*limit|too many requests|429|500|502|503|504|service.*unavailable|server.*error|internal.*error|network.*error|connection.*error|connection.*refused|other side closed|fetch failed|socket hang up|ended without|timed? out|timeout|terminated`

**Files to change**:
- `loop.go` — replace current retry block (around line 377) with pattern-matched retry
- Add `provider_error.go` (or inline in loop.go) with `isTransientError(err error) bool` using regexp

**Behaviour**:
- Transient errors: retry up to 5 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s)
- Fatal errors (auth, bad request, model not found): fail immediately, no retry
- Log retry attempt and reason at each backoff
title: Transient error retry patterns for provider calls

description:
After DDx migration completes (DDx tracker epic blocks this), physically move every package that should not be public into internal/:

- compaction/ → internal/compaction/
- prompt/ → internal/prompt/
- tool/ → internal/tool/
- session/ → internal/session/
- observations/ → internal/observations/
- modelcatalog/ → internal/modelcatalog/

- provider/anthropic/ → internal/provider/anthropic/
- provider/virtual/ → internal/provider/virtual/
- config/ → keep public subset (Options-relevant types only); move rest to internal/

Go compiler then blocks all external imports of these. Any consumer still depending on them surfaces loudly. This is the v0.5.0 trigger — paired with agent-X (tag v0.5.0).

After the move: go build ./... must pass; lints prove no cross-package paths violate internal/.
notes:
PRECONDITION discovered post agent-99cb5545 (commit f4b4967) + ddx-996f77d7 (commit d328a6af): the ConfigPath loader is registered via init() in cli/config/serviceconfig.go. DDx triggers it via blank import of agent/config (cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go:11). When config moves under internal/, DDx can no longer blank-import it; init() never fires; service.New(ConfigPath) silently falls back to no-loader.

FIX as part of this bead: add a blank import of internal/config to a public agent file (e.g. agent.go or service.go) so the init() registration always runs whenever agent is linked. After that, DDx can drop its blank-import workaround.

Two-step:
1. Move config/, compaction/, prompt/, tool/, session/, observations/, modelcatalog/, provider/openai/, provider/anthropic/, provider/virtual/ to internal/
2. Add `_ "github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent/internal/config"` to a public agent file so init runs

Then DDx removes the now-unnecessary blank import from cli/internal/agent/serviceconfig.go.
title: release: move all surface-area packages under internal/ (compiler-enforced boundary)

acceptance:
1. A testseam `Service.Execute` request for `Harness: "agent"` passes a non-empty tool list to the fake provider and to `ToolWiringHook`.
2. A fake provider can request a read-only tool against `WorkDir`; the service emits ordered `tool_call` and successful `tool_result` events with the real tool output, then a success final event.
3. The `DDX_AGENT_USE_SERVICE_CONTRACT=1` CLI path preserves the same built-in tool availability as the direct `agentcore.Run` path, with a command-verifiable regression test.
4. `go test -tags=testseam .` and `go test ./cmd/agent ./...` pass.
description:
The native `agent` harness is a primary harness, but `Service.Execute` currently builds an `agentcore.Request` without wiring any tools and calls `ToolWiringHook` with nil. This means the service contract can emit tool_call/tool_result events only for failed unknown-tool attempts, while the CLI path wires read/write/edit/bash/find/grep/ls/patch/task tools directly. Bring the service path to parity with the native CLI behavior and make the `agent` ToolEvents capability evidence-backed.

In scope: `ServiceExecuteRequest`/service options if a tool source needs to be exposed, `runNative`, the `DDX_AGENT_USE_SERVICE_CONTRACT=1` CLI bridge, testseam hooks, and CONTRACT-003 capability language if the public shape changes. Keep subprocess harness behavior out of scope.
title: service: wire native agent tools through Service.Execute

acceptance: Tests covering AC-FEAT-001-03 and AC-FEAT-001-06 exist in the runtime test suite; they prove retry exhaustion stops at the runtime retry ceiling, concurrent runs keep independent state, and the default Go test suite passes.
description:
<context-digest>
<principles>Runtime behavior should be proven at the `Run()` boundary · Regression tests should encode edge-case termination behavior, not just happy paths · Concurrent safety claims need explicit tests</principles>
<concerns>loop lifecycle | retries | cancellation | compaction fail-closed</concerns>
<practices>Add focused tests in `loop_test.go` or a companion runtime acceptance file · Use mock providers/tools with explicit call counters and controllable blocking points · Assert both result state and absence of extra provider calls</practices>
<adrs>ADR-001 for cost/telemetry semantics</adrs>
<governing>FEAT-001 AC-FEAT-001-03 and AC-FEAT-001-06</governing>
</context-digest>

Current loop coverage proves success, simple retries, cancellation before start, and compaction no-fit handling, but it does not explicitly cover retry exhaustion stopping at the configured runtime retry ceiling or concurrent `Run()` isolation. Add deterministic tests that:
- force repeated transient provider failures and prove the runtime stops after the documented retry budget without issuing a fourth provider call
- run multiple `Run()` calls concurrently with separate providers/tools/callbacks and prove session IDs, event streams, token totals, and tool logs do not leak across runs
- if needed to support the above, add a long-running mock tool path that can be cancelled so the runtime's documented cancellation semantics remain encoded at the boundary

Relevant files: `loop.go`, `loop_test.go`, `loop_external_test.go`.
title: Add loop lifecycle acceptance tests for retry exhaustion and concurrent isolation

acceptance:
- [ ] Session log of a 100% no-op-compaction execution contains zero compaction events
- [ ] Real compactions still emit events with the same data shape (messages_before, messages_after, tokens_freed)
- [ ] Test asserts the suppression at default verbosity
description:
Per CONTRACT-001 event schema: type=compaction events fire ONLY when actual work was performed (messages_after < messages_before OR token count drops). No-op compactions emit nothing at default verbosity.

Existing agent-22c95151 documents the original need; this bead is the realization in the new contract context. Close agent-22c95151 when this lands.
notes:
Suppression already in place at /Users/erik/Projects/agent/loop.go:145-147 (early-return when compactor signals no-op via (msgs, nil, nil)). The events the user observed earlier in this session were against the alpha22 binary which predated this fix. This bead adds a regression test (e707daf) to guard against the suppression being removed in future refactors.

Cross-ref: CONTRACT-003 specifies "type=compaction events fire ONLY when actual work was performed" as a named behavior. Test asserts this end-to-end via Callback inspection.

Closes ddx-side bead noted as superseding agent-22c95151.
title: compaction: suppress no-op compaction telemetry (supersedes agent-22c95151)

acceptance:
1. Harness metadata has an explicit auto-routing/full-coverage eligibility signal.
2. Profile/tier routing excludes non-eligible harnesses when Harness is empty, even if they are installed and otherwise score highly.
3. Explicit Harness requests still include the named harness regardless of auto-routing eligibility.
4. Tests cover gemini/opencode/pi as explicit-only and agent/codex/claude as auto-routing eligible.
5. go test ./internal/routing ./internal/harnesses . passes.
6. go test ./... passes.
description:
Automatic profile/tier routing currently enumerates every available non-test harness from the registry. That lets registered-but-partial harnesses compete in unattended queue work when their binary is installed. Add an explicit routing eligibility field to harness metadata so automatic routing includes only harnesses that have full coverage for the primary baseline.

Initial policy:
- auto-routing eligible: agent, codex, claude
- explicit-only / ad-hoc: gemini, opencode, pi, HTTP provider harness aliases unless explicitly requested or configured as a provider under the native agent route
- test-only harnesses remain excluded except explicit test override

Explicit harness requests must still work for ad-hoc harnesses. The gate applies only when no harness is explicitly requested by the caller.

In-scope files:
- internal/harnesses/harness.go
- internal/harnesses/registry.go
- internal/harnesses/registry_test.go
- service_routing.go
- internal/routing/engine.go
- internal/routing/engine_test.go
- service_test.go if capability/metadata assertions need updating

Out-of-scope:
- Promoting any secondary harness to full coverage.
- Reworking provider routing inside the native agent harness.
title: routing: gate automatic routing on full harness coverage

acceptance:
- [ ] cmd/ddx-agent-bench/ binary builds
- [ ] bench/corpus/ contains at least 5 task files with documented expected behavior
- [ ] Running the bench against a config with no credentials produces a clean "skipped: no credentials" report
- [ ] Running against bragi (when available) executes the corpus and reports per-task metrics
description:
Scaffold the integration / comparison suite per the plan. Lives in agent.

- cmd/ddx-agent-bench/ — runner binary. Discovers (harness, provider, model) candidates from config, runs corpus tasks against each, captures metrics, emits report.
- bench/corpus/ — initial test corpus (versioned with repo). Initial set: single tool call (list files in cmd/), multi-tool sequence (find *.go + count lines), tool error handling (read missing file), reasoning + tool combination (largest file in dir), long-output generation (latency check).
- bench/results/ — gitignored; local result storage.

Builds on compare.go/benchmark.go/quorum.go/condense.go infrastructure (those move from DDx in a separate bead — agent-X to be filed for that move). For this bead, scaffold the bench runner against current infrastructure; rewire to upstream compare/benchmark code when those land.

Credential management: layered (CLI flags > env > project config > user config > agent defaults).

Skips harnesses without credentials gracefully.
title: integration suite: scaffolding + initial corpus + bench runner binary

acceptance: Drift detection notice on changed source files; daily debounce works
description:
Implement drift detection per SD-007 Drift Detection section.

Hash computation:
- Concatenate source files content (auth.json + models.json)
- Compute SHA-256, truncate to 8 hex chars
- Store in SourceHash field

Check logic per SD-007:
- On ddx-agent providers or ddx-agent -p, check if source files changed
- If imported_from exists and hash differs, emit once per day:
  "agent: pi config changed since import — run ddx-agent import pi --diff to review"
- Debounce via mtime of ~/.config/agent/.import-check-{source}
- Per-source tracking (pi and opencode tracked independently)

Token expiry warnings per SD-007:
- If OAuth token expires within 24h, warn with hours remaining
- If already expired, warn but still import
notes: Implemented checkDrift with hash comparison and daily debounce via .import-check-* files
title: Implement drift detection

acceptance: Replaying a historical session log whose session.end data contains `cost_usd` = -1 renders 'Cost: unknown'; replay still renders known positive costs and explicit zero-cost local runs correctly; regression coverage includes a legacy log fixture or equivalent hand-written JSONL input.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: session/replay.go:108
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new pointer-based `SessionEndData.CostUSD` handling only treats `nil` as unknown. Historical `session.end` logs produced before this commit encoded unknown cost as `cost_usd: -1`. When those logs are replayed now, `Replay` falls through the non-positive branch and renders `Cost: $0 (local)` instead of `Cost: unknown`, which misclassifies existing session history and breaks the forward-compatibility expectation for replaying older JSONL logs.
Suggested fix: Preserve compatibility for historical session logs by treating negative `cost_usd` values as unknown during replay (and ideally add regression coverage with a hand-written legacy log fixture containing `cost_usd: -1`).
title: integration: preserve legacy unknown-cost replay semantics

acceptance: When Run executes with a StreamingProvider and NoStream=false, the successful llm.response event includes attempt metadata with provider_name, provider_system, requested_model, response_model/resolved_model, and cost source; streaming and non-streaming paths both pass regression tests covering metadata propagation.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit de8c772.
File: loop.go:162, loop.go:210, stream_consume.go:27, provider/openai/openai.go:184, provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:180
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: The new retry and attempt metadata only survives the non-streaming path. Run still prefers consumeStream whenever a provider implements StreamingProvider, which both OpenAI and Anthropic do. consumeStream assembles only content, tool calls, usage, model, and finish reason; it has no way to carry AttemptMetadata. The loop then backfills only AttemptIndex when resp.Attempt is nil. As a result, the default execution path loses ProviderName, ProviderSystem, RequestedModel, ResponseModel, ResolvedModel, and cost provenance even though this commit's acceptance requires runtime attempt metadata sufficient for provider identity and attribution. Current tests cover only non-streaming metadata population, so this regression is not caught.
Suggested fix: Extend the streaming path so provider metadata is emitted and preserved through StreamDelta and consumeStream, and add regression tests that run Run with streaming-enabled providers and assert the emitted llm.response attempt payload includes provider, model, and cost metadata on success.
title: correctness: streaming runs drop attempt metadata required by runtime retries

acceptance: Streaming timing uses real delta arrival timestamps rather than subtracting cumulative callback time; a regression test with callback sleep plus inter-delta provider delay verifies ddx.timing.generation_ms still includes the provider delay and EventLLMDelta timestamps remain monotonic.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: stream_consume.go:44
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: consumeStream now computes each delta timestamp as time.Now().Add(-callbackDelay) and grows callbackDelay by the duration of prior EventLLMDelta callbacks. When a later streamed delta becomes ready while an earlier callback is still running, this subtracts both observer latency and real provider generation time that elapsed during that callback. In the shipped providers, ChatStream uses a single-slot channel, so later deltas can queue behind the consumer and then be back-dated when read. That makes ddx.timing.generation_ms and delta event timestamps too small or even flat across distinct arrivals, which violates CONTRACT-001's requirement that timing reflect the streamed output window rather than an adjusted observer schedule.
Suggested fix: Capture an arrival timestamp at the producer or carry it on StreamDelta so consumeStream can use actual delta arrival times. At minimum, add a regression test that combines callback sleep with real delayBetween timing and verifies generation_ms still includes the provider delay that elapsed while the callback was running.
title: correctness: callback-delay compensation undercounts real stream generation

acceptance: HOME set to a fresh temp directory and running 'go run ./cmd/ddx-agent update --check-only' no longer fails because ~/.cache/agent is missing; cmd/ddx-agent has a regression test covering GetLatestRelease or cmdUpdate with an absent cache directory
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cmd/ddx-agent/update.go:138
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: GetLatestRelease now returns any cache-write error from saveCachedVersion. cmdUpdate and cmdVersion pass ~/.cache/agent/latest-version.json without creating ~/.cache/agent first, so a first-run update check fails after a successful GitHub response with 'open .../.cache/agent/latest-version.json: no such file or directory'. Reproduced with: HOME=/tmp/tmp.guOtfw5Bpt go run ./cmd/ddx-agent update --check-only
Suggested fix: Treat cache persistence as best-effort or create the parent cache directory before writing, and add a regression test that covers a missing cache directory.
title: integration: update check fails on first run when cache directory is absent

acceptance: For Anthropic streaming runs, token usage from message_start and message_delta is merged into the final Response and llm.response event; Input, Output, CacheRead, and CacheWrite are preserved; Total equals Input+Output; a regression test covers split usage deltas
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 51f778b.
File: provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:236, provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:275, stream_consume.go:84
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Anthropic streaming emits input tokens on the initial message_start delta and output/cache tokens on later message_delta events, but consumeStream replaces resp.Usage on each delta instead of accumulating it. In the default streaming path, the final Response therefore drops the earlier input token counts and retains only the last usage fragment. Anthropic's streaming code also never sets Total, so even if the fragments were merged the run-level total token count would remain zero. This leaves llm.response, session.end, and any cost/accounting derived from streaming Anthropic responses inconsistent with the non-streaming provider path.
Suggested fix: Accumulate streaming usage fragments in consumeStream instead of overwriting them, and have the Anthropic streaming path emit enough data to compute a correct final TokenUsage.Total, or emit the full merged usage on the terminal delta. Add a regression test that simulates Anthropic-style split usage deltas and asserts the final Response and emitted response event preserve input, output, cache, and total token counts.
title: correctness: anthropic streaming usage loses input tokens across deltas

acceptance: A session whose chat spans all have known cost preserves a root invoke_agent ddx.cost.amount equal to the sum of child costs even when provenance differs across turns; the root span only emits ddx.cost.source=unknown when at least one contributing chat span has unknown cost; automated tests cover mixed provider-reported/configured cost rollups.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: loop.go:289
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new root-span cost rollup logic marks the invoke_agent span as unknown whenever known child chat spans disagree on ddx.cost.source, currency, or pricing_ref. That drops a valid total for mixed but fully known sessions, which violates CONTRACT-001's rule that the root cost only becomes unknown when a contributing chat span has unknown cost. Reproduced against the current code by running a two-turn session with a provider-reported first turn and an exact configured-cost second turn: result.CostUSD=0.03 while the root span still emits ddx.cost.source=unknown.
Suggested fix: Roll up root ddx.cost.amount whenever every contributing chat span has known cost, and only force ddx.cost.source=unknown when a contributing span is actually unknown. If metadata such as pricing_ref or source cannot be represented cleanly at the root, omit or summarize those fields rather than discarding the known total. Add a regression test covering mixed known-cost provenance across turns.
title: correctness: preserve known root cost rollups across mixed known cost sources

acceptance: When a provider implements SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata and Chat returns an error, the ended chat span records ddx.provider.system with that provider identity instead of unknown, while OpenAI-compatible providers still record resolved server.address and server.port.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: loop.go:182
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new chat-span bootstrap path always sources ddx.provider.system from chatStartIdentity. When a provider does not implement ChatStartMetadata, chatStartIdentity returns "unknown" instead of preserving the existing session-level provider identity. Before this commit, StartChat seeded ProviderSystem from sessionProvider, so failed calls from providers that only implement SessionStartMetadata still retained a meaningful identity. This change regresses those error spans to "unknown". The repo already has such a provider in provider/virtual/virtual.go, which exposes SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata.
Suggested fix: Make chatStartIdentity fall back to sessionStartIdentity(provider) for providerSystem when ChatStartMetadata is unavailable, while still leaving server address and port unset unless the richer metadata hook is implemented.
title: integration: preserve provider identity fallback for providers without ChatStartMetadata

acceptance: A JSON payload using the DDx envelope field `title` but missing required `id` and `prompt` fails with an invalid prompt envelope error for -p, stdin, and @file input paths, while non-envelope JSON objects like `{"kind":"note","text":"hello"}` still pass through as raw prompt text; regression tests cover both behaviors.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go:381
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: `isPromptEnvelopeProbe` now treats `kind` plus `prompt`, `id`, `inputs`, `response_schema`, or `callback` as an envelope candidate, but it still ignores the optional `title` field even though `ddxPromptEnvelope` accepts and emits it. A malformed DDx payload like `{"kind":"prompt","title":"Inspect main"}` is therefore treated as raw prompt text instead of failing validation, so execution falls through to provider selection on all entry paths. Repro: `go run ./cmd/ddx-agent -p '{"kind":"prompt","title":"Inspect main"}' -provider nonexistent` returns `config: unknown provider "nonexistent"` instead of `invalid prompt envelope`; the same happens for stdin and `@file` because all three routes call `resolvePrompt` -> `parsePromptInput`.\nSuggested fix: include `title` in the probe heuristic for recognized envelope fields, and add regression coverage showing that `kind`+`title` without required `id`/`prompt` fails for inline, stdin, and `@file` inputs while non-envelope JSON such as `{"kind":"note","text":"hello"}` still passes through as raw prompt text.
title: correctness: reject malformed prompt envelopes with only title

acceptance: Network error during streaming causes consumeStream to return a non-nil error; partial content is not treated as a successful response; tests cover error propagation from both OpenAI and Anthropic providers
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: provider/openai/openai.go:213-244, provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:219-271
Category: bug
Severity: high
Description: Both ChatStream goroutines iterate stream.Next() and then unconditionally send Done:true. If stream.Next() returns false due to a network error, stream.Err() is never checked. The goroutine sends Done:true and the channel closes normally. consumeStream returns a partial Response with no error, and the agent loop treats it as a successful response. This silently corrupts tool call arguments or truncates content.
Suggested fix: After the stream loop exits, check stream.Err(). If non-nil, send a StreamDelta with an embedded error signal OR close the channel and handle via a separate error channel. Simplest approach: add an Error field to StreamDelta, check it in consumeStream, and return the error.
title: bug: stream errors swallowed in OpenAI and Anthropic ChatStream

acceptance: A JSON payload intended as a DDx prompt envelope but missing the required id field fails with an invalid prompt envelope error for -p, stdin, and @file input paths, while non-envelope JSON objects still pass through as raw prompt text; regression tests cover both behaviors.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go:331
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new probe heuristic only treats JSON as a DDx prompt envelope when `kind` is paired with `id`, `inputs`, `response_schema`, or `callback`. A malformed envelope like {"kind":"prompt","prompt":"Read main.go"} is therefore treated as raw prompt text instead of failing validation, so execution proceeds to provider selection. This violates the structured-I/O contract in the PRD and leaves invalid envelope input non-deterministic. Repro: `go run ./cmd/ddx-agent -p '{"kind":"prompt","prompt":"Read main.go"}' -provider nonexistent` returns `config: unknown provider "nonexistent"` instead of `invalid prompt envelope`.\nSuggested fix: Treat objects that contain `kind` plus `prompt` as envelope candidates too, so missing required `id` still reaches envelope validation; add regression coverage for missing-id envelopes across inline, stdin, and @file inputs.
title: correctness: reject malformed prompt envelopes missing id

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 62f9f00.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl (agent-82042311 record)
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: When adding retroactive <measure-results> to bead agent-82042311, the ddx bead update --notes command replaced the existing planning note rather than appending. The original note was: 'This bead should complete after agent-a8bf4d0b and before adapter/automation/tooling work. It is the control point for the fixed task subset and calibrated thresholds.' This sequencing and dependency context was destroyed. The ddx bead update --notes flag performs full replacement (documented as 'New notes'), so existing content must be read and concatenated before updating.
Suggested fix: Restore the original planning note by prepending it to the current measure-results content using ddx bead update --notes with the concatenated value.
title: correctness: agent-82042311 planning note overwritten by retroactive measure-results

acceptance: If attempt cost provenance is unknown, llm.response and session.end do not emit a positive cost_usd derived from DefaultPricing; known provider-reported or configured costs still propagate; regression tests cover known-cost and unknown-cost cases.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit de8c772.
File: loop.go:219, loop.go:241, provider/openai/openai.go:83, provider/anthropic/anthropic.go:90, pricing.go:12
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: This change introduces structured cost provenance on resp.Attempt.Cost and explicitly marks both providers as source=unknown, but the runtime still unconditionally computes cost_usd from DefaultPricing and emits it on every successful llm.response. That produces internally contradictory telemetry: the same event can say the attempt cost source is unknown while also publishing a numeric USD value from a static fallback table. CONTRACT-001 explicitly forbids generic stale pricing tables as a fallback and requires unknown cost to remain unknown when no reported or configured cost exists.
Suggested fix: Stop deriving per-attempt and session cost from DefaultPricing once attempt-level cost provenance exists. Use provider-reported or configured runtime pricing only when available, otherwise omit numeric cost and keep the rollup unknown. Add tests that assert unknown-cost attempts do not emit positive cost_usd values.
title: correctness: llm.response cost_usd conflicts with unknown attempt cost provenance

acceptance: GlobTool and GrepTool WalkDir skip .git/ directories; unit test confirms .git contents are excluded from results
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: tool/glob.go and tool/grep.go (WalkDir callbacks)
Category: performance
Severity: medium
Description: GlobTool and GrepTool walk the entire directory tree including .git/, node_modules/, vendor/, and other commonly-ignored directories. This produces noisy results, exposes git internals in output, and causes significant slowdowns on repos with large dependency trees or deep .git/objects/ directories. Established coding tools universally skip .git at minimum.
Suggested fix: Add a directory skip list (at minimum .git) in the WalkDir callback. Consider also skipping node_modules, vendor, .hg, .svn. Optionally respect .gitignore via a library like go-gitignore.
title: performance: glob/grep WalkDir should skip .git and VCS directories

acceptance: A plain JSON prompt like {"kind":"note","title":"hello"} remains raw prompt text for -p, stdin, and @file inputs, while malformed DDx prompt envelopes like {"kind":"prompt","title":"Inspect main"} still fail with an invalid prompt envelope error; regression tests cover both behaviors.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go:375
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: `isPromptEnvelopeProbe` now treats any JSON object with top-level `kind` plus `title` as a DDx prompt envelope candidate. That fixes malformed `{"kind":"prompt","title":"..."}` envelopes, but it also re-breaks backward compatibility for plain JSON prompts whose `kind` is not the DDx envelope discriminator. Repro: `go run ./cmd/ddx-agent -p '{"kind":"note","title":"hello"}' -provider nonexistent` now returns `invalid prompt envelope: kind, id, and prompt are required` instead of passing the JSON through as raw prompt text; the same regression occurs for stdin and `@file` because all three paths call `resolvePrompt` -> `parsePromptInput`. This violates the earlier plain-JSON compatibility requirement while over-matching objects that are not DDx prompt envelopes.\nSuggested fix: narrow the probe so `title` only contributes to envelope detection when the object is actually a DDx prompt envelope candidate, for example by requiring `kind == "prompt"` before `title` triggers validation; add regression coverage proving `{"kind":"note","title":"hello"}` stays raw text for inline, stdin, and `@file`, while malformed `{"kind":"prompt","title":"Inspect main"}` still fails validation.
title: correctness: preserve non-envelope JSON with kind+title

acceptance: cmd/ddx-agent includes fuzz coverage for prompt parsing that seeds valid envelopes, malformed envelopes, and plain JSON payloads; fuzzing parsePromptInput or resolvePrompt does not panic; malformed envelope-shaped inputs continue to return deterministic invalid-envelope errors while non-envelope JSON remains raw prompt text
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go:322
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The last-commit change extends parsePromptInput, which is a CLI boundary parser and user-input validator for DDx prompt envelopes, but the repo still has no fuzz coverage for this path. The active testing concern requires fuzzing parsers, deserializers, and user-input validators, and searching for Go fuzz targets only finds coverage in tool/fuzz_test.go. This leaves the prompt-envelope state machine protected only by example-based tests despite several recent regressions in the same code path.
Suggested fix: add Go fuzz tests for parsePromptInput or resolvePrompt in cmd/ddx-agent, seeded with valid envelopes, malformed envelopes, and plain JSON payloads. The fuzz target should at minimum assert no panic and stable classification between raw prompt passthrough versus invalid-envelope error for malformed envelope-shaped inputs.
title: drift: add fuzz coverage for prompt envelope parsing

acceptance: With a large system prompt and a small KeepRecentTokens budget, compaction still triggers when required but the next provider call retains the active user prompt as a verbatim user message; add a regression test that fails if the final provider request contains only system prompt plus summary after compaction.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: compaction/compactor.go:48; compaction/config.go:74; loop.go:89
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The fix subtracts non-persisted system-prompt tokens directly from KeepRecentTokens and clamps the result to zero. When the system prompt is larger than the keep budget, FindCutPoint now returns len(messages), so CompactMessages summarizes the entire live history, including the current user prompt. A repro with ContextWindow=25, KeepRecentTokens=5, and an 80-character system prompt shows the final provider call receives only the system prompt plus the generated summary; the explicit user prompt  is no longer present as a real user message. SD-006 says session prefix messages are excluded from preserved user messages and shows post-compaction ordering that retains recent user messages after the system prompt. Compacting away the active prompt lets the summarizer paraphrase or omit the instruction that the next model turn is supposed to answer.
Suggested fix: Account for system-prompt tokens in the compaction trigger and post-compaction fit calculation without letting them reduce the preserved recent-conversation budget to zero. Preserve at least the last real user message/current prompt verbatim, or add cut-point logic that excludes prefix context from the keep budget while still enforcing a bounded final request size.
title: correctness: compaction can summarize away the active user prompt when prefix exhausts keep budget

acceptance: CI uses a golangci-lint version compatible with the checked-in config, or the config is migrated to the installed version; make lint passes in the repo; the CI workflow's lint step runs successfully with the committed configuration
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .github/workflows/ci.yml:18
Category: drift
Severity: high
Description: The new CI workflow installs golangci-lint with @latest, but the repository still uses the older .golangci.yml schema. With the current latest binary (golangci-lint 2.11.4), the lint step fails immediately with 'unsupported version of the configuration: ' before any analysis runs. That means hx-74ed3216 was closed even though one of the newly wired quality gates is not actually enforceable in CI and the workflow is now broken on every run.
Suggested fix: either pin golangci-lint to a config-compatible v1 release in CI, or migrate .golangci.yml to the v2 schema and verify make lint passes with the installed version before keeping the bead closed.
title: drift: pin or migrate golangci-lint config before latest CI install

acceptance: GlobTool truncates results at a defined maximum; output includes truncation indicator; unit test verifies truncation behavior
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: tool/glob.go:57-87
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: GrepTool caps results at maxGrepResults=200 with a truncation indicator, but GlobTool has no such limit. A pattern like '**/*' on a large monorepo could return tens of thousands of paths, causing excessive memory usage and an unusably large tool response that wastes LLM context tokens.
Suggested fix: Add a maxGlobResults constant (e.g. 500) with truncation behavior matching GrepTool. Append a '(results truncated at N matches)' line when the limit is hit.
title: correctness: GlobTool needs result count limit like GrepTool

acceptance: CONTRACT-001 explicitly defines how the root invoke_agent span represents known session totals when child chat spans have mixed known provenance; the contract no longer requires an impossible or ambiguous ddx.cost.source/pricing_ref combination at the root; conformance tests or checklist coverage include a mixed provider-reported/configured session example.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-001-otel-telemetry-capture.md:221
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: CONTRACT-001 says the root invoke_agent span should emit a session cost rollup whenever every contributing chat span has known cost, and only become unknown when any child cost is unknown. But the contract never defines what ddx.cost.source, ddx.cost.currency, and ddx.cost.pricing_ref should look like when all child chat costs are known yet their provenance differs across turns, such as one provider-reported turn plus one configured-cost turn. That makes the required root shape ambiguous: ddx.cost.source is required when amount is known, but the allowed enum only models single-attempt provenance. The current implementation fell into this gap and downgraded a known total to unknown when provenance metadata differed.
Suggested fix: Amend the root-span cost-rollup section to specify a conformant representation for mixed known-cost provenance. Either add an explicit aggregate/root rollup source shape, or state that the root may still emit ddx.cost.amount and currency while omitting or summarizing provenance fields that cannot be represented cleanly. Add a contract-focused regression test or checklist entry covering mixed provider-reported/configured sessions.
title: correctness: define mixed-provenance root cost rollup semantics

acceptance: Streaming tool call with multi-chunk arguments assembles complete JSON; TestConsumeStream_ToolCallAssembly covers split-chunk scenario; openai ChatStream maps by index to carry ID forward for subsequent arg fragments
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: provider/openai/openai.go:226-230
Category: bug
Severity: high
Description: In ChatStream, the loop over choice.Delta.ToolCalls overwrites delta fields for each call (only last wins when multiple arrive in one chunk). Critically, OpenAI only sends tool call ID in the FIRST chunk; subsequent argument chunks have ID="" and index. consumeStream checks 'if delta.ToolCallID != ""' and ignores ID-less deltas, so all argument fragments after the initial chunk are silently dropped. Tool calls assembled from streaming will have truncated or empty arguments.
Suggested fix: Track tool calls by index (not just ID) in ChatStream. Maintain a map[int]string for known IDs so subsequent arg chunks can be dispatched with the correct ID.
title: bug: OpenAI streaming tool call arguments lost after first chunk

Category: drift

Description: SD-003 preset table (line 159) lists the default preset as ddx-agent, but the implementation in prompt/presets.go uses agent. SD-005 references SD-003 as the authority for preset details, creating a confusing cross-reference where the governing design doc disagrees with both the implementation and the downstream docs. Additionally, several description strings in SD-003 are stale: claude is missing tool-aware, codex is missing no fluff, cursor is missing edit-heavy.

acceptance: When the compactor returns nil in the no-fit prefix path, Run must still emit a matching EventCompactionEnd after EventCompactionStart; add regression coverage that asserts balanced compaction start/end events for the fail-closed scenario.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: compaction/summarize.go:328; compaction/summarize.go:352; loop.go:101; loop.go:153
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new fail-closed path returns result=nil when prefix-aware compaction cannot fit. In Run, runCompaction emits EventCompactionStart before invoking the compactor, but if compResult is nil it returns at loop.go:153-154 without emitting EventCompactionEnd. AGENTS.md requires EventCompactionEnd after compaction on both success and error paths. This regression becomes reachable specifically because the reviewed commit introduces new nil-return branches for exhausted no-fit cases.
Suggested fix: emit EventCompactionEnd for the no-compaction path as well, including success=false or a dedicated no-op outcome payload, and add callback-based regression coverage for the no-fit prefix scenario so start/end event pairs remain balanced.
title: correctness: no-fit compaction path omits compaction.end event

Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 4df0f7e moved `streamStart` earlier inside `consumeStream`, but `ddx.timing.first_token_ms` is still measured after the chat span starts. `Run` starts the `chat` span at loop.go:175, then emits `EventLLMRequest` before calling `consumeStream`; because callbacks are user-supplied and can block, the exported attribute can still under-report first-token latency by the callback time and therefore violate CONTRACT-001's requirement that the value measure from span start to first streamed token.
Suggested fix: Capture the chat span/request start timestamp in `Run` and thread it into `consumeStream` so `Attempt.Timing.FirstToken` is derived from the same timestamp that anchors the `chat` span. Add an end-to-end test where the request callback sleeps before streaming begins and assert the chat span's `ddx.timing.first_token_ms` includes that delay.
title: correctness: first_token_ms still excludes pre-stream callback latency

acceptance: A Run configured with both SystemPrompt and Compactor accounts for the system prompt when deciding whether to compact; a regression test demonstrates compaction still triggers when the history alone is under budget but the provider request (system prompt + history) exceeds the context limit; Result.Messages remains free of duplicated system messages.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 7e225853fcb708575f478050865d4eb9e0322c87.
File: loop.go:60; loop.go:109; loop.go:174
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new continuity refactor removes `SystemPrompt` from the persisted `messages` slice and only prepends it to `providerMessages` immediately before each provider call. `req.Compactor` still runs against `messages`, so its token estimate and cut-point decisions no longer include the system prompt even though the next provider request does. In long sessions with a large system prompt or AGENTS/developer wrapper, compaction can now decide that the history fits when the actual provider request exceeds the configured context budget. SD-006 explicitly requires the loop to keep the system prompt out of persisted history while still treating it as part of the live request prefix and context-window math. Current tests cover continuity and resumed compaction state, but none exercise compaction with a non-empty `SystemPrompt`, so this regression is not caught.
Suggested fix: Include the system prompt in compaction token accounting without persisting it into `Result.Messages`/`Request.History`, and add a regression test that runs `Run` with both `SystemPrompt` and `Compactor` enabled and verifies compaction triggers based on the full provider request size rather than the history slice alone.
title: integration: compaction ignores system prompt tokens in Run continuity path

acceptance: With ContextWindow=80, ReserveTokens=0, KeepRecentTokens=20, EffectivePercent=100, a 260-character system prompt prefix, history [120-char user, 120-char assistant, DO-THE-THING], and a mock summarizer response of x, NewCompactor must not return a non-nil result whose TokensAfter exceeds 80; add regression coverage for this exhaustion case.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 1d16a3c72774e6d4f451a036fc7a62df5c0b9bfb.
File: compaction/summarize.go:329; compaction/summarize.go:335; compaction/summarize.go:352; compaction/summarize.go:360; compaction/summarize.go:365; compaction/summarize.go:370
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new prefix-aware summary budgeting fixes the normal fit case, but when no summary can fit inside the remaining post-prefix window the exhausted-cutpoint paths still return a successful compaction result. In compactMessages, the result==nil fallback retries compactAtCutIndex with effectiveWindow=0, and the over-budget bestResult path returns the last candidate even after result.TokensAfter+prefixTokens stayed above the effective window. A direct executable repro in the current tree uses ContextWindow=80, ReserveTokens=0, KeepRecentTokens=20, EffectivePercent=100, a 260-character system prompt (estimated prefixTokens=67), history [120-char user, 120-char assistant, DO-THE-THING], and a mock summarizer response of "x". NewCompactor returns a non-nil result with TokensAfter=99, which still exceeds the 80-token effective budget.
Suggested fix: when every valid cut point still leaves the rebuilt request over budget, fail closed instead of returning an unbounded fallback or over-budget bestResult. Return nil/no compaction (or a dedicated warning result) unless the final TokensAfter including prefix fits within the effective window, and add a regression test for the 260-char prefix repro.
title: correctness: no-fit prefix-aware compaction still returns an over-budget result

acceptance: A two-run regression test that resumes from Result.Messages with a fresh compactor preserves prior summary content and file-op XML after the second compaction; long-session continuity no longer depends on reusing the same compactor closure across Run calls
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 7e225853fcb708575f478050865d4eb9e0322c87.
File: agent.go:251; loop.go:60; compaction/compactor.go:40; compaction/summarize.go:100; compaction/summarize.go:179
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new continuity API says callers can feed Result.Messages back into Request.History to continue a session, but that is not sufficient once compaction has already occurred. compaction.NewCompactor keeps previousSummary and previousFileOps in hidden closure state. On a resumed Run, only the message list is carried forward; a fresh compactor starts with empty previousSummary/previousFileOps. When compaction happens again, CompactMessages filters prior <summary> injections out of messages[:cutIndex] and passes the empty previousSummary to Summarize, so the older compacted context and accumulated file-op state can be dropped from the next summary. This makes long sessions silently lose earlier context across Run boundaries unless callers also preserve the exact compactor instance, which the new API and tests do not require or document.
Suggested fix: Make continuity durable across Run boundaries by serializing enough compaction state into the resumed history/result contract, or by teaching compaction to recover previous summary/file-op state from carried History. Add a regression test that chains two Run calls with compaction on both sides and verifies the second compaction preserves information from the first summary and its file-op XML even when the resumed run uses a fresh compactor.
title: correctness: resumed sessions lose prior compaction state on next compaction

acceptance: For the 80-token context-window repro with a 260-character system prompt prefix, agent.Run must not call Provider.Chat with provider messages whose estimated token count exceeds the effective window; add regression coverage for the no-fit compaction path at the Run integration level.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: loop.go:114; loop.go:153; loop.go:179
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: The new no-fit path in compactMessages now returns result=nil for over-budget prefix cases, but Run treats a nil compaction result as a no-op and immediately builds providerMessages from the original history. In the same repro covered by TestCompactor_SystemPromptPrefixNoFitFailsClosed, the unchanged history still estimates to 135 tokens with the system prompt prefix included (67 prefix + 31 user + 33 assistant + 4 active user), which exceeds the 80-token effective window and will still be sent to the provider. The patch therefore fixes the compactor return value but does not actually prevent an over-budget LLM request in the agent loop.
Suggested fix: when compaction was needed but the compactor cannot produce an in-budget history, fail the iteration before calling the provider or add a compactor/loop contract that surfaces an explicit over-budget outcome. Add a Run-level regression test that asserts no provider Chat call occurs with the 80-token/260-char-prefix repro unless the request fits within budget.
title: integration: no-fit prefix compaction still sends an over-budget provider request

acceptance: With a large system prompt and a small KeepRecentTokens budget, compaction still triggers when required but the next provider call retains the active user prompt as a verbatim user message; add a regression test that fails if the final provider request contains only system prompt plus summary after compaction.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: compaction/compactor.go:48; compaction/config.go:74; loop.go:89
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The fix subtracts non-persisted system-prompt tokens directly from KeepRecentTokens and clamps the result to zero. When the system prompt is larger than the keep budget, FindCutPoint now returns len(messages), so CompactMessages summarizes the entire live history, including the current user prompt. A repro with ContextWindow=25, KeepRecentTokens=5, and an 80-character system prompt shows the final provider call receives only the system prompt plus the generated summary; the explicit user prompt DO-THE-THING is no longer present as a real user message. SD-006 says session prefix messages are excluded from preserved user messages and shows post-compaction ordering that retains recent user messages after the system prompt. Compacting away the active prompt lets the summarizer paraphrase or omit the instruction that the next model turn is supposed to answer.
Suggested fix: Account for system-prompt tokens in the compaction trigger and post-compaction fit calculation without letting them reduce the preserved recent-conversation budget to zero. Preserve at least the last real user message/current prompt verbatim, or add cut-point logic that excludes prefix context from the keep budget while still enforcing a bounded final request size.
title: correctness: compaction can summarize away the active user prompt when prefix exhausts keep budget

acceptance: When an OpenAI-compatible provider with a custom BaseURL returns an error, the ended chat span still records the correct ddx.provider.system, server.address, and server.port values.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: loop.go:174
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: StartChat seeds every chat span from sessionStartIdentity, which only returns provider name and model. annotateChatSpan adds the resolved provider system and server identity only after a successful response. When Chat or consumeStream returns an error, the span ends without ddx.provider.system or server.address/server.port even though those fields are already known by the provider, so failed OpenAI-compatible requests are misattributed and lose the metadata this commit set out to capture.
Suggested fix: Extend the chat-start metadata path so StartChat receives providerSystem and server identity up front, ensuring error spans are populated without relying on a successful response annotation.
title: integration: failed chat spans lose resolved provider identity
